| **Symbol / Formula** | **Expression** | **Physical Meaning / Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| Spatial Degradation | $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Convolution + Additive Noise Model |
| Frequency Degradation | $G(u,v) = H(u,v) F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | DFT of the above |
| Inverse Filter Estimate | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ | Core restoration equation |
| Noise-Amplified Form | $\hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) + \dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ | Shows noise problem |
| Pseudo-Inverse | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \begin{cases} G/H & \text{if } \vert H(u,v)\vert \neq 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Avoids division-by-zero |
| Truncated Inverse Filter | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ for $u^2 + v^2 \le D_0$ | Radial cutoff at radius $D_0$ |
| Parseval's Energy Relation | $\sum \vert F(u,v)\vert^2 = \sum \vert f(x,y)\vert^2$ | Energy conservation in DFT |
| Restoration MSE | $E\{e^2\} = E\{(f - \hat{f})^2\}$ | Mean Square Error metric |
| Optimum Wiener Filter (related) | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \left[ \dfrac{H^{\ast}(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + S_\eta/S_f} \right] G(u,v)$ | When noise exists (advanced) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always write the noise-amplified form $F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ explicitly in the answer. It demonstrates that you understand *why* pure inverse filtering fails in the presence of noise — this is the single most-tested conceptual point in Module 2.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications of Inverse Filtering:**
> - **Astronomical Imaging** — Removing atmospheric turbulence (known PSF via "speckle imaging").
> - **Satellite Remote Sensing** — Compensating for sensor motion blur (known velocity & exposure time).
> - **Medical Imaging** — Deconvolution of microscope and CT blur (when PSF is calibratable).
> - **Forensic Science** — Recovering license plates from CCTV motion-blurred footage.
> - **Astronaut Training Simulators** — Restoring long-exposure photographs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Degradation Model to Inverse Filter

### Starting Postulate
We are given the linear, space-invariant degradation equation in the spatial domain:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

### Step 1 — Apply 2-D DFT to Both Sides
By the **Convolution Theorem**, convolution in spatial domain becomes pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

> **Reasoning:** The DFT of $h \ast f$ is $H \cdot F$ (multiplication of spectra), and the DFT of additive noise is $N(u,v)$.

### Step 2 — Isolate the Original Image Spectrum
Rearrange the equation to isolate $F(u,v)$:

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v) - N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

### Step 3 — Assume Negligible Noise (Ideal Case)
In the **ideal noise-free scenario** where $\eta(x,y) \to 0$, we have $N(u,v) \to 0$. Therefore:

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

### Step 4 — Define the Inverse Filter Transfer Function
The **Inverse Filter** is mathematically defined as the reciprocal of the degradation transfer function:

$$H_{\text{inv}}(u,v) = \frac{1}{H(u,v)}$$

### Step 5 — Final Inverse Filter Equation

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = H_{\text{inv}}(u,v) \cdot G(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}}$$

The restored spatial image is recovered by applying the **Inverse DFT**:

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left\{ \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \right\}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Why Pure Inverse Filtering Fails in Noise

### Starting Point — Re-substitute $G(u,v)$ Back
To expose the noise dependence, substitute $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ directly into the inverse filter:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

### Step 1 — Distribute the Denominator
Split the fraction into two terms:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H(u,v)F(u,v)}{H(u,v)} + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

### Step 2 — Simplify the First Term
The first term simplifies exactly to the original spectrum:

$$\frac{H(u,v)F(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = F(u,v)$$

### Step 3 — Keep the Noise Term Explicit
The recovered spectrum now contains an additive noise error term:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

### Step 4 — Magnitude Analysis
Take the magnitude of both sides to expose the noise problem:

$$|\hat{F}(u,v)| = |F(u,v)| + \left|\frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right|$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Failure Mode
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** For frequencies where $H(u,v) \to 0$ (high-frequency zeros common in motion blur and defocus), the ratio $\frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \to \infty$. This is **noise amplification** — the noise term dominates and overwhelms the true signal, rendering the restored image useless. This is the **central limitation** of inverse filtering.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Pseudo-Inverse Filter (Workaround)

### Problem
A pure division by zero is undefined in floating-point arithmetic, leading to NaN/Inf pixel values.

### Solution
Define a threshold $\varepsilon > 0$. The **Pseudo-Inverse Filter** is:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}, & \text{if } \vert H(u,v)\vert > \varepsilon \\[6pt] 0, & \text{if } \vert H(u,v)\vert \le \varepsilon \end{cases}$$

### Reasoning
At frequencies where the degradation is severely weak (near-zero), we set the estimate to zero rather than amplify noise. This avoids division by zero but introduces a **bias** in the high-frequency content (loss of image detail/edges).

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Scenario** | **Filter Used** | **Pros** | **Cons** |
|---|---|---|---|
| No noise + Known PSF | Pure Inverse Filter | Exact recovery possible | Fails if $H=0$ anywhere |
| Noise + Known PSF | Truncated Inverse / Wiener | Suppresses noise amplification | Loses high-frequency detail |
| Severe Bandwidth Limit | Pseudo-Inverse | Computationally stable | Bias error at zeros of $H$ |
| Unknown PSF | Blind Deconvolution | No prior info needed | Iterative, non-convex |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Inverse Filtering Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete **Inverse Filtering Signal Flow** as implemented in a digital image restoration system. It maps every functional block, data transformation, and decision point in the recovery process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Image f x y] --> B[Convolution with PSF h x y]
    N[Additive Noise eta x y] --> B
    B --> C[Degraded Image g x y]
    C --> D[2D DFT: G u v]
    D --> E{Divide by H u v}
    K[Known PSF h x y] --> L[2D DFT: H u v]
    L --> E
    E --> F{H u v near zero?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Set to 0: Pseudo Inverse]
    F -->|No| H[Normalize: G over H]
    G --> I[Restored Spectrum Fhat u v]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Inverse 2D DFT]
    J --> M[Restored Image fhat x y]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Frequency-Domain Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FrequencyDomain[Frequency Domain Operations]
        F1[F u v Original] --> M1[Multiply by H]
        N1[N u v Noise] --> A1[Add to Signal]
        M1 --> A1
        A1 --> G1[G u v Degraded]
    end
    G1 --> DIV{H u v known}
    DIV --> DIV2[Divide G by H]
    DIV2 --> FHAT[Fhat u v Restored]
    FHAT --> NOISE[Plus N divided by H]
    NOISE --> AMP{Noise Amplified at H approx 0}
    AMP -->|Yes| FAIL[Restoration Fails]
    AMP -->|No| OK[Good Restoration]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic — When to Use Which Inverse Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start Restoration] --> Q1{Is PSF H known?}
    Q1 -->|No| BLIND[Use Blind Deconvolution]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is noise negligible?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| PURE[Use Pure Inverse Filter]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Has zeros within passband?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| TRUNC[Use Truncated or Wiener Filter]
    Q3 -->|No| PURE2[Use Pseudo Inverse Filter]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of inverse filtering," always show three parallel paths:
> 1. The **degradation path** (image → PSF → noise → degraded).
> 2. The **DFT path** (spatial → frequency).
> 3. The **restoration path** (division by $H$ → IDFT → restored image).
> Examiners award full marks only when all three paths are labeled correctly.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define Inverse Filtering. State the fundamental equation used in inverse filtering and explain its physical meaning.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Inverse filtering is a frequency-domain image restoration technique used to recover an original image $f(x,y)$ from a degraded observation $g(x,y)$ by dividing the degraded image spectrum $G(u,v)$ by the known degradation transfer function $H(u,v)$.

**Core equation:**

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Physical meaning:** The inverse filter mathematically reverses (deconvolves) the blurring effect imposed by the imaging system, provided the PSF is known and noise is negligible. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*Why does pure inverse filtering fail in the presence of additive noise? Write the equation that illustrates this failure.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In the presence of additive noise $\eta(x,y)$, the recovered spectrum becomes:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

The second term represents **amplified noise**. At frequencies where $H(u,v) \to 0$ (zeros of the transfer function), the ratio $\frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \to \infty$, causing the noise to dominate the restored image and producing severe artifacts. Hence pure inverse filtering is impractical for noisy images. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry an *internal choice*. Solve EITHER Option A OR Option B. Each Part B question is split into (a) [7 marks] and (b) [7 marks] sub-questions.

---

### Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Starting from the spatial-domain degradation model, derive the frequency-domain inverse filter equation. Show every algebraic step clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (Spatial Domain):**

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

**Step 1 — Apply 2-D DFT to both sides:**

$$G(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\} = \mathcal{F}\{h \ast f + \eta\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Convolution Theorem** ($h \ast f \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} H \cdot F$) and linearity of DFT:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) \cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the unknown $F(u,v)$:**

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v) - N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 4 — Assume negligible noise** ($N(u,v) \approx 0$):

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Inverse Filter Operator:**

$$\boxed{\hat{F}(u,v) = H_{\text{inv}}(u,v) \cdot G(u,v), \quad \text{where} \quad H_{\text{inv}}(u,v) = \frac{1}{H(u,v)}}$$

**Step 6 — Recover spatial image via Inverse DFT:**

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\left\{ \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \right\}$$

**Conclusion:** The inverse filter is the reciprocal of the degradation function applied in the frequency domain. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** Explain with mathematical justification why pure inverse filtering fails for noisy images. Compare it with the pseudo-inverse and truncated inverse filters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Failure Analysis:** Substituting $G(u,v) = H \cdot F + N$ into the inverse filter:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

The recovered spectrum equals the original spectrum **plus a noise error term**. At high frequencies or wherever $H(u,v) \to 0$:

$$\frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \to \infty \implies \text{Noise dominates} \implies \text{Restoration fails}$$

**Pseudo-Inverse Filter:** Avoids division by zero using a threshold $\varepsilon$:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \begin{cases} G/H & \text{if } \vert H(u,v)\vert > \varepsilon \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

*Pros:* Numerically stable. *Cons:* Loss of high-frequency detail (edges become blurred).

**Truncated Inverse Filter:** Restricts restoration to a circular region of radius $D_0$ in the frequency plane:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \quad \text{for} \quad u^2 + v^2 \le D_0$$

*Pros:* Avoids the high-frequency noise explosion. *Cons:* Loses fine details beyond $D_0$.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Filter | Noise Handling | Detail Preservation |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Inverse | Poor (amplifies noise) | Best (in theory) |
| Pseudo-Inverse | Good (clipped) | Moderate |
| Truncated | Good (band-limited) | Moderate |

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the assumptions and preconditions required for applying inverse filtering in image restoration. Mention at least three scenarios where inverse filtering is used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Key Assumptions of Inverse Filtering:**

1. **Linearity** — The degradation system must be linear in its response to the input image.
2. **Space-Invariance (Shift-Invariance)** — The PSF $h(x,y)$ is the same at every spatial location $(x,y)$ across the image.
3. **Known Degradation Function** — The PSF $h(x,y)$ (or its DFT $H(u,v)$) must be completely known *a priori*. Inverse filtering is therefore a **non-blind** technique.
4. **Additive Noise Model** — The noise $\eta(x,y)$ is assumed additive and zero-mean, though ideally negligible.
5. **No Zeros in $H(u,v)$** — Either $H(u,v)$ has no zeros in the frequency plane, or the noise is zero (idealized case).

**Application Scenarios:**

1. **Atmospheric Blur in Astronomy** — Turbulence PSF is measured using guide stars; inverse filtering sharpens telescope images.
2. **Motion Blur Compensation** — For constant-velocity camera shake, $H(u,v)$ is a sinc function in the direction of motion; inverse filtering removes the directional blur.
3. **Out-of-Focus Deblurring** — The PSF is a circular disk (Airy pattern) whose radius depends on the defocus distance; inverse filtering re-focuses the image.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** A $4 \times 4$ image is blurred by a $2 \times 2$ averaging filter and corrupted by additive Gaussian noise. Write the frequency-domain inverse filter equation and explain how you would implement it on a digital computer step-by-step. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Original image $f(x,y)$ of size $4 \times 4$.
- Degradation PSF $h(x,y)$ = $2 \times 2$ averaging filter (each entry $= 1/4$).
- Additive noise $\eta(x,y) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.

**Frequency-Domain Equation:**

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step-by-Step Implementation:**

**Step 1 — Acquire the degraded image $g(x,y)$** from the sensor.

**Step 2 — Zero-pad** $g(x,y)$ to size $8 \times 8$ (or $4 \times 4$ for circular convolution) to prevent wrap-around artifacts.

**Step 3 — Construct the PSF matrix** of size $4 \times 4$:

$$h(x,y) = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Zero-pad** $h(x,y)$ to the same size as the padded image.

**Step 5 — Compute 2-D DFT of both** $g$ and $h$ to get $G(u,v)$ and $H(u,v)$.

**Step 6 — Apply the pseudo-inverse threshold** $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \begin{cases} G(u,v)/H(u,v) & \text{if } \vert H(u,v)\vert > \varepsilon \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 7 — Apply Inverse 2-D DFT** to obtain $\hat{f}(x,y)$.

**Step 8 — Take the real part** (imaginary part should be near-zero; discard to remove numerical artifacts).

**Step 9 — Normalize** pixel values to $[0, 255]$ range for display.

**Conclusion:** This pipeline recovers the image but is highly sensitive to the noise term $\frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$. For noisy images, a Wiener filter is preferred in practice. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the noise term** in the derivation — examiners explicitly test whether you can write $F + N/H$. **(−2 Marks)**
> 2. **Confusing Inverse Filtering with High-Pass Filtering** — they are mathematically different. Inverse filtering uses $1/H(u,v)$, not arbitrary high-pass kernels.
> 3. **Not mentioning the assumption that $H(u,v)$ is known** — inverse filtering is a non-blind technique. **(−1 Mark)**
> 4. **Missing the Convolution Theorem** in the derivation — students often jump from spatial to frequency domain without justification. **(−2 Marks)**
> 5. **Not drawing the three-path block diagram** (degradation / DFT / restoration) in 14-mark questions. **(−3 Marks)**
> 6. **Confusing pseudo-inverse with Wiener filter** — pseudo-inverse uses a hard threshold $\varepsilon$; Wiener uses a smooth noise-to-signal ratio $S_\eta / S_f$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Inverse Filtering (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Inverse filtering is a **non-blind**, frequency-domain image restoration method that uses $H_{\text{inv}}(u,v) = 1/H(u,v)$ to undo a known degradation.
- **Core Equation:** $\hat{F}(u,v) = G(u,v)/H(u,v)$.
- **Noise-Amplified Form:** $\hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) + N(u,v)/H(u,v)$ — always write this in exams.
- **Failure Mode:** Noise explodes at frequencies where $H(u,v) \to 0$ (high-frequency zeros).
- **Convolution Theorem:** Convolution in spatial $\Leftrightarrow$ Multiplication in frequency — mandatory prerequisite.
- **Workarounds:** Pseudo-inverse (hard threshold $\varepsilon$), Truncated inverse (radial cutoff $D_0$), Wiener filter (optimal MMSE).
- **Preconditions:** Linear, shift-invariant system; PSF must be **known**; ideally noise-free.
- **Key Difference from Wiener:** Wiener uses $S_\eta/S_f$ ratio; Inverse uses $1/H$ blindly.
- **Block Diagram (3 paths):** Degradation path, DFT path, Restoration path — must be drawn for full marks.
- **KTU Frequency:** Asked in **Dec 2022, May 2023, Dec 2023, May 2024** — extremely high-yield topic.
- **Cross-Module Link:** Connects Module 2 (restoration) with Module 1 (DFT & filtering) — examiners love linking questions.
- **Memory Mnemonic:** "**G over H gives F, but N over H gives pain**" — remember the noise term.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wiener Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT525 - FUNDAMENTALS OF DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wiener Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wiener Filtering (Formal KTU Definition):**
> The **Wiener Filter** is a non-causal, optimum linear filter in the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** sense, used for the **restoration of degraded images**. It statistically minimizes the error between the estimated (restored) image $\hat{F}(u,v)$ and the original (uncorrupted) image $F(u,v)$ in the frequency domain by exploiting the power spectral densities of both the signal and the additive noise.

Mathematically, the Wiener filter is the solution to the minimization problem:

$$E\left[ \vert F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v) \vert^2 \right] = \text{minimum}$$

where $E[\cdot]$ denotes the **expectation operator** and the minimization is performed over all linear estimators.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Smart Headphones for Images"**
> Imagine you are recording a professor's lecture in a noisy classroom. The **Inverse Filter** is like trying to subtract *all* noise at once — it amplifies frequencies where the noise dominates, resulting in horrible screeching sounds (the audio equivalent of noise amplification). The **Wiener Filter**, on the other hand, behaves like *adaptive noise-cancelling headphones*. It intelligently *weights* each frequency component based on the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at that frequency — preserving clean signal frequencies and suppressing noisy ones. It asks, *"How much of the true signal is buried in noise here?"* and adjusts the restoration gain accordingly.

### Key Constants & Metrics

- **Mean Square Error (MSE):** The expected squared deviation between the restored and true image.
- **Power Spectral Density (PSD):** Describes how the signal/noise power is distributed across frequencies, denoted as $S_f(u,v)$ and $S_\eta(u,v)$.
- **Noise-to-Signal Power Ratio:** $S_\eta(u,v) / S_f(u,v)$, a dimensionless quantity critical for tuning the filter.
- **Transfer Function:** $H(u,v)$, assumed **known or estimated** a priori.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Wiener filter belongs to the family of **restoration filters** (as opposed to enhancement filters). It is the *statistical* counterpart to deterministic inverse filtering. Always remember the three pre-requisites: (1) the degradation function is known, (2) signal and noise are **wide-sense stationary** random fields, and (3) power spectra are known or estimated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The Wiener filter operates on a **degradation model** that mathematically describes how an ideal image becomes corrupted.

1. **Spatial Domain Degradation Model:**
The observed (degraded) image $g(x,y)$ is the result of a linear degradation function $H$ acting on the original image $f(x,y)$, with additive noise $\eta(x,y)$:

$$g(x,y) = (f * h)(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

2. **Frequency Domain Equivalent (via 2-D DFT):**
Taking the 2-D Discrete Fourier Transform of both sides:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

3. **Failure of the Direct Inverse Filter:**
The naive inverse filter computes $\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$. At frequencies where $H(u,v) \to 0$ or $N(u,v)$ is large, this **blows up** the noise catastrophically.

4. **Wiener's Statistical Cure:**
Instead of exact inversion, the Wiener filter applies a *frequency-dependent gain* $W(u,v)$ to the observed spectrum:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v) \cdot G(u,v)$$

5. **Derivation of the Optimum Gain $W(u,v)$:**
By minimizing the MSE $E[\vert F - \hat{F} \vert^2]$ using **orthogonality principle** of linear estimation theory, we obtain the famous Wiener transfer function:

$$W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v) S_f(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 S_f(u,v) + S_\eta(u,v)}$$

6. **Equivalent Form Using SNR:**
Dividing numerator and denominator by $S_f(u,v)$:

$$W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + \frac{S_\eta(u,v)}{S_f(u,v)}}$$

where $\frac{S_\eta(u,v)}{S_f(u,v)}$ is the **Noise-to-Signal Power Ratio (NSR)**.

7. **Key Behavioural Properties:**
- Where $H(u,v) = 0$, $W(u,v) = 0$ (no amplification of zeros).
- Where noise dominates ($S_\eta \gg S_f$), $W \to 0$ (suppress noisy frequencies).
- Where signal dominates and $H$ is well-behaved, $W \to 1/H$ (recovers inverse filter behaviour).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Spatial degradation model |
| 2 | $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | Frequency-domain degradation |
| 3 | $\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v)G(u,v)$ | Wiener filter output |
| 4 | $W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)S_f(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 S_f(u,v) + S_\eta(u,v)}$ | **Master Wiener Equation** |
| 5 | $W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + S_\eta(u,v)/S_f(u,v)}$ | Form with NSR |
| 6 | $E[e^2] = E[\vert F - \hat{F} \vert^2]$ | MSE objective function |
| 7 | $W(u,v) = \frac{1}{H(u,v)} \cdot \frac{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + K}$ | Approximate form (constant $K$) |
| 8 | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \left[\frac{1}{H(u,v)} \cdot \frac{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + K}\right] G(u,v)$ | **Final restoration equation** in full |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where $K$ is used:** In practical KTU problems, when the exact PSD ratio $S_\eta/S_f$ is unknown, a *constant scalar* $K$ is used as an approximation. A small $K \to 0$ behaves like the inverse filter; a large $K$ behaves like a low-pass filter (over-smoothing).

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Imaging:** Restoring MRI and CT scans corrupted by sensor noise.
- **Satellite Remote Sensing:** Deblurring and denoising Earth observation images.
- **Astronomical Imaging:** Recovering faint celestial signals buried in photon noise.
- **Forensic & Surveillance:** Enhancing CCTV footage for identification.
- **Microscopy:** Improving resolution in fluorescence and electron microscopy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Master Wiener Filter Equation (Full Derivation)

We start with the frequency-domain observation model and seek a linear filter of the form $\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v) G(u,v)$ that minimizes the mean square error.

**Step 1 — Define the estimation error in the frequency domain:**

$$E(u,v) = F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) - W(u,v) G(u,v)$$

**Step 2 — Formulate the Mean Square Error as a scalar cost function:**

$$J(W) = E\left[ \vert E(u,v) \vert^2 \right] = E\left[ (F - W G)(F^* - W^* G^*) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Expand the product inside the expectation:**

$$J(W) = E\left[ F F^* \right] - W^* E[F^* G] - W E[F G^*] + W W^* E[G G^*]$$

**Step 4 — Identify the power spectral densities and cross-spectra using the Wiener-Khinchin theorem:**

- $E[F F^*] = S_f(u,v)$ (signal PSD)
- $E[G G^*] = S_g(u,v)$ (degraded image PSD)
- $E[F G^*] = S_{fg}(u,v)$ (cross-PSD between original and observed)

$$J(W) = S_f - W^* S_{fg} - W S_{fg}^* + W W^* S_g$$

**Step 5 — Take the complex derivative of $J$ with respect to $W^*$ and set it to zero (orthogonality condition):**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial W^*} = -S_{fg} + W S_g = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the optimum $W$:**

$$W_{\text{opt}}(u,v) = \frac{S_{fg}(u,v)}{S_g(u,v)}$$

**Step 7 — Express the cross-PSD $S_{fg}$ in terms of $H$ and the signal/noise PSDs:**

Since $G = H F + N$ and assuming signal and noise are **uncorrelated** ($E[F N^*] = 0$):

$$S_{fg} = E[F^* G] = E[F^*(H F + N)] = H^* E[F^* F] + E[F^* N] = H^* S_f$$

**Step 8 — Express the degraded PSD $S_g$ using uncorrelated assumption:**

$$S_g = E[G G^*] = E[(H F + N)(H^* F^* + N^*)] = \vert H \vert^2 S_f + S_\eta$$

**Step 9 — Substitute Step 7 and Step 8 into the expression for $W_{\text{opt}}$:**

$$\boxed{W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v) S_f(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 S_f(u,v) + S_\eta(u,v)}}$$

This is the **fundamental Wiener filter equation** in its complete form.

---

### Derivation 2: Connection to the Constant-$K$ Approximation

When the PSD ratio is approximated as a constant, set:

$$\frac{S_\eta(u,v)}{S_f(u,v)} \approx K$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the master equation:**

$$W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v) S_f(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 S_f(u,v) + K S_f(u,v)} = \frac{H^*}{\vert H \vert^2 + K}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $H$:**

$$W(u,v) = \frac{1}{H(u,v)} \cdot \frac{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + K}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to the observed spectrum:**

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \cdot \frac{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 + K}$$

**Step 4 — Note limiting cases:**

- If $K = 0$: $\hat{F} = G/H$ → **Inverse Filter** (recovers the classical result).
- If $K \to \infty$: $\hat{F} \to 0$ → complete suppression (over-restored to zero).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect students to clearly mention the *three assumptions* used: (1) signal and noise are **zero-mean**, (2) they are **uncorrelated**, and (3) noise has **zero mean**. Skipping any of these typically costs 2 marks.

---

### Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem Setup:**
Given a $1$-D degradation $H(u) = 0.5$, signal PSD $S_f(u) = 4$, noise PSD $S_\eta(u) = 1$, and observed spectrum $G(u) = 10 + 5j$. Compute $\hat{F}(u)$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\vert H \vert^2$:**

$$\vert H \vert^2 = (0.5)(0.5) = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Wiener formula:**

$$W = \frac{(0.5)(4)}{(0.25)(4) + 1} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Compute estimated spectrum:**

$$\hat{F} = W \cdot G = 1 \cdot (10 + 5j) = 10 + 5j$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Since noise is small relative to signal, the Wiener filter trusts the observation fully, behaving nearly like a pass-through.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Wiener Image Restoration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Original Image f x y] --> degrade[Degradation Function H]
    noise[Additive Noise eta x y] --> degrade
    degrade --> obs[Observed Image g x y]
    obs --> dft1[2D DFT to G u v]
    dft1 --> mult[Multiplier: W u v times G u v]
    psd1[Signal PSD Sf] --> wienerCore[Wiener Filter Solver]
    psd2[Noise PSD S eta] --> wienerCore
    hfunc[Degradation H u v] --> wienerCore
    wienerCore --> wfunc[Transfer Function W u v]
    wfunc --> mult
    mult --> idft[Inverse 2D DFT]
    idft --> outputR[Restored Image f hat x y]
```

### Diagram 2: Decision Flow Inside the Wiener Filter Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Receive H, Sf, S eta] --> compH[Compute magnitude squared of H]
    compH --> compNum[Compute numerator: H star times Sf]
    compNSR[Compute NSR: S eta divided by Sf] --> compDen[Compute denominator: magnitude squared of H times Sf plus S eta]
    compNum --> ratio[Form ratio W]
    compDen --> ratio
    ratio --> guard{W equals zero check}
    guard --> apply[Apply W to observed spectrum G]
    apply --> finish[Output estimated spectrum F hat]
```

### Diagram 3: Behavioural Comparison with Inverse Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    invBlock[Inverse Filter] --> invProp[Amplifies noise at H equals zero]
    invProp --> invFail[Result: Severe noise blow up]
    wienerBlock[Wiener Filter] --> wienerProp[Suppresses noisy frequencies]
    wienerProp --> wienerOK[Result: Smoothed but stable restoration]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The block diagram above maps the three inputs into the filter (signal PSD, noise PSD, degradation function) into the core solver that produces $W(u,v)$, which is then multiplied with the DFT of the observed image to yield the restored spectrum. Notice how the architecture explicitly closes the loop by feeding the result back through the inverse DFT to obtain the spatial-domain estimate $\hat{f}(x,y)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define Wiener filtering in the context of image restoration. Why is it preferred over direct inverse filtering?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Wiener filter is a statistically optimal linear filter that restores a degraded image by minimizing the **mean square error** between the restored estimate $\hat{F}(u,v)$ and the original image $F(u,v)$. It uses the **power spectral densities** of the signal and noise. **(1 mark)**
Direct inverse filtering applies $1/H(u,v)$ to the observed spectrum, which **amplifies noise catastrophically** at frequencies where $H(u,v)$ is small or zero. **(1 mark)**
Wiener filtering overcomes this by introducing a frequency-dependent weighting $W(u,v)$ that automatically suppresses noisy frequencies and retains signal-dominant ones, providing a stable and smooth restoration. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
State the assumptions made in deriving the Wiener filter restoration equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The signal $f(x,y)$ and noise $\eta(x,y)$ are **zero-mean random fields**. **(1 mark)**
2. The signal and noise are **statistically uncorrelated** (i.e., $E[f \cdot \eta] = 0$). **(1 mark)**
3. Both signal and noise are **wide-sense stationary** random processes, so their power spectral densities exist. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Set A]`

**(a)** Derive the frequency-domain expression for the Wiener filter used in image restoration. Clearly state the assumptions used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A degraded image is observed with the following parameters in the frequency domain at a particular location:
- $H(u,v) = 0.4 + 0.3j$
- $S_f(u,v) = 8$
- $S_\eta(u,v) = 2$
- $G(u,v) = 12 - 6j$

Compute the Wiener filter transfer function $W(u,v)$ and the restored spectrum $\hat{F}(u,v)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to the **Section 3, Derivation 1** of these notes. The step-wise marking scheme is:

- Step 1 (Error definition): 1 mark
- Step 2 (MSE objective): 1 mark
- Step 3 (Expansion): 1 mark
- Step 4 (Orthogonality derivative): 1 mark
- Step 5 (Cross-PSD identification): 1 mark
- Step 6 (Final expression): 1 mark
- Assumptions clearly stated: 1 mark

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $H^*$ and $\vert H \vert^2$:**

$$H^* = 0.4 - 0.3j$$

$$\vert H \vert^2 = (0.4)^2 + (0.3)^2 = 0.16 + 0.09 = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Numerator:**

$$H^* \cdot S_f = (0.4 - 0.3j)(8) = 3.2 - 2.4j$$

**Step 3 — Denominator:**

$$\vert H \vert^2 \cdot S_f + S_\eta = (0.25)(8) + 2 = 2 + 2 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Wiener transfer function:**

$$W = \frac{3.2 - 2.4j}{4} = 0.8 - 0.6j$$

**Step 5 — Restored spectrum:**

$$\hat{F} = W \cdot G = (0.8 - 0.6j)(12 - 6j)$$

$$\hat{F} = 0.8 \times 12 - 0.8 \times 6j - 0.6j \times 12 + 0.6j \times 6j$$

$$\hat{F} = 9.6 - 4.8j - 7.2j + 3.6j^2$$

Since $j^2 = -1$:

$$\hat{F} = 9.6 - 12.0j - 3.6 = 6.0 - 12.0j$$

**Final Answer:** $\hat{F}(u,v) = 6.0 - 12.0j$

**Verification:** Magnitude of $W$ is $\sqrt{0.64 + 0.36} = 1$, indicating a unit-gain pass-through (signal dominates noise at this frequency).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Set B]`

**(a)** Explain the degradation model in the spatial and frequency domains. How does the Wiener filter overcome the limitations of the inverse filter? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the limiting cases of the Wiener filter as the parameter $K$ (approximating the NSR) approaches zero and infinity. What is the geometric interpretation of $K$ in the context of the trade-off between inverse filtering and noise smoothing? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

**Degradation Model (3 marks):**
Spatial: $g(x,y) = f(x,y) * h(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$
Frequency: $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$

The inverse filter computes $\hat{F} = G/H$, which becomes unstable when $H \to 0$ (singularities) or when $N$ is non-negligible. **(2 marks)**

**Wiener Cure (4 marks):**
The Wiener filter introduces a *soft* denominator $\vert H \vert^2 + S_\eta/S_f$ that never becomes zero, providing a smooth, bounded restoration. It also adapts gain to local SNR. **(2 marks)** It uses the orthogonality principle and statistical PSDs to yield the MSE-optimal estimate. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Limiting Cases (7 marks):**

- **Case 1: $K \to 0$** (noise-free assumption): $W \to 1/H$, recovering the **inverse filter**. Restoration is aggressive, including noise amplification. **(2 marks)**
- **Case 2: $K \to \infty$** (severe noise assumption): $W \to 0$, completely suppressing the output. Image is overly smooth. **(2 marks)**
- **Geometric Interpretation:** $K$ is a **regularization parameter** that controls the trade-off between fidelity to the inverse-filter estimate and noise suppression. **(1 mark)**
- **Practical Tuning:** $K$ is usually chosen empirically (or via spectral estimation) as the average NSR over the spectrum. **(1 mark)**
- **Connection to Tikhonov Regularization:** The Wiener filter is a *special case* of Tikhonov-Miller regularization where the regularization term is signal-dependent. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the Conjugate:** Students often write $H$ instead of $H^*$ in the numerator. This is a guaranteed **1-mark loss** in part-(b) numericals.
> 2. **Skipping assumptions:** The orthogonality principle and uncorrelatedness of signal and noise are not optional — without them, the Wiener filter formula is mathematically invalid. Examiners explicitly look for these statements.
> 3. **Confusing $S_f$ and $S_\eta$:** Signal PSD goes with $H^*$ in the numerator; noise PSD goes in the denominator *additive* term. Interchanging them is a **2-mark error**.
> 4. **Ignoring the constant-$K$ approximation:** KTU frequently tests the limiting-case sub-question. Failing to mention $K \to 0$ and $K \to \infty$ loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to take inverse DFT:** A common mistake is to treat $\hat{F}(u,v)$ as the final answer. The actual restored image requires $\hat{f}(x,y) = \text{IDFT}[\hat{F}(u,v)]$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wiener filter is MSE-optimal** among all linear filters when signal and noise are stationary and uncorrelated.
- **Master formula:** $W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v) S_f(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v) \vert^2 S_f(u,v) + S_\eta(u,v)}$.
- **Inverse filter is a special case** of the Wiener filter when $S_\eta \to 0$.
- **NSR term** $S_\eta/S_f$ is the key regularizer that prevents noise amplification.
- **Constant-$K$ form** is widely used in practice when PSDs are not precisely known.
- **Behavioural fingerprint:** Where $H = 0$ or noise dominates, $W \to 0$ (suppress); where signal is strong, $W \to 1/H$ (recover).
- **The three pre-requisites:** (i) Known degradation function $H$, (ii) zero-mean uncorrelated signal/noise, (iii) wide-sense stationarity.
- **Related filters to compare with (often asked):** Inverse filter, Constrained Least Squares (CLS) filter, Geometric Mean filter, Tikhonov regularization.
- **Practical limitation:** Wiener filter is *non-causal* (uses entire frequency domain) and assumes PSDs are known — a strong assumption in real systems.
- **KTU-specific:** Always re-derive the formula from the orthogonality principle in part-(a) 7-mark questions; merely stating the formula yields partial credit only.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Segmentation - Thresholding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT525 - FUNDAMENTALS OF DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Image Segmentation - Thresholding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Image Segmentation

**Image Segmentation** is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple meaningful regions (sets of pixels) based on specific characteristics such as intensity, color, texture, or spatial location. The primary goal is to simplify the representation of an image into something more meaningful and easier to analyze, often locating objects and boundaries (lines, curves, etc.) within the image.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEADT525, segmentation is formally defined as:
> *Segmentation subdivides an image into its constituent regions or objects, where the level of detail depends on the problem being solved — i.e., segmentation should stop when the objects of interest have been isolated.*

Mathematically, for a digital image $I(x,y)$ containing $N$ pixels, segmentation produces a set of disjoint sub-regions $R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots, R_n$ such that:

$$\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i = I(x,y)$$

Where every pixel belongs to exactly one region, and no region overlaps with another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thresholding** is the **simplest, fastest, and most widely used** segmentation technique. It works on the principle of intensity-based classification, converting a grayscale image into a binary image by comparing pixel intensities against a threshold value $T$.

## 1.2 Thresholding — The Academic Definition

**Thresholding** is an intensity-based segmentation technique that converts a grayscale image into a binary image by partitioning the pixel intensities into two or more classes using one or more threshold values $T$. A pixel $I(x,y)$ is classified as either *foreground (object)* or *background* based on whether its intensity is greater than, less than, or equal to the threshold.

The general thresholding operation is defined as:

$$g(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f(x, y) \geq T \\ 0 & \text{if } f(x, y) < T \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $f(x, y)$ is the original input grayscale image
- $g(x, y)$ is the resulting binary (output) image
- $T$ is the threshold value, typically ranging between **0 and L-1** (where $L$ = 256 for 8-bit images)
- Pixel value **1** represents the *object (foreground)*
- Pixel value **0** represents the *background*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Switch" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Magic Tape on a Door**
> Imagine you are standing in a dimly lit room looking at a dark wooden door against a light-colored wall. You cannot see fine details like wood grain or paint texture — you only see a single bright object (the wall) and a single dark object (the door). If a friend asked you, "Tell me which pixels are door and which are wall?" you would simply say: *"Anything darker than a certain shade of brown is the door, anything lighter is the wall."*
>
> That "certain shade of brown" is your **threshold**. You have just performed **thresholding**.

In technical terms, thresholding classifies every pixel as belonging to either of two "teams":
- **Team Foreground (Object):** pixels with intensity $\geq T$
- **Team Background:** pixels with intensity $< T$

The choice of $T$ decides the entire outcome — set it too low and the wall becomes "door"; set it too high and the door disappears into the wall.

## 1.4 Why Thresholding Matters in Engineering

Thresholding is a foundational pre-processing step in nearly every computer vision pipeline:

- **Medical Imaging:** Isolating tumors, organs, or bone structures from MRI/CT scans
- **Document Scanning:** Separating text from paper background in OCR (Optical Character Recognition)
- **Industrial Inspection:** Detecting defective parts, cracks, or missing components on assembly lines
- **Satellite/Aerial Imagery:** Identifying buildings, roads, vegetation, and water bodies
- **Biometric Security:** Locating fingerprints, iris patterns, or faces for authentication
- **Traffic Systems:** Counting vehicles, reading license plates

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme exam, thresholding questions often test whether you can **justify the choice of T**, **explain limitations (noise, illumination)**, and **apply Otsu's method** — the gold-standard automatic threshold selection algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Categories of Thresholding Techniques

Thresholding methods are broadly classified based on how the threshold value $T$ is determined:

### A) Global Thresholding
A **single, fixed threshold value $T$** is applied to the entire image. The threshold is computed from the global histogram of the image and remains constant for every pixel.

$$g(x, y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f(x, y) \geq T \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- **Advantage:** Simple, fast, computationally inexpensive
- **Disadvantage:** Fails when illumination is non-uniform across the image

### B) Local (Adaptive / Variable) Thresholding
The threshold value $T(x,y)$ **varies across the image** based on local neighborhood statistics. A different $T$ is computed for each pixel using a sliding window.

$$T(x, y) = \mu_{\text{local}}(x, y) + C$$

Where $\mu_{\text{local}}(x,y)$ is the local mean intensity in a neighborhood around $(x,y)$, and $C$ is a constant offset.

- **Advantage:** Handles non-uniform illumination effectively
- **Disadvantage:** Computationally more expensive

### C) Multiple (Multi-Level) Thresholding
Uses **more than one threshold** to partition the image into multiple classes. For example, with thresholds $T_1$ and $T_2$, three classes can be separated:

$$g(x, y) = \begin{cases} a & \text{if } f(x, y) \leq T_1 \\ b & \text{if } T_1 < f(x, y) \leq T_2 \\ c & \text{if } f(x, y) > T_2 \end{cases}$$

Used for color images or images with multiple distinguishable intensity regions.

## 2.2 Fundamental Thresholding Strategies

| Strategy | Description | Best Use Case |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| **Single Global Threshold** | One $T$ for the entire image | Bimodal histograms, uniform illumination |
| **Iterative Threshold Selection** | Algorithm refines $T$ iteratively | When histogram has clear valleys |
| **Otsu's Method** | Maximizes between-class variance automatically | Most common automatic method |
| **Adaptive (Local) Threshold** | $T$ varies spatially | Non-uniform illumination |
| **Multilevel Thresholding** | Multiple $T$ values | Multiple distinct objects |
| **Multiband Thresholding** | Different $T$ per color band | Color image segmentation |

## 2.3 Otsu's Method — The Gold Standard

**Otsu's Method** (1979) is the most celebrated automatic global thresholding technique. It exhaustively searches for the threshold $T$ that **maximizes the between-class variance** $\sigma_B^2$ (equivalently, **minimizes the within-class variance** $\sigma_W^2$).

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. Compute the **histogram** of the image (probability distribution $p_i$ for each intensity $i$).
2. Compute the **total mean** $\mu_T$ of all intensities:

$$\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i$$

3. For each candidate threshold $t = 0, 1, 2, \dots, L-1$, split the histogram into:
   - **Class 0 (Background):** intensities $[0, t]$
   - **Class 1 (Foreground):** intensities $[t+1, L-1]$

4. Compute the **weights** (probabilities) of each class:

$$w_0(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} p_i \quad \text{and} \quad w_1(t) = \sum_{i=t+1}^{L-1} p_i$$

5. Compute the **class means**:

$$\mu_0(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{t} i \cdot p_i}{w_0(t)} \quad \text{and} \quad \mu_1(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=t+1}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i}{w_1(t)}$$

6. Compute the **between-class variance**:

$$\sigma_B^2(t) = w_0(t) \cdot w_1(t) \cdot \left[ \mu_0(t) - \mu_1(t) \right]^2$$

7. The **optimal threshold** $T^*$ is the value of $t$ that maximizes $\sigma_B^2(t)$:

$$T^* = \arg\max_{t \in [0, L-1]} \left\{ \sigma_B^2(t) \right\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Otsu's method requires **only a single pass through all possible thresholds** (0–255 for 8-bit images), making it computationally efficient despite being a search-based algorithm.

## 2.4 Iterative Threshold Selection Algorithm

This is a converging iterative method that does not require any prior knowledge of the image content.

### Algorithm Steps:

1. Select an **initial threshold $T_0$**. Common choices:
   - Mean of all pixel intensities: $T_0 = \mu_T$
   - Midpoint of the intensity range: $T_0 = (L-1)/2 = 127.5$

2. **Segment** the image using $T_0$ into two groups: $G_1$ (pixels $\geq T_0$) and $G_0$ (pixels $< T_0$).

3. Compute the **mean intensity** of each group:

$$\mu_1 = \frac{1}{N_1} \sum_{(x,y) \in G_1} f(x, y) \quad \text{and} \quad \mu_0 = \frac{1}{N_0} \sum_{(x,y) \in G_0} f(x, y)$$

4. Compute a **new threshold**:

$$T_{k+1} = \frac{\mu_0 + \mu_1}{2}$$

5. **Repeat steps 2–4** until convergence: $\vert T_{k+1} - T_k \vert < \epsilon$ (where $\epsilon$ is a small tolerance, e.g., $\epsilon = 0.5$).

## 2.5 The Role of Histogram Shape

The shape of the image histogram directly determines thresholding strategy:

- **Bimodal Histogram:** Two clear peaks → **Single global threshold** at the valley between peaks
- **Multimodal Histogram:** Multiple peaks → **Multilevel thresholding**
- **Unimodal Histogram:** Single peak → Thresholding is **generally not suitable**; consider edge-based or region-based methods
- **Broad/Flat Histogram:** No clear peaks → Adaptive or advanced methods needed

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Range |
|---|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $g(x,y) = 1$ if $f(x,y) \geq T$ else $0$ | Basic binary thresholding | Output is 0 or 1 |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{\mu_0 + \mu_1}{2}$ | Iterative threshold update | Intensity (0–255) |
| 3 | $\sigma_B^2(t) = w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$ | Otsu's between-class variance | Squared intensity units |
| 4 | $w_k(t) = \sum_{i \in \text{Class}_k} p_i$ | Class probability (weight) | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $\mu_k(t) = \dfrac{\sum_{i \in \text{Class}_k} i \cdot p_i}{w_k(t)}$ | Class mean intensity | Intensity (0–255) |
| 6 | $T(x,y) = \mu_{\text{local}}(x,y) - C$ | Adaptive threshold (Bernsen's variant) | Intensity (0–255) |
| 7 | $\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i$ | Global image mean | Intensity (0–255) |
| 8 | $p_i = n_i / N$ | Histogram normalized probability | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 9 | $T^* = \arg\max_t \{\sigma_B^2(t)\}$ | Optimal Otsu threshold | Intensity (0–255) |
| 10 | $T(x,y) = \mu_{\text{local}}(x,y) \cdot (1 - k)$ | Niblack's local threshold | Intensity (0–255) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control:** In semiconductor manufacturing, thresholding identifies defective chips on wafers (defect appears as intensity anomaly).
- **Medical Diagnostics:** Brain tumor segmentation in MRI uses Otsu's method as a baseline before region growing.
- **Satellite Imaging:** Distinguishing cloud cover from land surface in weather prediction.
- **Robotics:** Floor vs. obstacle detection in autonomous mobile robots.
- **Agriculture:** Ripeness detection of fruits on conveyor belts using reflectance thresholding.
- **Forensics:** Fingerprint enhancement for criminal identification databases.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Otsu's Method on a Toy Histogram

Consider a 4-bit grayscale image (intensity levels $0$ to $3$) with the following histogram probabilities:

| Intensity $i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---------------|---|---|---|---|
| Probability $p_i$ | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.30 | 0.20 |

Total probabilities sum: $0.20 + 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✓

### Step 1: Compute the Global Mean $\mu_T$

$$\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{3} i \cdot p_i = (0)(0.20) + (1)(0.30) + (2)(0.30) + (3)(0.20)$$

$$\mu_T = 0 + 0.30 + 0.60 + 0.60 = 1.50$$

### Step 2: Evaluate Otsu's Criterion for Each Candidate Threshold $t = 0, 1, 2$

We need to compute $\sigma_B^2(t) = w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$ for each $t$.

#### **Case 1: Threshold $t = 0$**

- **Class 0 (Background):** $\{i = 0\}$, so $w_0 = p_0 = 0.20$, $\mu_0 = 0$
- **Class 1 (Foreground):** $\{i = 1, 2, 3\}$, so $w_1 = 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 0.80$

$$\mu_1 = \frac{(1)(0.30) + (2)(0.30) + (3)(0.20)}{0.80} = \frac{0.30 + 0.60 + 0.60}{0.80} = \frac{1.50}{0.80} = 1.875$$

$$\sigma_B^2(0) = (0.20)(0.80)(0 - 1.875)^2 = 0.16 \cdot 3.5156 = 0.5625$$

#### **Case 2: Threshold $t = 1$**

- **Class 0 (Background):** $\{i = 0, 1\}$, so $w_0 = 0.20 + 0.30 = 0.50$

$$\mu_0 = \frac{(0)(0.20) + (1)(0.30)}{0.50} = \frac{0.30}{0.50} = 0.60$$

- **Class 1 (Foreground):** $\{i = 2, 3\}$, so $w_1 = 0.30 + 0.20 = 0.50$

$$\mu_1 = \frac{(2)(0.30) + (3)(0.20)}{0.50} = \frac{0.60 + 0.60}{0.50} = \frac{1.20}{0.50} = 2.40$$

$$\sigma_B^2(1) = (0.50)(0.50)(0.60 - 2.40)^2 = 0.25 \cdot 3.24 = 0.81$$

#### **Case 3: Threshold $t = 2$**

- **Class 0 (Background):** $\{i = 0, 1, 2\}$, so $w_0 = 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 0.80$

$$\mu_0 = \frac{(0)(0.20) + (1)(0.30) + (2)(0.30)}{0.80} = \frac{0.30 + 0.60}{0.80} = \frac{0.90}{0.80} = 1.125$$

- **Class 1 (Foreground):** $\{i = 3\}$, so $w_1 = 0.20$

$$\mu_1 = \frac{(3)(0.20)}{0.20} = 3.00$$

$$\sigma_B^2(2) = (0.80)(0.20)(1.125 - 3.00)^2 = 0.16 \cdot 3.5156 = 0.5625$$

### Step 3: Compare and Select Optimal Threshold

| Threshold $t$ | $w_0$ | $w_1$ | $\mu_0$ | $\mu_1$ | $\sigma_B^2(t)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0.20 | 0.80 | 0.000 | 1.875 | 0.5625 |
| **1** | **0.50** | **0.50** | **0.60** | **2.40** | **0.8100** |
| 2 | 0.80 | 0.20 | 1.125 | 3.000 | 0.5625 |

$$T^* = \arg\max_t \{\sigma_B^2(t)\} = 1$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal Otsu threshold is $T^* = 1$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Iterative Threshold Selection

Using the same 4-bit image with mean $\mu_T = 1.50$ as our initial guess $T_0$.

### Iteration 1:

**Step A:** Apply $T_0 = 1.50$ to segment the image:
- **Group $G_0$** (pixels $< 1.50$): intensities $\{0, 1\}$
- **Group $G_1$** (pixels $\geq 1.50$): intensities $\{2, 3\}$

**Step B:** Compute group means (weighted by probability):

$$\mu_0 = \frac{(0)(0.20) + (1)(0.30)}{0.20 + 0.30} = \frac{0.30}{0.50} = 0.60$$

$$\mu_1 = \frac{(2)(0.30) + (3)(0.20)}{0.30 + 0.20} = \frac{1.20}{0.50} = 2.40$$

**Step C:** Compute new threshold:

$$T_1 = \frac{\mu_0 + \mu_1}{2} = \frac{0.60 + 2.40}{2} = \frac{3.00}{2} = 1.50$$

### Convergence Check:

$$\vert T_1 - T_0 \vert = \vert 1.50 - 1.50 \vert = 0.00 < \epsilon$$

The algorithm **converges in a single iteration** with $T^* = 1.50$. Note that the iterative method gives a slightly different result from Otsu ($T^* = 1$) — this illustrates that different methods can produce different thresholds on the same image.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Adaptive (Local) Thresholding

Consider a $5 \times 5$ image patch where non-uniform illumination causes the left half to be darker than the right half. A global threshold would fail, but local thresholding succeeds.

**Setup:** Local window size = $3 \times 3$, constant $C = 10$.

For pixel $(2,2)$, the $3 \times 3$ local mean is computed as:

$$\mu_{\text{local}}(2,2) = \frac{1}{9} \sum_{i=-1}^{1} \sum_{j=-1}^{1} f(2+i, 2+j)$$

If $\mu_{\text{local}}(2,2) = 145$, then:

$$T(2,2) = \mu_{\text{local}}(2,2) - C = 145 - 10 = 135$$

This local $T$ is applied **only** to the center pixel $(2,2)$. The process is repeated for every pixel by sliding the window.

> [!NOTE]
> This is **Niblack's method** (a specific adaptive thresholding algorithm). Other variants include **Sauvola's method** and **Bernsen's method**, each differing in how $T$ is computed from local statistics.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Global vs. Adaptive vs. Otsu

| Criterion | Global Threshold | Otsu's Method | Adaptive Threshold |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Computational Cost** | Very Low | Low–Moderate | High |
| **Illumination Robustness** | Poor | Poor–Moderate | Excellent |
| **Histogram Requirement** | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Noise Sensitivity** | High | High | Moderate |
| **Parameter Tuning** | Manual | None (automatic) | Window size, $C$ |
| **Best For** | Bimodal + uniform lighting | Bimodal images | Text/documents, uneven lighting |
| **Failure Mode** | Non-uniform light | Unimodal histograms | Homogeneous texture |

## 3.5 Relationship: Within-Class Variance ↔ Between-Class Variance

A foundational identity underlying Otsu's method is the **total variance decomposition**:

$$\sigma_T^2 = \sigma_W^2 + \sigma_B^2$$

Where:
- $\sigma_T^2$ is the **total variance** of the image (constant, independent of $T$)
- $\sigma_W^2$ is the **within-class variance** (sum of variances of both classes)
- $\sigma_B^2$ is the **between-class variance** (weighted distance between class means)

Since $\sigma_T^2$ is constant, **maximizing $\sigma_B^2$ is equivalent to minimizing $\sigma_W^2$**. This is why Otsu's method can be implemented equivalently in either form, though the $\sigma_B^2$ formulation is preferred because it is computationally cheaper (avoids computing second-order moments).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thresholding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Grayscale Image f of x,y] --> B[Histogram Computation]
    B --> C{Histogram Shape Analysis}
    C -->|Bimodal| D[Global Threshold Selection]
    C -->|Multimodal| E[Multilevel Thresholding]
    C -->|Unimodal| F[Adaptive or Edge-Based Method]
    D --> G[Threshold Value T Computation]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Threshold Type?}
    H -->|Fixed| I[Otsu or Iterative Method]
    H -->|Variable| J[Local Mean or Niblack Method]
    I --> K[Apply T Pixel-by-Pixel]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Binary Output Image g of x,y]
    L --> M[Post-Processing Morphological Operations]
    M --> N[Segmented Object Regions]
```

## 4.2 Otsu's Method Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Image f of x,y] --> B[Compute Histogram p_i for i=0 to L-1]
    B --> C[Compute Global Mean mu_T]
    C --> D[Initialize t = 0]
    D --> E[Partition into Class 0 and Class 1 at t]
    E --> F[Compute w_0, w_1, mu_0, mu_1]
    F --> G[Compute sigma_B_squared of t]
    G --> H{t = L - 1?}
    H -->|No| I[t = t + 1]
    I --> E
    H -->|Yes| J[Select t_star maximizing sigma_B_squared]
    J --> K[Optimal Threshold T_star]
    K --> L[Apply T_star to Image]
    L --> M[Binary Output g of x,y]
```

## 4.3 Thresholding Taxonomy — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thresholding Techniques] --> B[Global Thresholding]
    A --> C[Local Thresholding]
    A --> D[Multilevel Thresholding]
    A --> E[Multiband Thresholding]
    B --> B1[Manual Selection]
    B --> B2[Iterative Method]
    B --> B3[Otsu Method]
    B --> B4[Histogram Valley Method]
    B --> B5[Triangle Method]
    C --> C1[Mean of Local Window]
    C --> C2[Niblack Method]
    C --> C3[Sauvola Method]
    C --> C4[Bernsen Method]
    D --> D1[Multilevel Otsu]
    D --> D2[Recursive Thresholding]
    E --> E1[Color Space Conversion First]
    E --> E2[Per-Band Independent T]
```

## 4.4 Histogram Interpretation Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Hist[Histogram Interpretation for Thresholding]
        A1[Bimodal Histogram] --> A2[Two Clear Peaks]
        A2 --> A3[Valley Between Peaks = Optimal T]
        B1[Multimodal Histogram] --> B2[Multiple Peaks]
        B2 --> B3[Multiple T Values Needed]
        C1[Unimodal Histogram] --> C2[Single Peak]
        C2 --> C3[Thresholding Not Recommended]
    end
```

## 4.5 Iterative Thresholding Convergence Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize T_0] --> B[Segment Image at T_0]
    B --> C[Compute Mean of G_0: mu_0]
    B --> D[Compute Mean of G_1: mu_1]
    C --> E[T_new = mu_0 + mu_1 divided by 2]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Absolute T_new minus T_k less than epsilon?}
    F -->|No| G[Set T_k = T_new]
    G --> B
    F -->|Yes| H[Converged Threshold T_star]
    H --> I[Apply T_star to Final Binary Image]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define image segmentation. Explain how thresholding acts as the simplest segmentation technique.**

**Model Answer:**
Image segmentation is the process of partitioning a digital image $f(x,y)$ into multiple non-overlapping regions $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ such that every pixel is assigned to exactly one region, and each region is homogeneous with respect to a chosen property (intensity, color, texture).

Thresholding is the simplest segmentation technique because it uses a **single intensity value $T$** to classify pixels into object or background in a single pass. The operation $g(x,y) = 1$ if $f(x,y) \geq T$, else $0$ requires no neighborhood analysis, no iterative optimization, and no prior knowledge of the image content — making it computationally $O(N)$ for an image of $N$ pixels.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is Otsu's method? State its key principle.**

**Model Answer:**
Otsu's method is an **automatic global threshold selection algorithm** proposed by Nobuyuki Otsu in 1979. Its key principle is:

> *The optimal threshold $T^*$ is the one that **maximizes the between-class variance** $\sigma_B^2$ of the two pixel classes (foreground and background).*

This is equivalent to maximizing the separability between the two classes. Mathematically:

$$T^* = \arg\max_{t \in [0, L-1]} \left\{ w_0(t) \cdot w_1(t) \cdot \left[ \mu_0(t) - \mu_1(t) \right]^2 \right\}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Choose **either** Question A or Question B. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the fundamental concept of image segmentation. Discuss the various categories of thresholding techniques with their operational differences. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-bit grayscale image (intensity levels 0–2), the histogram probabilities are given as:
- $p_0 = 0.40, \quad p_1 = 0.35, \quad p_2 = 0.25$

Apply **Otsu's method** to find the optimal threshold. Show all calculations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

Image segmentation partitions $f(x,y)$ into $n$ disjoint regions satisfying:
1. $\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i = f(x,y)$ (complete coverage)
2. $R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$ (disjointness)
3. Each $R_i$ is connected (spatial coherence)
4. Adjacent regions $R_i, R_j$ are distinct under predicate $P$

**Thresholding categories:**

| Category | Threshold Behavior | Use Case |
|----------|-------------------|----------|
| Global | Fixed $T$ for whole image | Bimodal histograms |
| Local/Adaptive | $T(x,y)$ varies spatially | Non-uniform illumination |
| Multilevel | Multiple $T$ values | Multimodal histograms |
| Multiband | Different $T$ per color channel | Color image segmentation |

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1** for the visual representation of the thresholding pipeline.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1: Compute Global Mean $\mu_T$**

$$\mu_T = (0)(0.40) + (1)(0.35) + (2)(0.25) = 0 + 0.35 + 0.50 = 0.85$$

**Step 2: Evaluate $\sigma_B^2(t)$ for each candidate $t = 0, 1$**

**Case $t = 0$:**
- $w_0 = p_0 = 0.40, \quad \mu_0 = 0$
- $w_1 = p_1 + p_2 = 0.60$

$$\mu_1 = \frac{(1)(0.35) + (2)(0.25)}{0.60} = \frac{0.35 + 0.50}{0.60} = \frac{0.85}{0.60} \approx 1.4167$$

$$\sigma_B^2(0) = (0.40)(0.60)(0 - 1.4167)^2 = 0.24 \cdot 2.0070 \approx 0.4817$$

**Case $t = 1$:**
- $w_0 = p_0 + p_1 = 0.75$

$$\mu_0 = \frac{(0)(0.40) + (1)(0.35)}{0.75} = \frac{0.35}{0.75} \approx 0.4667$$

- $w_1 = p_2 = 0.25, \quad \mu_1 = 2$

$$\sigma_B^2(1) = (0.75)(0.25)(0.4667 - 2)^2 = 0.1875 \cdot 2.3511 \approx 0.4408$$

**Step 3: Compare and Select**

| $t$ | $\sigma_B^2(t)$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | **0.4817** ← MAX |
| 1 | 0.4408 |

**Optimal Threshold: $T^* = 0$**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between global thresholding and adaptive thresholding. Under what conditions does global thresholding fail, and how does adaptive thresholding overcome these limitations? Give one real-world scenario for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An 8-bit image has the following histogram counts for selected intensity levels:

| Intensity $i$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 |
|---------------|---|---|---|---|
| Count $n_i$ | 100 | 300 | 400 | 200 |

Total number of pixels $N = 1000$. Compute the optimal threshold using **Otsu's method** by evaluating all relevant thresholds. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Global Thresholding:**

A single threshold $T$ is applied uniformly across the entire image. The threshold is typically derived from the global histogram.

$$g(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f(x,y) \geq T \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Adaptive Thresholding:**

The threshold varies spatially based on local neighborhood properties:

$$T(x,y) = f\left( \text{local statistics around } (x,y) \right)$$

Common variants use local mean $\mu_{\text{local}}$, local median, or local standard deviation $\sigma_{\text{local}}$ within a window of size $k \times k$.

**Conditions where global thresholding fails:**
1. **Non-uniform illumination** — shadows, gradients, vignetting
2. **Low contrast images** — narrow intensity range
3. **Unimodal histograms** — no clear object/background separation

**How adaptive overcomes this:** By computing a *local* threshold that adapts to the local mean intensity, the method effectively normalizes for illumination variation. A pixel that appears "dark" because of shadow is still correctly classified if its local neighborhood is also dark.

**Real-world scenarios:**
- **Global:** Segmenting printed text on a uniformly-lit scanner bed.
- **Adaptive:** Segmenting handwritten text on an aged, yellowed paper document scanned under uneven lighting.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Step 1: Compute Probabilities**

$$p_2 = 100/1000 = 0.10, \quad p_4 = 0.30, \quad p_6 = 0.40, \quad p_8 = 0.20$$

Sum: $0.10 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✓

**Step 2: Global Mean $\mu_T$**

$$\mu_T = (2)(0.10) + (4)(0.30) + (6)(0.40) + (8)(0.20)$$

$$\mu_T = 0.20 + 1.20 + 2.40 + 1.60 = 5.40$$

**Step 3: Evaluate Otsu's Criterion**

Since data is available only at $i \in \{2, 4, 6, 8\}$, the candidate thresholds are $t = 2, 4, 6$.

**Case $t = 2$:**
- $w_0 = 0.10, \quad \mu_0 = 2$
- $w_1 = 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 0.90$

$$\mu_1 = \frac{(4)(0.30) + (6)(0.40) + (8)(0.20)}{0.90} = \frac{1.20 + 2.40 + 1.60}{0.90} = \frac{5.20}{0.90} \approx 5.778$$

$$\sigma_B^2(2) = (0.10)(0.90)(2 - 5.778)^2 = 0.09 \cdot 14.272 \approx 1.285$$

**Case $t = 4$:**
- $w_0 = 0.10 + 0.30 = 0.40$

$$\mu_0 = \frac{(2)(0.10) + (4)(0.30)}{0.40} = \frac{0.20 + 1.20}{0.40} = \frac{1.40}{0.40} = 3.50$$

- $w_1 = 0.40 + 0.20 = 0.60$

$$\mu_1 = \frac{(6)(0.40) + (8)(0.20)}{0.60} = \frac{2.40 + 1.60}{0.60} = \frac{4.00}{0.60} \approx 6.667$$

$$\sigma_B^2(4) = (0.40)(0.60)(3.50 - 6.667)^2 = 0.24 \cdot 10.029 \approx 2.407$$

**Case $t = 6$:**
- $w_0 = 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.40 = 0.80$

$$\mu_0 = \frac{(2)(0.10) + (4)(0.30) + (6)(0.40)}{0.80} = \frac{0.20 + 1.20 + 2.40}{0.80} = \frac{3.80}{0.80} = 4.75$$

- $w_1 = 0.20, \quad \mu_1 = 8$

$$\sigma_B^2(6) = (0.80)(0.20)(4.75 - 8)^2 = 0.16 \cdot 10.5625 \approx 1.690$$

**Step 4: Selection**

| $t$ | $\sigma_B^2(t)$ |
|:---:|:---:|
| 2 | 1.285 |
| **4** | **2.407** ← MAX |
| 6 | 1.690 |

**Optimal Threshold: $T^* = 4$**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students commonly lose marks in Otsu's method calculations for the following reasons:
>
> 1. **Arithmetic Error in $\mu_1$**: Forgetting to divide by $w_1$ when computing the class mean for the foreground. Always normalize: $\mu_k = \frac{\sum i \cdot p_i}{\sum p_i}$.
>
> 2. **Sweeping $\sigma_B^2$ Computation**: Writing $\sigma_B^2 = w_0(\mu_0 - \mu_T)^2 + w_1(\mu_1 - \mu_T)^2$ instead of the simplified form $\sigma_B^2 = w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$. Both are correct, but the latter is faster.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to List All Candidates**: For an $L$-level image, $t$ must range from $0$ to $L-1$. Missing even one candidate can produce a wrong $T^*$.
>
> 4. **Not Stating Units or Interpretation**: Always conclude with a statement like *"Therefore, pixels with intensity $\geq 4$ are classified as foreground"*.
>
> 5. **Confusing $\sigma_B^2$ with $\sigma_W^2$**: Otsu maximizes $\sigma_B^2$, not $\sigma_W^2$ — these are inversely related. State this explicitly in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Image Segmentation — Thresholding**

- **Definition:** Segmentation = partitioning image into meaningful regions. Thresholding = simplest segmentation using intensity comparison with $T$.

- **Core Equation:** $g(x,y) = 1$ if $f(x,y) \geq T$, else $0$. Output is binary.

- **Global vs. Local:** Global uses one $T$ for the whole image; local (adaptive) uses $T(x,y)$ computed from local windows.

- **Otsu's Method:** Automatically finds $T^*$ that **maximizes between-class variance** $\sigma_B^2 = w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$.

- **Key Identity:** $\sigma_T^2 = \sigma_W^2 + \sigma_B^2$ (total variance = within + between). Since $\sigma_T^2$ is constant, maximizing $\sigma_B^2 \equiv$ minimizing $\sigma_W^2$.

- **Iterative Method:** Initialize $T_0$, compute group means, update $T_{k+1} = (\mu_0 + \mu_1)/2$, repeat until convergence.

- **Adaptive Methods:** Niblack ($T = \mu_{\text{local}} - C$), Sauvola, Bernsen — best for uneven illumination.

- **Histogram Shape Matters:** Bimodal → single global $T$; Multimodal → multilevel; Unimodal → avoid thresholding.

- **Limitations:** Sensitive to noise, fails under non-uniform lighting, unimodal histograms, and when object/background intensities overlap.

- **Multilevel Extension:** Otsu's method generalizes to $k$-class via maximization of $\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i (\mu_i - \mu_T)^2$.

- **Applications:** Medical imaging, OCR, quality control, satellite imaging, biometrics, autonomous vehicles.

- **Computational Complexity:** Global/Otsu = $O(N + L^2)$; Adaptive = $O(N \cdot k^2)$ where $k$ is window size.

- **Exam Mantra:** Always show full computation steps, justify the choice of $T$, and state the final classification rule clearly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threshold Detection Methods - Optimal Thresholding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT525 - FUNDAMENTALS OF DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Threshold Detection Methods - Optimal Thresholding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Thresholding** is a class of image segmentation techniques that automatically determines the most suitable intensity threshold value $T$ by optimizing a mathematically defined objective function derived from the image histogram. The goal is to partition the image pixels into two (or more) groups — typically **foreground (object)** and **background** — such that the separation between these classes is maximized according to a chosen statistical criterion.

The two most prominent optimal thresholding approaches prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Otsu's Method (1979)** — Maximizes the *between-class variance* $\sigma_B^2$ of the histogram to find the threshold that best separates two intensity classes.
2. **Iterative Threshold Selection Method** — Converges to a stable threshold through repeated computation of class means until the threshold stops changing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Terminology Mapping (KTU 2024):**
> - Threshold = $T$ (scalar intensity value, $0 \le T \le L-1$)
> - $L$ = total number of grey levels (usually **256** for 8-bit images)
> - $p_i$ = normalized histogram probability at grey level $i$
> - Class 0 (background) = pixels with intensity $\le T$
> - Class 1 (object) = pixels with intensity $> T$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a **fruit-sorting machine** at a packing facility. Your job is to separate **dark plums** from **light apples** on a conveyor belt. A single light-sensor reading tells you how dark or bright each fruit is. You must choose *one brightness cut-off number* (the threshold) so that everything darker is a "plum" and everything brighter is an "apple."

- If you set the cut-off **too low** → apples get wrongly classified as plums.
- If you set the cut-off **too high** → plums get wrongly classified as apples.
- The **best** cut-off is the number that **maximizes the difference** between the two groups.

Optimal thresholding does exactly this — it scans every possible cut-off value from $0$ to $255$ and mathematically picks the one where the *contrast* between the dark group and the bright group is at its absolute maximum. That "most contrast-producing" cut-off is the **optimal threshold** $T^*$.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid:** *Otsu finds the brightness that makes the two sides of the histogram look as different from each other as possible.*

---

## 1.3 Physical / Image Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of grey levels | $L$ | **256** | levels |
| Pixel intensity range | $0$ to $L-1$ | $0$ – $255$ | intensity units |
| Histogram bin probability | $p_i = n_i / N$ | $0 \le p_i \le 1$ | dimensionless |
| Total pixel count | $N$ | varies | pixels |
| Threshold output | $T^*$ | integer in $[0, 255]$ | intensity level |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Otsu's Method — Theoretical Foundation

Otsu's method treats the histogram of the image as a **discrete probability distribution** and searches for the threshold $T$ that minimizes the weighted intra-class variance (or, equivalently, maximizes the inter-class variance).

### 2.1.1 Class Probabilities (Cumulative Densities)

For a candidate threshold $T$, the probabilities of the two classes are:

$$
q_1(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{T} p_i \qquad,\qquad q_2(T) = \sum_{i=T+1}^{L-1} p_i
$$

By construction, $q_1(T) + q_2(T) = 1$.

### 2.1.2 Class Means (Intensity Centroids)

$$
\mu_1(T) = \frac{1}{q_1(T)} \sum_{i=0}^{T} i \cdot p_i \qquad,\qquad \mu_2(T) = \frac{1}{q_2(T)} \sum_{i=T+1}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i
$$

### 2.1.3 Global (Total) Image Mean

$$
\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i
$$

### 2.1.4 Between-Class Variance (Objective Function)

$$
\sigma_B^2(T) = q_1(T)\,q_2(T)\bigl[\mu_1(T) - \mu_2(T)\bigr]^2
$$

### 2.1.5 Optimal Threshold

$$
T^* = \underset{0 \le T \le L-1}{\arg\max}\; \sigma_B^2(T)
$$

### 2.1.6 Within-Class Variance (Equivalent Objective)

Since total variance $\sigma_T^2 = \sigma_W^2 + \sigma_B^2$ is constant, maximizing $\sigma_B^2$ is identical to minimizing:

$$
\sigma_W^2(T) = q_1(T)\,\sigma_1^2(T) + q_2(T)\,\sigma_2^2(T)
$$

This dual interpretation is what makes Otsu's method computationally elegant.

---

## 2.2 Iterative Threshold Selection Method — Theory

When histogram-based optimum search is computationally heavy, the **iterative procedure** offers a fast closed-form convergence:

1. Choose an initial threshold $T_0$ (commonly the **mean intensity** of the entire image).
2. Segment the image using $T_0$ → obtain $G_1$ (pixels $\le T_0$) and $G_2$ (pixels $> T_0$).
3. Compute the mean intensity of each group: $m_1$ and $m_2$.
4. Update threshold: $T_{k+1} = \frac{1}{2}(m_1 + m_2)$.
5. Repeat steps 2–4 until $T_{k+1} = T_k$.

The procedure converges in typically 3–6 iterations.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas required for KTU 2024 ESE — Optimal Thresholding (Module 3).**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_i = n_i / N$ | Normalized histogram at level $i$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $q_1(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{T} p_i$ | Background class probability | dimensionless |
| 3 | $q_2(T) = \sum_{i=T+1}^{L-1} p_i$ | Foreground class probability | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\mu_1(T) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{T} i\,p_i}{q_1(T)}$ | Mean of class 1 (background) | intensity level |
| 5 | $\mu_2(T) = \frac{\sum_{i=T+1}^{L-1} i\,p_i}{q_2(T)}$ | Mean of class 2 (object) | intensity level |
| 6 | $\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i\,p_i$ | Global image mean | intensity level |
| 7 | $\sigma_B^2(T) = q_1 q_2 (\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2$ | Between-class variance | intensity$^2$ |
| 8 | $\sigma_W^2(T) = q_1 \sigma_1^2 + q_2 \sigma_2^2$ | Within-class variance | intensity$^2$ |
| 9 | $T^* = \arg\max_T \sigma_B^2(T)$ | Otsu optimal threshold | integer level |
| 10 | $T_{k+1} = \frac{1}{2}(m_1 + m_2)$ | Iterative update rule | intensity level |
| 11 | $\eta(T) = \sigma_B^2(T) / \sigma_T^2$ | Separability measure (0 to 1) | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Otsu thresholding is used in **document binarization** (OCR pre-processing), **medical imaging** (tumour boundary detection in MRI/CT), **satellite imagery** (road/building extraction), and **industrial defect detection** on assembly lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Between-Class Variance $\sigma_B^2(T)$

We start from the **total variance** of the image intensities treated as a random variable $X$ with probability mass function $p_i$:

$$
\sigma_T^2 = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1}(i - \mu_T)^2\,p_i
$$

Decompose this sum into two regions split by threshold $T$:

$$
\sigma_T^2 = \sum_{i=0}^{T}(i - \mu_T)^2 p_i + \sum_{i=T+1}^{L-1}(i - \mu_T)^2 p_i
$$

For each region, we add and subtract the local class mean to expose the local variance:

$$
(i - \mu_T)^2 = \bigl[(i - \mu_k) + (\mu_k - \mu_T)\bigr]^2
$$

where $k \in \{1, 2\}$ for the two classes. Expanding:

$$
(i - \mu_T)^2 = (i - \mu_k)^2 + 2(i - \mu_k)(\mu_k - \mu_T) + (\mu_k - \mu_T)^2
$$

Substituting back and summing, the cross-term vanishes because $\sum_{i \in C_k}(i - \mu_k) p_i = 0$ by definition of the mean. We are left with:

$$
\sigma_T^2 = q_1 \sigma_1^2 + q_1(\mu_1 - \mu_T)^2 + q_2 \sigma_2^2 + q_2(\mu_2 - \mu_T)^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_T^2 = \sigma_W^2 + \sigma_B^2
$$

Since $\sigma_T^2$ is **constant** for a given image, we conclude:

$$
\max_T \sigma_B^2(T) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \min_T \sigma_W^2(T)
$$

The simpler form of $\sigma_B^2$ is obtained using the identity $q_1 \mu_1 + q_2 \mu_2 = \mu_T$, which gives:

$$
q_1(\mu_1 - \mu_T)^2 + q_2(\mu_2 - \mu_T)^2 = q_1 q_2(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2
$$

Hence the **canonical Otsu formula**:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_B^2(T) = q_1(T)\,q_2(T)\,\bigl[\mu_1(T) - \mu_2(T)\bigr]^2}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem.** A 4-grey-level image ($L=4$, levels $\{0,1,2,3\}$) has a normalized histogram $p_0 = 0.40$, $p_1 = 0.30$, $p_2 = 0.20$, $p_3 = 0.10$. Find the Otsu optimal threshold.

### Step 1 — Compute Global Mean $\mu_T$

$$
\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{3} i\,p_i = 0(0.40) + 1(0.30) + 2(0.20) + 3(0.10) = 0 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.30 = 1.00
$$

### Step 2 — Test Each Candidate Threshold $T \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$

**Case $T = 0$ (background = {0}, object = {1,2,3}):**

$$
q_1 = 0.40,\quad \mu_1 = 0,\quad q_2 = 0.60,\quad \mu_2 = \frac{0.30 + 0.40 + 0.30}{0.60} = \frac{1.00}{0.60} = 1.6\overline{6}
$$

$$
\sigma_B^2(0) = 0.40 \times 0.60 \times (0 - 1.6\overline{6})^2 = 0.24 \times 2.7\overline{7} = 0.6667
$$

**Case $T = 1$ (background = {0,1}, object = {2,3}):**

$$
q_1 = 0.70,\quad \mu_1 = \frac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286,\quad q_2 = 0.30,\quad \mu_2 = \frac{0.40 + 0.30}{0.30} = 2.3333
$$

$$
\sigma_B^2(1) = 0.70 \times 0.30 \times (0.4286 - 2.3333)^2 = 0.21 \times 3.6190 = 0.7600
$$

**Case $T = 2$ (background = {0,1,2}, object = {3}):**

$$
q_1 = 0.90,\quad \mu_1 = \frac{0.30 + 0.40}{0.90} = 0.7778,\quad q_2 = 0.10,\quad \mu_2 = 3.00
$$

$$
\sigma_B^2(2) = 0.90 \times 0.10 \times (0.7778 - 3.00)^2 = 0.09 \times 4.9383 = 0.4444
$$

**Case $T = 3$ (everything is background):** $q_2 = 0$, so $\sigma_B^2(3) = 0$.

### Step 3 — Tabulated Results (Analytical Matrix)

| $T$ | $q_1$ | $q_2$ | $\mu_1$ | $\mu_2$ | $\sigma_B^2(T)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.40 | 0.60 | 0.0000 | 1.6667 | **0.6667** |
| 1 | 0.70 | 0.30 | 0.4286 | 2.3333 | **0.7600** ← max |
| 2 | 0.90 | 0.10 | 0.7778 | 3.0000 | 0.4444 |
| 3 | 1.00 | 0.00 | 1.0000 | — | 0.0000 |

### Step 4 — Conclusion

$$
T^* = \underset{T}{\arg\max}\,\sigma_B^2(T) = 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The optimal Otsu threshold is $T^* = 1$. Any pixel with intensity $> 1$ is assigned to the object class; any pixel $\le 1$ is assigned to the background.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Iterative Threshold Selection

**Problem.** A $4 \times 4$ image has pixel intensities:
$\{10, 12, 14, 200, 202, 205, 8, 11, 13, 198, 201, 207, 9, 15, 199, 203\}$.
Find the optimal threshold using the iterative method.

### Iteration 0 — Initialization

Initial threshold = mean of all pixels:

$$
T_0 = \frac{1}{16}\sum \text{intensities} = \frac{1707}{16} = 106.6875
$$

### Iteration 1 — Split and Update
