topics.title: Control Strategies Templating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT525 - FUNDAMENTALS OF DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Strategies Templating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Control Strategies Templating** in Digital Image Processing (DIP) refers to the systematic design and application of **template-based control methodologies** where predefined reference patterns (templates) are used to guide, regulate, or direct image processing operations such as object detection, segmentation, tracking, and quality inspection. A template is a small reference image (sub-image) $T$ of size $m \times n$ that is systematically compared against every possible spatial location within a larger search image $I$ of size $M \times N$ to identify regions of interest (ROI) based on **similarity metrics** and **threshold-based decision rules**.

The control strategy aspect refers to the decision-making logic that determines:
- **When** template matching should be triggered (initiation control)
- **How** the template should be spatially searched (search control)
- **What** similarity threshold separates "match" from "non-match" (acceptance control)
- **How** the system should respond post-match (response control)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Template matching is classified as a **pattern-recognition-driven control strategy** under the broader umbrella of image-based decision systems. It is the foundation for **machine vision**, **biometric authentication**, **medical imaging diagnostics**, and **autonomous navigation systems**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are looking for your friend **Rahul** in a crowded college auditorium during Onam celebrations. You mentally carry a **mental snapshot** (template) of his face — his round face, spectacles, and beard. Your eyes systematically scan the crowd (search image). At each person you look at, your brain instantly computes a **similarity score** between what you see and your mental template.

- If the score is **high** (≥ threshold) → "Yes, that's Rahul!" (match accepted)
- If the score is **low** (< threshold) → "Move on" (match rejected)

This is **exactly** how template-based control strategies work in image processing:
- **Mental template** ↔ **Reference image** $T(x, y)$
- **Crowd** ↔ **Search image** $I(x, y)$
- **Visual comparison** ↔ **Similarity metric** $D(u, v)$ or $R(u, v)$
- **Decision threshold** ↔ **Acceptance control parameter** $T_h$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for engineers?** Template-based control is the precursor to modern **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)**. Understanding classical template matching gives you the conceptual foundation to understand deep learning feature detection layers.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Template | $T$ | Reference sub-image of size $m \times n$ |
| Search Image | $I$ | Larger image of size $M \times N$ |
| Search Window Position | $(u, v)$ | Top-left coordinate of template placement |
| Similarity Metric | $D$ or $R$ | Quantitative measure of match quality |
| Acceptance Threshold | $T_h$ | Critical value for match decision |
| False Positive | FP | Non-match incorrectly accepted |
| False Negative | FN | True match incorrectly rejected |
| **Detection Rate** | $\eta$ | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ expressed in **%** |
| **False Alarm Rate** | $\alpha$ | $\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ expressed in **%** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The control strategy templating pipeline follows a structured five-stage control loop:

1. **Template Acquisition Stage**
   - Reference image $T$ is captured, normalized, and stored.
   - Preprocessing (noise removal, intensity normalization) is applied to ensure invariance.

2. **Search Space Definition Stage**
   - Define search region: $u \in [1, M-m+1]$, $v \in [1, N-n+1]$.
   - Total search positions: $(M-m+1)(N-n+1)$.

3. **Similarity Computation Stage**
   - At each position $(u, v)$, compute similarity metric between $T$ and the candidate region $I(u:v+u-1, v:v+n-1)$.
   - The metric quantifies the degree of correspondence.

4. **Threshold-Based Decision Stage (Control Core)**
   - Compare computed metric with threshold $T_h$.
   - Output a binary control signal: MATCH (1) or NO MATCH (0).

5. **Response / Action Stage**
   - On match: trigger downstream action (alarm, marking, tracking, classification).
   - On no match: continue search or terminate.

### 2.2 The Three Principal Similarity Metrics

#### A. Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) — Distance-Based

$$
SSD(u, v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[ I(u+i-1, v+j-1) - T(i, j) \right]^2
$$

- **Range:** $[0, 255^2 \cdot m \cdot n]$ (for 8-bit images)
- **Best match:** Minimum SSD value
- **Intuition:** Penalizes large intensity differences quadratically.

#### B. Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD) — Robust Distance

$$
SAD(u, v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left\vert I(u+i-1, v+j-1) - T(i, j) \right\vert
$$

- **Range:** $[0, 255 \cdot m \cdot n]$
- **Best match:** Minimum SAD value
- **Intuition:** Linear penalty, more robust to outliers than SSD.

#### C. Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) — Correlation-Based

$$
NCC(u, v) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[I(u+i-1, v+j-1) - \bar{I}_{u,v}\right]\left[T(i,j) - \bar{T}\right]}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[I(u+i-1, v+j-1) - \bar{I}_{u,v}\right]^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[T(i,j) - \bar{T}\right]^2}}
$$

- **Range:** $[-1, +1]$
- **Best match:** Maximum NCC value (ideally = 1)
- **Intuition:** Invariant to linear brightness and contrast changes.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Best Match Criterion | Threshold Rule | Units |
|--------------|----------|----------------------|----------------|-------|
| **SSD** | $\sum \sum (I - T)^2$ | Minimize | $SSD \leq T_h$ | intensity$^2$ |
| **SAD** | $\sum \sum \vert I - T \vert$ | Minimize | $SAD \leq T_h$ | intensity |
| **NCC** | $\frac{\sum(I-\bar{I})(T-\bar{T})}{\sqrt{\sum(I-\bar{I})^2 \sum(T-\bar{T})^2}}$ | Maximize | $NCC \geq T_h$ | dimensionless |
| **Mean Absolute Error** | $\frac{SAD}{m \cdot n}$ | Minimize | $MAE \leq T_h$ | intensity/pixel |
| **Match Position** | $(u^*, v^*) = \arg\min/\max D(u,v)$ | — | — | pixels |
| **Computational Cost** | $(M-m+1)(N-n+1) \cdot m \cdot n$ | — | — | operations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** When using `|` in formulas inside markdown tables, NEVER use the pipe character `|` directly. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax. The SAD formula above uses `\vert` correctly.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Control Strategy Templating Application |
|-------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Industrial Automation** | PCB defect detection on assembly lines |
| **Medical Imaging** | Tumor region identification in MRI/CT scans |
| **Automotive** | Traffic sign recognition in ADAS systems |
| **Biometrics** | Fingerprint and iris pattern matching |
| **Surveillance** | Suspect identification in CCTV footage |
| **Robotics** | Visual servoing for object grasping |
| **Remote Sensing** | Cloud masking and feature extraction from satellite imagery |

### 2.5 Control Strategy Variants

1. **Fixed Template Strategy** — Single static reference image; sensitive to scale/rotation.
2. **Multi-Resolution Strategy** — Hierarchical search (coarse-to-fine); reduces computation.
3. **Multi-Template Strategy** — Multiple references; handles intra-class variation.
4. **Deformable Template Strategy** — Elastic deformation of template; handles non-rigid patterns.
5. **Adaptive Template Strategy** — Template updates online based on scene statistics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From SSD to Normalized Cross-Correlation

The NCC metric is derived as a **statistical improvement** over the basic SSD formulation to achieve invariance to linear intensity transformations. We derive it step by step.

#### Step 1: Starting with the Raw Squared Error

At search position $(u, v)$, the raw squared error between the search region and the template is:

$$
E(u, v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[I(u+i-1, v+j-1) - T(i, j)\right]^2
$$

**Logic:** This expands into three terms when we square the difference.

#### Step 2: Algebraic Expansion of the Squared Term

$$
E(u, v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[I^2 - 2 \cdot I \cdot T + T^2\right]
$$

**Logic:** Using the identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$.

#### Step 3: Separation into Independent Terms

$$
E(u, v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}I^2 - 2\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}I \cdot T + \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}T^2
$$

**Logic:** The first term depends only on the search region, the middle term is the cross-term, and the last term is a constant for a given template.

#### Step 4: Observation — $\sum I^2$ is Search-Position-Dependent

The first term $\sum I^2$ varies with $(u, v)$, which corrupts the matching score when the search region has variable brightness.

**Logic:** A bright region will have a large $\sum I^2$ even if it does NOT match the template, biasing the result.

#### Step 5: Introduction of Mean Subtraction for Invariance

Define the local mean of the search region and template:

$$
\bar{I}_{u,v} = \frac{1}{m \cdot n}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}I(u+i-1, v+j-1)
$$

$$
\bar{T} = \frac{1}{m \cdot n}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}T(i, j)
$$

**Logic:** Subtracting the mean removes the DC (average brightness) component, achieving brightness invariance.

#### Step 6: Define Mean-Centered Variables

$$
I'(u+i-1, v+j-1) = I(u+i-1, v+j-1) - \bar{I}_{u,v}
$$

$$
T'(i, j) = T(i, j) - \bar{T}
$$

**Logic:** These zero-mean variables capture only the *pattern structure*, not the absolute brightness.

#### Step 7: Construct the Cross-Correlation Function

The unnormalized cross-correlation is:

$$
C(u, v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}I' \cdot T' = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[I - \bar{I}_{u,v}\right]\left[T - \bar{T}\right]
$$

**Logic:** This measures how much the search region's deviation pattern aligns with the template's deviation pattern.

#### Step 8: Normalization for Scale Invariance

Divide by the product of standard deviations (square roots of variances) to achieve full normalization:

$$
\sigma_{I} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[I - \bar{I}_{u,v}\right]^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{T} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[T - \bar{T}\right]^2}
$$

#### Step 9: Final NCC Expression

$$
NCC(u, v) = \frac{C(u, v)}{\sigma_{I} \cdot \sigma_{T}} = \frac{\sum\sum\left[I - \bar{I}_{u,v}\right]\left[T - \bar{T}\right]}{\sqrt{\sum\sum\left[I - \bar{I}_{u,v}\right]^2 \cdot \sum\sum\left[T - \bar{T}\right]^2}}
$$

**Logic:** By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, $NCC \in [-1, +1]$. The value $+1$ indicates a perfect linear match, $-1$ indicates a perfect negative correlation, and $0$ indicates no correlation.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: SSD Calculation

**Problem:** A $3 \times 3$ template $T$ is searched in a $4 \times 4$ image $I$. Find the position of minimum SSD.

Given template:
$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Given image:
$$
I = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 3 & 7 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Solution — Compute SSD at valid positions $(u, v) \in \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2)\}$:**

#### At Position $(1,1)$: Candidate region is $I[1:3, 1:3]$

$$
I_{1,1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is identical to $T$, so:
$$
SSD(1,1) = 0
$$

#### At Position $(1,2)$: Candidate region is $I[1:3, 2:4]$

$$
I_{1,2} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 & 6 \\ 4 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Differences: $(0, 0, 0, -2, 0, 0, -3, 0, 0)$, squared sum:
$$
SSD(1,2) = 0+0+0+4+0+0+9+0+0 = 13
$$

#### At Position $(2,1)$: Candidate region is $I[2:4, 1:3]$

$$
I_{2,1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 5 \\ 3 & 7 & 8 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Differences: $(0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 1)$, squared sum:
$$
SSD(2,1) = 0+0+0+0+1+1+0+1+1 = 4
$$

#### At Position $(2,2)$: Candidate region is $I[2:4, 2:4]$

$$
I_{2,2} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 & 6 \\ 4 & 8 & 9 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Differences: $(1, 3, 3, 0, 3, 3, 0, 0, 0)$, squared sum:
$$
SSD(2,2) = 1+9+9+0+9+9+0+0+0 = 37
$$

#### Final Decision

$$
(u^*, v^*) = \arg\min_{u,v} SSD(u,v) = (1, 1), \quad SSD_{\min} = 0
$$

This indicates a **perfect match** at position $(1, 1)$, which is consistent with the visual observation that the top-left $3 \times 3$ sub-matrix of $I$ is exactly equal to $T$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Insight:** The position of minimum SSD corresponds to the global best match. In practical control strategies, we set $T_h = $ some small constant above $SSD_{\min}$ to allow for minor image variations (e.g., sensor noise).

### 3.3 Computational Complexity Analysis

Total operations for full exhaustive search:

$$
C_{total} = (M - m + 1)(N - n + 1) \cdot m \cdot n \cdot K
$$

where $K$ is the operations per pixel metric (e.g., $K = 3$ for SAD with subtraction, absolute value, and accumulation).

**Example:** For $M = N = 512$, $m = n = 32$:
$$
C_{total} = (481)^2 \cdot (32)^2 \cdot 3 \approx 7.13 \times 10^{8} \text{ operations}
$$

This motivates the use of **fast search strategies** such as the **2D log search**, **three-step search**, or **diamond search** used in motion estimation standards.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Template Matching Control Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Image Acquisition] --> B[Preprocess Search Image I]
    B --> C[Load Template T from Database]
    C --> D[Initialize Search Position u=1, v=1]
    D --> E[Extract Candidate Window W from I at u,v]
    E --> F[Compute Similarity Metric D u,v]
    F --> G{All Positions Searched?}
    G -- No --> H[Increment u,v Position]
    H --> E
    G -- Yes --> I[Find Global Best Match Position]
    I --> J{Best Score Meets Threshold T_h?}
    J -- No --> K[Output: NO MATCH FOUND]
    J -- Yes --> L[Output: MATCH at u_star, v_star]
    L --> M[Trigger Control Action: Alarm, Mark, Track]
    M --> N[End]
    K --> N
```

### 4.2 Control Strategy Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Control Strategies Templating] --> A1[Fixed Template]
    ROOT --> A2[Multi-Resolution Pyramid]
    ROOT --> A3[Multi-Template Ensemble]
    ROOT --> A4[Deformable Elastic]
    ROOT --> A5[Adaptive Online Update]
    A1 --> B1[Single Reference]
    A1 --> B2[Exhaustive Search]
    A2 --> C1[Coarse-to-Fine Search]
    A2 --> C2[Gaussian Pyramid Levels]
    A3 --> D1[Class Prototypes]
    A3 --> D2[Bayesian Fusion]
    A4 --> E1[Snake Active Contour]
    A4 --> E2[Spring Mesh Models]
    A5 --> F1[Kalman Filter Update]
    A5 --> F2[Mean Shift Tracking]
```

### 4.3 Similarity Metric Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START[Choose Similarity Metric] --> Q1{Invariance Needed?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2[Brightness Constant]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3[Brightness Variable]
    Q2 --> M1[Use SSD or SAD]
    Q3 --> Q4{Contrast Variable?}
    Q4 -- No --> M2[Use Mean Subtracted SSD]
    Q4 -- Yes --> M3[Use NCC]
    M1 --> OUT1[Minimize Metric]
    M2 --> OUT2[Minimize Metric]
    M3 --> OUT3[Maximize Correlation]
```

### 4.4 Template Matching Search Space Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SearchSpace[Search Image I of size M by N]
        P1[u=1, v=1]
        P2[u=1, v=2]
        P3[u=1, v=N-n+1]
        P4[u=M-m+1, v=1]
        P5[u=M-m+1, v=N-n+1]
    end
    TEMPL[Template T m by n] -.->|Slides across| P1
    TEMPL -.->|Slides across| P2
    TEMPL -.->|Slides across| P3
    TEMPL -.->|Slides across| P4
    TEMPL -.->|Slides across| P5
    P1 --> SCORE[Similarity Map D u,v]
    P2 --> SCORE
    P3 --> SCORE
    P4 --> SCORE
    P5 --> SCORE
    SCORE --> MAX[Find Global Optimum]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define template matching in digital image processing. List any two similarity measures used in template matching.**

**Model Answer:**

Template matching is a fundamental control strategy in DIP where a small reference image (template) $T$ of size $m \times n$ is systematically compared with all possible sub-regions of a larger search image $I$ of size $M \times N$ to locate regions of interest. The comparison is quantified using a similarity metric, and the position with the optimal score is declared the match.

**Two similarity measures:**
1. **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD):** $SSD(u, v) = \sum\sum [I - T]^2$ — best match at minimum value.
2. **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC):** $NCC(u, v) = \frac{\sum(I-\bar{I})(T-\bar{T})}{\sqrt{\sum(I-\bar{I})^2 \sum(T-\bar{T})^2}}$ — best match at maximum value (close to +1).

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of the threshold parameter $T_h$ in a template-based control strategy. What happens if $T_h$ is set too low or too high?**

**Model Answer:**

The threshold $T_h$ is the **decision control parameter** in template matching that separates match from non-match outcomes. It operates on the output of the similarity metric.

- **If $T_h$ is set too low (strict):** The system becomes highly selective. Only near-perfect matches are accepted. This leads to a **high false negative rate (missed detections)** but a low false positive rate.
- **If $T_h$ is set too high (lenient):** The system accepts many candidates as matches. This leads to a **high false positive rate (false alarms)** but a low false negative rate.

**Engineering trade-off:** The optimal $T_h$ is determined using **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve analysis** to balance detection rate $\eta$ and false alarm rate $\alpha$.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE of the following questions.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for the **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)** similarity metric from the basic Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) formulation. Explain why NCC is preferred over SSD in real-world applications with variable lighting conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $2 \times 2$ template $T = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 10 \\ 15 & 20 \end{bmatrix}$ is searched in the $3 \times 3$ image $I = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 10 & 8 \\ 15 & 20 & 18 \\ 12 & 25 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$. Compute the SSD at all four valid positions and identify the best match location. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: Write the basic SSD expression**

$$
SSD(u, v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[I(u+i-1, v+j-1) - T(i, j)\right]^2
$$

**Step 2: Expand the squared term using $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$**

$$
SSD(u, v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}I^2 - 2\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}I \cdot T + \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}T^2
$$

**Step 3: Identify the problem — the $\sum I^2$ term depends on search position and corrupts the match score when brightness varies.**

**Step 4: Subtract the local mean to remove the DC brightness component**

Let $I' = I - \bar{I}_{u,v}$ and $T' = T - \bar{T}$. Construct the cross-correlation:

$$
C(u, v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}I' \cdot T'
$$

**Step 5: Normalize by the standard deviations to achieve scale invariance**

$$
NCC(u, v) = \frac{C(u, v)}{\sigma_I \cdot \sigma_T} = \frac{\sum\sum(I-\bar{I}_{u,v})(T-\bar{T})}{\sqrt{\sum\sum(I-\bar{I}_{u,v})^2 \cdot \sum\sum(T-\bar{T})^2}}
$$

**Why NCC is preferred under variable lighting:**

1. **Brightness invariance:** Mean subtraction removes the effect of additive illumination changes $I' = I + c$.
2. **Contrast invariance:** Normalization by $\sigma_I \cdot \sigma_T$ removes the effect of multiplicative gain changes $I' = a \cdot I$.
3. **Bounded output:** $NCC \in [-1, +1]$ provides a normalized confidence score, enabling easy thresholding.
4. **Statistical interpretation:** NCC is the **Pearson correlation coefficient** between the template and search region, with established statistical significance tests.

##### Model Solution — Part (b):

Valid search positions: $(u, v) \in \{(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2)\}$.

**At $(1,1)$: Candidate = $\begin{bmatrix} 5 & 10 \\ 15 & 20 \end{bmatrix}$**

Differences from $T$: $(0, 0, 0, 0)$
$$
SSD(1,1) = 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 0
$$

**At $(1,2)$: Candidate = $\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 8 \\ 20 & 18 \end{bmatrix}$**

Differences from $T$: $(5, -2, 5, -2)$, squared: $(25, 4, 25, 4)$
$$
SSD(1,2) = 25 + 4 + 25 + 4 = 58
$$

**At $(2,1)$: Candidate = $\begin{bmatrix} 15 & 20 \\ 12 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$**

Differences from $T$: $(0, 0, -3, 5)$, squared: $(0, 0, 9, 25)$
$$
SSD(2,1) = 0 + 0 + 9 + 25 = 34
$$

**At $(2,2)$: Candidate = $\begin{bmatrix} 20 & 18 \\ 25 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$**

Differences from $T$: $(5, -2, 0, 10)$, squared: $(25, 4, 0, 100)$
$$
SSD(2,2) = 25 + 4 + 0 + 100 = 129
$$

**Best Match:** $(u^*, v^*) = (1, 1)$ with $SSD_{\min} = 0$ — **perfect match**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **template-based control strategies** in image processing with a suitable block diagram. Differentiate between **fixed template** and **multi-template** strategies, listing two advantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a $5 \times 5$ search image and a $3 \times 3$ template, calculate the total number of search positions and the total number of multiplication operations required for SSD computation. Justify your answer with the relevant formula. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Concept:** Template-based control strategies use reference images (templates) to direct image processing operations such as detection, segmentation, and tracking. The control loop comprises template acquisition, search, similarity computation, threshold-based decision, and response generation.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

$$
\text{Input Image } I \rightarrow \text{Preprocessing} \rightarrow \text{Search Engine} \rightarrow \text{Similarity Metric Calculator}
$$
$$
\text{Template } T \rightarrow \text{Template Database} \rightarrow \text{Search Engine}
$$
$$
\text{Similarity Map} \rightarrow \text{Threshold Comparator} \rightarrow \text{Decision} \rightarrow \text{Control Action}
$$

**Fixed Template Strategy:**
- Uses ONE static reference image.
- **Advantages:** (1) Simple implementation, low memory; (2) Fast computation, suitable for real-time systems with controlled environments.

**Multi-Template Strategy:**
- Uses MULTIPLE reference images representing intra-class variations.
- **Advantages:** (1) Robust to pose, illumination, and occlusion variations; (2) Higher detection accuracy in unconstrained environments.

##### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1: Calculate the number of valid search positions**

Using the formula for search positions:
$$
P = (M - m + 1) \cdot (N - n + 1) = (5 - 3 + 1) \cdot (5 - 3 + 1) = 3 \cdot 3 = 9 \text{ positions}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total number of multiplication operations for SSD**

For each position, SSD requires:
- 1 multiplication per pixel for the difference term (or squaring)
- Total pixels in template: $m \cdot n = 3 \cdot 3 = 9$

Multiplications per position: 9 (one squaring operation per pixel)
Total multiplications:
$$
\text{Total} = P \cdot m \cdot n = 9 \cdot 9 = 81 \text{ multiplications}
$$

**Step 3: Justification**

The general formula for total SSD operations across the entire search space is:
$$
C_{SSD} = (M - m + 1)(N - n + 1) \cdot m \cdot n = 9 \cdot 9 = 81
$$

This exhaustive search complexity is **quadratic in image dimensions** and motivates the use of fast search algorithms in production systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SSD minimization with NCC maximization:** Students often forget that SSD finds the *minimum* while NCC finds the *maximum*. Mark deduction: **-2 marks**.
> 2. **Ignoring the constant term $\sum T^2$ in SSD:** When comparing two candidate regions, $\sum T^2$ is identical, so it can be dropped. Students who retain it unnecessarily complicate the calculation.
> 3. **Wrong search position formula:** Writing $M \cdot N$ instead of $(M-m+1)(N-n+1)$. The template cannot extend beyond image boundaries.
> 4. **Forgetting mean subtraction in NCC:** NCC is NOT the same as raw cross-correlation. Mean centering is the key to illumination invariance.
> 5. **Mixing up detection rate and false alarm rate:** These are inverse quantities; high detection rate with low false alarm rate is the engineering goal.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Template matching** is a control strategy that locates a reference pattern $T$ within a search image $I$ by exhaustively comparing at all valid positions.
- [x] The **search position count** is $(M-m+1)(N-n+1)$, where $M \times N$ is the image size and $m \times n$ is the template size.
- [x] **SSD (Sum of Squared Differences)** finds the *minimum* — sensitive to brightness changes.
- [x] **SAD (Sum of Absolute Differences)** finds the *minimum* — more robust to outliers than SSD.
- [x] **NCC (Normalized Cross-Correlation)** finds the *maximum* (close to +1) — invariant to linear brightness and contrast changes.
- [x] The **threshold $T_h$** controls the trade-off between **detection rate $\eta$** and **false alarm rate $\alpha$**.
- [x] **Fixed template** = single reference (fast, simple); **Multi-template** = multiple references (robust, accurate).
- [x] **Multi-resolution / pyramidal search** reduces computational cost by coarse-to-fine search.
- [x] **Deformable templates** handle non-rigid patterns (e.g., medical organs, handwritten characters).
- [x] NCC is bounded in $[-1, +1]$ and has a clear statistical interpretation as the Pearson correlation coefficient.
- [x] Template matching is the **conceptual foundation** for CNN-based feature detection in modern deep learning.
- [x] The total SSD computational cost is $O((M-m+1)(N-n+1) \cdot m \cdot n)$.
- [x] In NCC derivation, mean subtraction provides **brightness invariance**; variance normalization provides **contrast invariance**.
- [x] **ROC curve analysis** is the standard method for selecting the optimal $T_h$.
- [x] Template-based control is widely used in **biometrics, medical imaging, industrial inspection, and ADAS**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Preparation Tip:** For maximum marks, always (1) state the formula explicitly, (2) justify the choice of metric based on the application scenario, (3) show all calculation steps in numerical problems, and (4) conclude with a clear match position and acceptance verdict.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation Issues in Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT525 - FUNDAMENTALS OF DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaluation Issues in Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Segmentation Evaluation** refers to the systematic procedure of quantitatively or qualitatively assessing the performance, accuracy, and robustness of an image segmentation algorithm. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Fundamentals of Digital Image Processing*, segmentation evaluation is the critical post-processing step that determines *how well* a segmentation algorithm has partitioned an image into meaningful regions corresponding to real-world objects.

The formal definition as per the Gonzalez & Woods framework (universally adopted by KTU modules) states:

> **Segmentation Evaluation** is the process of measuring the degree of correspondence between the machine-generated segmented output and the ideal reference segmentation (ground truth) or, in the absence of ground truth, the internal statistical coherence of the segmentation result.

The evaluation framework is broadly classified into two paradigms:

| Paradigm | Description | Ground Truth Required? |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Supervised (Empirical Discrepancy) Methods** | Compares algorithm output against a human-annotated reference image | Yes |
| **Unsupervised (Empirical Goodness) Methods** | Evaluates segmentation purely based on intrinsic image features | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Evaluation Issues in Segmentation is a **high-weightage topic** in Module 3. Students must memorize the difference between *supervised* and *unsupervised* evaluation, the formulas for **Pratt's Figure of Merit**, **Dice Coefficient**, and **Jaccard Index**, as these are recurrent questions in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a student drawing a map of Kerala by hand. To judge how good the map is, you have two options:
1. **Compare it to a printed atlas** (you have a perfect reference, so you measure the distance between your coastline line and the real coastline) — this is **supervised evaluation**.
2. **Just look at the map and ask:** "Is the coastline smooth and continuous? Do the inland districts have a uniform color? Is there a clear boundary between two adjacent districts?" — this is **unsupervised evaluation**.

In the same way, segmentation evaluation asks: *"Did the computer do a good job separating objects from the background?"* If we already have a perfect hand-drawn reference (ground truth), we measure discrepancies. If we don't, we judge the segmentation by its internal consistency.

### 1.3 Why is Evaluation Necessary?

- **Algorithm Selection:** Choosing the best segmentation method (thresholding, edge-based, region-based) for a specific application.
- **Parameter Tuning:** Optimizing thresholds, number of clusters, or edge sensitivity.
- **Benchmarking:** Comparing novel algorithms against state-of-the-art techniques in research.
- **Quality Assurance:** In medical imaging (tumor detection), an error of even 2-3 pixels can be life-critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** The evaluation metrics must be **task-dependent**. A metric that works for medical imaging may not be suitable for satellite imagery.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Segmentation Evaluation Methods

The entire evaluation framework can be structured into the following hierarchy:

#### A. Analytical Methods
- Purely theoretical assessment of algorithm properties: processing time, memory, complexity, and prior assumptions. Does not analyze the actual output image.

#### B. Empirical Methods (the dominant category in KTU syllabus)
Empirical methods are further split into:

**1. Unsupervised Methods (Goodness Methods)** — No ground truth.
- **Intra-region uniformity:** Measures how homogeneous pixels are *within* a region.
- **Inter-region contrast:** Measures how distinct adjacent regions are.
- **Region shape measures:** Compactness, smoothness, boundary regularity.

**2. Supervised Methods (Discrepancy Methods)** — Uses ground truth.
- **Region-based (Area-based) measures:** Compare pixel labels (foreground vs. background).
- **Boundary-based (Contour-based) measures:** Compare extracted boundaries.
- **Combined measures:** Hybrid metrics.

### 2.2 Unsupervised Evaluation Metrics in Detail

**a) Intra-Region Uniformity (U):**
Measures the variance of pixel intensities within a single segmented region. Lower variance means a more uniform region.

$$U = 1 - \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{R} \frac{\sigma_i^2}{N_i}$$

where:
- $R$ = number of regions
- $\sigma_i^2$ = variance of intensities in region $i$
- $N_i$ = number of pixels in region $i$
- $C$ = a normalization constant (often $C = \sigma_{global}^2$)
- Value of $U$ ranges in $[0, 1]$, with **1** indicating perfect uniformity.

**b) Inter-Region Contrast (C):**
Measures the average gray-level difference between neighboring regions and their boundaries.

$$C = \frac{1}{|B|} \sum_{(p,q) \in B} \frac{\vert f(p) - f(q) \vert}{f(p) + f(q)}$$

where $B$ is the set of pixel pairs straddling region boundaries, $f(p)$ and $f(q)$ are intensities of pixels $p$ and $q$ on either side of the boundary.

### 2.3 Supervised Evaluation Metrics in Detail (The KTU Hot-Spot)

**a) Confusion Matrix (Pixel-level Classification):**

| | Predicted Foreground (P) | Predicted Background (N) |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Foreground (T)** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Background (F)** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

From this matrix, several indices are derived:

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**b) Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC):**
Also called the *Dice Coefficient* or *F1-Score*. Widely used in medical image segmentation.

$$DSC = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

- Range: $[0, 1]$, with **1** being perfect overlap.
- For $DSC \ge 0.7$, the segmentation is generally accepted in medical imaging.

**c) Jaccard Index (Intersection over Union - IoU):**

$$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$$

- Range: $[0, 1]$, with **1** being perfect overlap.
- Mathematically, $J$ and $DSC$ are related: $DSC = \dfrac{2J}{1 + J}$ and $J = \dfrac{DSC}{2 - DSC}$

**d) Pratt's Figure of Merit (Boundary-based Metric):**

The most famous boundary discrepancy metric in the KTU syllabus. It evaluates how close the detected boundary is to the ideal (ground truth) boundary.

$$FOM = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot d_i^2}$$

where:
- $N$ = total number of detected boundary pixels
- $d_i$ = perpendicular distance from the $i^{th}$ detected boundary pixel to the nearest ideal boundary pixel
- $\alpha$ = scaling constant (typically $\alpha = 1/9$)
- $FOM \in (0, 1]$, where **1** indicates a perfect match.

**e) Hausdorff Distance (Geometric Boundary Measure):**
Measures the worst-case distance between two boundaries.

$$H(A, B) = \max \left( \max_{a \in A} \min_{b \in B} d(a, b), \, \max_{b \in B} \min_{a \in A} d(b, a) \right)$$

A smaller Hausdorff distance indicates a better segmentation.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Type | Key Use |
|--------|---------|-------|------|---------|
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Supervised | Region-based |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Supervised | Region-based |
| **Dice Coefficient** | $\frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Supervised | Medical Imaging |
| **Jaccard Index (IoU)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Supervised | Object Detection |
| **Pratt's FOM** | $\frac{1}{N} \sum \frac{1}{1+\alpha d_i^2}$ | $(0,1]$ | Supervised | Edge-based |
| **Hausdorff Distance** | $\max(\max \min d, \max \min d)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Supervised | Boundary-based |
| **Intra-Region Uniformity** | $1 - \frac{1}{C} \sum \frac{\sigma_i^2}{N_i}$ | $[0,1]$ | Unsupervised | Goodness |
| **Inter-Region Contrast** | $\frac{1}{\vert B \vert} \sum \frac{\vert f(p) - f(q) \vert}{f(p) + f(q)}$ | $[0,1]$ | Unsupervised | Goodness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In autonomous vehicles, Dice Coefficient and IoU are used to evaluate road/lane segmentation. In medical AI, Hausdorff Distance is used to evaluate tumor boundary accuracy. In PCB inspection (a major KTU industrial application), Pratt's FOM is used to evaluate edge detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Jaccard Index and Dice Coefficient

This derivation is a **favourite KTU question** as it directly tests algebraic fluency. We will derive $DSC$ in terms of $J$.

**Starting Point (Definitions):**

Let $A$ = ground truth region, $B$ = segmented region.
- $J = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$
- $DSC = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$

**Step 1:** Recall the set identity:
$$\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into $J$:
$$J = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert}$$

**Step 3:** Let $x = \vert A \cap B \vert$, $y = \vert A \vert$, $z = \vert B \vert$. Then:
$$J = \frac{x}{y + z - x}$$

**Step 4:** Express $DSC$ using same variables:
$$DSC = \frac{2x}{y + z}$$

**Step 5:** From Step 3, solve for $(y+z)$:
$$J(y + z - x) = x \implies J(y+z) = x + Jx = x(1+J)$$
$$\implies (y + z) = \frac{x(1+J)}{J}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into $DSC$:
$$DSC = \frac{2x}{\frac{x(1+J)}{J}} = \frac{2x \cdot J}{x(1+J)} = \frac{2J}{1+J}$$

**Step 7:** The inverse relationship can also be derived:
Starting from $DSC = \frac{2J}{1+J}$,
$$DSC(1+J) = 2J \implies DSC + DSC \cdot J = 2J \implies DSC = 2J - DSC \cdot J = J(2 - DSC)$$
$$\implies J = \frac{DSC}{2 - DSC}$$

This algebraic relationship is highly testable.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Pratt's Figure of Merit Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style 14-mark format):**
A reference boundary consists of **N = 6** detected pixels. The perpendicular distances from these detected pixels to the nearest ideal boundary pixels are: $d_1=1, d_2=2, d_3=3, d_4=1, d_5=2, d_6=1$ pixels. Calculate Pratt's Figure of Merit using the standard scaling constant $\alpha = 1/9$. Comment on the segmentation quality.

**Step 1:** Recall the Pratt's FOM formula:
$$FOM = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot d_i^2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $N = 6$ and $\alpha = 1/9$:
$$FOM = \frac{1}{6} \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{1}{1 + \frac{d_i^2}{9}} = \frac{1}{6} \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{9}{9 + d_i^2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute each individual term:

| Pixel $i$ | $d_i$ | $d_i^2$ | $9 + d_i^2$ | $\frac{9}{9+d_i^2}$ |
|-----------|-------|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 10 | 0.900 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 | 13 | 0.692 |
| 3 | 3 | 9 | 18 | 0.500 |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 10 | 0.900 |
| 5 | 2 | 4 | 13 | 0.692 |
| 6 | 1 | 1 | 10 | 0.900 |

**Step 4:** Sum the values:
$$\sum = 0.900 + 0.692 + 0.500 + 0.900 + 0.692 + 0.900 = 4.584$$

**Step 5:** Compute FOM:
$$FOM = \frac{4.584}{6} = 0.764$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation:
Since $FOM = 0.764$, which is close to 1, this represents a **good quality segmentation** with high boundary accuracy. The maximum possible FOM is 1.0 (when all $d_i = 0$).

### 3.3 Worked Example: Dice and Jaccard from a Confusion Matrix

**Problem Statement:**
For a binary segmentation problem, the following is observed:
- $TP = 80$, $FP = 10$, $FN = 20$, $TN = 890$

Calculate the Dice Coefficient, Jaccard Index, Precision, and Recall. Verify the relationship $DSC = \frac{2J}{1+J}$.

**Step 1: Compute Precision:**
$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 10} = \frac{80}{90} = 0.8889$$

**Step 2: Compute Recall:**
$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 3: Compute Dice Coefficient:**
$$DSC = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2(80)}{2(80) + 10 + 20} = \frac{160}{190} = 0.8421$$

**Step 4: Compute Jaccard Index:**
$$J = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 10 + 20} = \frac{80}{110} = 0.7273$$

**Step 5: Verify the Relationship:**

$$\frac{2J}{1+J} = \frac{2(0.7273)}{1 + 0.7273} = \frac{1.4546}{1.7273} = 0.8421$$

This exactly matches our computed $DSC = 0.8421$. **The relationship is verified.**

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection Guide for Evaluation Metrics

| Scenario | Best Metric | Reason |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| Medical tumor segmentation | Dice Coefficient (DSC) | Penalizes both false positives and false negatives equally; common in literature |
| Autonomous driving (lane detection) | Jaccard Index (IoU) | Standard benchmark in Pascal VOC, MS-COCO datasets |
| Edge detection on noisy images | Pratt's Figure of Merit | Directly penalizes boundary displacement |
| Shape matching / contour alignment | Hausdorff Distance | Captures worst-case boundary deviation |
| No ground truth available | Intra-region uniformity + Inter-region contrast | Combined goodness assessment |
| Algorithm benchmarking (speed) | Analytical (Time complexity) | Purely theoretical; no output analysis needed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Complete Taxonomy of Segmentation Evaluation

```mermaid

    A["SEGMENTATION EVALUATION"] --> B["ANALYTICAL METHODS"]
    A --> C["EMPIRICAL METHODS"]

    C --> D["UNSUPERVISED METHODS"]

    E --> F["Region Based Measures"]
    E --> G["Boundary Based Measures"]
    E --> H["Combined Measures"]

    F --> F1["Dice Coefficient"]
    F --> F2["Jaccard Index IoU"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Pratt's Figure of Merit Workflow

```mermaid

    P6 --> P7["Apply Pratt Formula 1 over 1 plus alpha d_i squared"]

```

```mermaid
    CM1 --> M2["Recall = TP over TP plus FN"]
    CM1 --> M3["Dice = 2TP over 2TP plus FP plus FN"]
    CM1 --> M4["Jaccard = TP over TP plus FP plus FN"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define segmentation evaluation. Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised evaluation methods.*

**Model Answer:**
Segmentation evaluation is the process of assessing the quality of an image segmentation algorithm's output, either by comparing it with a known ground truth or by analyzing its internal statistical properties.

**Difference:**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|--------|------------|--------------|
| Ground truth | Required | Not required |
| Metrics | Dice, Jaccard, Pratt's FOM, Hausdorff | Intra-region uniformity, inter-region contrast |
| Application | Medical imaging, benchmarking | Real-time systems, exploratory analysis |
| Accuracy | High (objective) | Approximate (subjective) |

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for clear distinction)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three boundary-based segmentation evaluation metrics and state the formula for Pratt's Figure of Merit.*

**Model Answer:**
Three boundary-based metrics:
1. Pratt's Figure of Merit (FOM)
2. Hausdorff Distance
3. Mean Boundary Distance

Pratt's FOM formula:
$$FOM = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{1 + \alpha d_i^2}$$

where $N$ = number of detected boundary pixels, $d_i$ = perpendicular distance to nearest ideal boundary pixel, $\alpha$ = scaling constant (typically $1/9$).

(3 marks: 1 for listing + 2 for correct formula with definitions)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) – From KTU Model Paper 2024**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **supervised methods of segmentation evaluation**. Discuss region-based and boundary-based measures with suitable formulas. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a binary segmentation, the following values are obtained: $TP = 120$, $FP = 30$, $FN = 50$, $TN = 800$. Calculate the **Dice Coefficient**, **Jaccard Index**, **Precision**, and **Recall**. Verify the relationship $DSC = \dfrac{2J}{1+J}$. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) – Supervised Methods (7 marks)

Supervised evaluation methods rely on a **ground truth** reference image, typically hand-segmented by an expert. The output of the segmentation algorithm is compared pixel-by-pixel (region-based) or boundary-by-boundary (contour-based) against this reference.

**1. Region-Based Measures:**
These compare the overlap between segmented regions and ground truth regions.
- **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC):** $DSC = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$
- **Jaccard Index (IoU):** $J = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$
- **Precision:** $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$
- **Recall:** $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$

**2. Boundary-Based Measures:**
These compare extracted edges/boundaries.
- **Pratt's Figure of Merit (FOM):** Measures average displacement between detected and ideal boundary pixels.
- **Hausdorff Distance:** Measures the maximum of minimum distances between two boundary sets — captures worst-case error.
- **Mean Boundary Distance:** Average Euclidean distance between corresponding boundary points.

**3. Combined Measures:**
Hybrid approaches that weigh both region overlap and boundary accuracy. Example: Tanimoto measure.

**Advantages:** Objective, repeatable, quantifiable.
**Disadvantages:** Ground truth generation is expensive and may itself contain inter-observer variability.

#### Part (b) – Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Precision**
$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{120}{120 + 30} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80$$

**Step 2: Recall**
$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{120}{120 + 50} = \frac{120}{170} = 0.7059$$

**Step 3: Dice Coefficient**
$$DSC = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2(120)}{2(120) + 30 + 50} = \frac{240}{320} = 0.75$$

**Step 4: Jaccard Index**
$$J = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{120}{120 + 30 + 50} = \frac{120}{200} = 0.60$$

**Step 5: Verify Relationship**
$$\frac{2J}{1+J} = \frac{2(0.60)}{1 + 0.60} = \frac{1.20}{1.60} = 0.75$$

This matches the computed $DSC = 0.75$. **Relationship verified.** ✓

---

**Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What are the **unsupervised methods of segmentation evaluation**? Explain intra-region uniformity and inter-region contrast with formulas. (7 marks)

**(b)** The detected boundary of a segmented image consists of 5 points with perpendicular distances to the ground truth boundary as $d = \{1, 2, 0, 3, 1\}$ pixels. Using Pratt's Figure of Merit with $\alpha = 1/9$, calculate the FOM and comment on the segmentation quality. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) – Unsupervised Methods (7 marks)

Unsupervised (goodness) methods evaluate segmentation **without** a ground truth. They rely on intrinsic image properties.

**1. Intra-Region Uniformity ($U$):**
Measures how homogeneous pixel intensities are *within* each region. A high-quality segmentation should have regions where pixel values are very similar.

$$U = 1 - \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{R} \frac{\sigma_i^2}{N_i}$$

where $\sigma_i^2$ = variance of region $i$, $N_i$ = pixel count of region $i$, $C$ = normalization constant. Value $U \to 1$ indicates excellent uniformity.

**2. Inter-Region Contrast ($C$):**
Measures the contrast *between* adjacent regions across their shared boundary. Higher contrast implies better separation.

$$C = \frac{1}{\vert B \vert} \sum_{(p,q) \in B} \frac{\vert f(p) - f(q) \vert}{f(p) + f(q)}$$

**3. Region Shape Measures:**
Compactness, smoothness, boundary regularity.

**Advantages:** No ground truth needed, suitable for online/real-time systems.
**Disadvantages:** Less reliable; metric may not align with perceptual quality.

#### Part (b) – Pratt's FOM Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 5$, $d = \{1, 2, 0, 3, 1\}$, $\alpha = 1/9$.

**Formula:** $FOM = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{1 + \alpha d_i^2} = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} \frac{9}{9 + d_i^2}$

**Individual term computation:**

| $i$ | $d_i$ | $d_i^2$ | $9 + d_i^2$ | $\frac{9}{9+d_i^2}$ |
|-----|-------|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 10 | 0.9000 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 | 13 | 0.6923 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 9 | 1.0000 |
| 4 | 3 | 9 | 18 | 0.5000 |
| 5 | 1 | 1 | 10 | 0.9000 |

**Sum:** $0.9000 + 0.6923 + 1.0000 + 0.5000 + 0.9000 = 3.9923$

**FOM:** $\frac{3.9923}{5} = 0.7985$

**Comment:** FOM $\approx 0.80$ indicates a **good quality segmentation**, with detected boundaries closely matching the ground truth. An FOM of 1.0 would indicate a perfect match.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the multiplier in Dice:** Many students incorrectly write $DSC = \frac{TP}{TP+FP+FN}$ (this is Jaccard, not Dice). Remember the **factor of 2** in the numerator of Dice.
> 2. **Confusing TP/FP/TN/FN:** These are computed on the *pixel level*, not on object level. A pixel correctly classified as foreground is a TP.
> 3. **Pratt's FOM miscalculation:** Students often omit the factor $\alpha$ or set it incorrectly. The default $\alpha = 1/9$ is a standard KTU convention; do not assume $\alpha = 1$.
> 4. **Range of FOM:** FOM is in $(0, 1]$, **not** $[0, 1]$. It can never be exactly 0 unless segmentation is infinitely bad.
> 5. **Dice vs. Jaccard units:** Both are unitless ratios. Do not write "pixels" or "mm" as units.
> 6. **Hausdorff Distance direction:** Always remember Hausdorff is *symmetric* — you must take the max of both directional min-distances.
> 7. **Inter-region contrast formula:** The denominator should be $f(p) + f(q)$ (not $2 \cdot f(p)$ or similar variants). This is a common trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

- **Segmentation Evaluation** = assessing the quality/accuracy of a segmentation output.
- **Two main paradigms:** *Supervised* (ground truth required) and *Unsupervised* (no ground truth).
- **Analytical methods** evaluate the algorithm itself (speed, memory); **Empirical methods** evaluate the output image.
- **Dice Coefficient (DSC):** $\frac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$ — used in medical imaging.
- **Jaccard Index (IoU):** $\frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ — used in object detection benchmarks.
- **Key Relationship:** $DSC = \frac{2J}{1+J}$ and $J = \frac{DSC}{2-DSC}$.
- **Precision:** $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ ; **Recall:** $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
- **Pratt's Figure of Merit:** $\frac{1}{N} \sum \frac{1}{1+\alpha d_i^2}$, with $\alpha = 1/9$ as standard. Range $(0, 1]$.
- **Hausdorff Distance:** Worst-case (max) of minimum distances between two boundaries. Lower is better.
- **Intra-Region Uniformity:** Variance-based; higher is better (max = 1).
- **Inter-Region Contrast:** Boundary pixel difference; higher is better.
- **FOM = 1** → perfect boundary match ; **FOM close to 0** → poor segmentation.
- **Dice ≥ 0.7** is generally accepted as "good" in medical imaging.
- **KTU 2024 favorite questions:** numerical problems on Dice/Jaccard calculation, Pratt's FOM computation, and the relationship $DSC = \frac{2J}{1+J}$.
- **Pitfall:** Do not confuse *TP* (correctly classified foreground pixels) with *TN*.
- **Engineering Applications:** Medical AI (Dice, Hausdorff), Autonomous vehicles (IoU), PCB inspection (Pratt's FOM), Satellite imaging (Region-based metrics).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Transforms - 2D Fourier Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT525 - FUNDAMENTALS OF DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Image Transforms - 2D Fourier Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **2D Discrete Fourier Transform (2D-DFT)** is a mathematical transformation that decomposes a digital image from its native **spatial domain** representation $f(x,y)$ into its constituent **frequency domain** representation $F(u,v)$. It expresses the image as a weighted sum of complex exponential basis functions, revealing the rate of intensity change across the image — a foundational tool for filtering, analysis, compression, and enhancement.

For a digital image of size $M \times N$, the forward and inverse 2D-DFT pairs are defined as:

**Forward 2D-DFT:**
$$F(u,v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi\left(\frac{ux}{M} + \frac{vy}{N}\right)}$$

**Inverse 2D-DFT:**
$$f(x,y) = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{u=0}^{M-1} \sum_{v=0}^{N-1} F(u,v) \, e^{+j2\pi\left(\frac{ux}{M} + \frac{vy}{N}\right)}$$

where $u = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$ and $v = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2D-DFT is the cornerstone of frequency domain image processing. It is **separable** and **symmetric**, meaning it can be computed as a sequence of 1D transforms along rows and then columns — a property exploited heavily in the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithm.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Music of an Image"

Imagine looking at a photograph through a special prism — but instead of splitting light into colors, this prism splits **intensity patterns** into **spatial frequencies**.

- **Low frequencies** = slowly varying intensities → flat skies, smooth walls, broad gradients.
- **High frequencies** = rapidly varying intensities → edges, textures, noise, fine details.

Just as an audio equalizer shows you the bass and treble components of a song, the **Fourier transform shows you the "bass and treble" components of an image**. A blurry photograph has its energy concentrated in low frequencies; a sharp, detailed photograph has significant energy in high frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** JPEG compression (via DCT, a close cousin of DFT), MRI reconstruction, astronomical image deconvolution, and edge-detection sharpening filters all fundamentally rely on understanding frequency-domain image content.

## 1.3 The Complex Spectrum

The output $F(u,v)$ is generally a **complex number**, decomposed as:

$$F(u,v) = R(u,v) + j \, I(u,v)$$

From this, three physically meaningful quantities are derived:

| Quantity | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnitude (Spectrum)** | $\vert F(u,v) \vert = \sqrt{R^2(u,v) + I^2(u,v)}$ | Strength of each frequency component |
| **Phase Angle** | $\phi(u,v) = \arctan\!\left[\dfrac{I(u,v)}{R(u,v)}\right]$ | Spatial alignment/positioning of frequencies |
| **Power Spectrum** | $P(u,v) = \vert F(u,v) \vert^2$ | Energy distribution across frequencies |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** The phase spectrum is often ignored by beginners, but it is actually **more important than the magnitude** in preserving recognizable image structure. Swapping the phase between two images produces a hybrid image that looks like the second image!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why and How the 2D-DFT Works

The 2D-DFT is built on a foundational principle: **any well-behaved 2D signal (image) can be expressed as a weighted superposition of complex sinusoidal "basis patterns."**

**Step-by-step conceptual logic:**

1. **Decomposition into Basis Functions:** Each complex exponential $e^{-j2\pi(ux/M + vy/N)}$ is a 2D sinusoidal pattern whose spatial frequency increases with $u$ and $v$.
2. **Projection via Inner Product:** $F(u,v)$ is essentially the **inner product** (correlation) of the image $f(x,y)$ with each basis pattern — measuring how strongly that frequency is "present" in the image.
3. **Reconstruction:** The inverse transform sums these weighted basis patterns back together, perfectly recovering $f(x,y)$.

## 2.2 Critical Property — Separability

The 2D-DFT can be computed as **two sequential 1D-DFTs**:

$$F(u,v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \left[ \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi vy/N} \right] e^{-j2\pi ux/M}$$

- First, compute a 1D-DFT across each **row**.
- Then, compute a 1D-DFT across each **column** of the intermediate result.

This reduces computational complexity from $O(M^2 N^2)$ to $O(MN \log_2 MN)$ via the FFT algorithm.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Formula | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward 2D-DFT | $F(u,v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y)\, e^{-j2\pi(ux/M + vy/N)}$ | Spatial → Frequency conversion |
| 2 | Inverse 2D-DFT | $f(x,y) = \frac{1}{MN}\sum_{u=0}^{M-1}\sum_{v=0}^{N-1} F(u,v)\, e^{+j2\pi(ux/M + vy/N)}$ | Frequency → Spatial reconstruction |
| 3 | Magnitude Spectrum | $\vert F(u,v) \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + I^2}$ | Frequency strength |
| 4 | Phase Spectrum | $\phi(u,v) = \arctan(I/R)$ | Structural geometry preservation |
| 5 | Power Spectrum | $P(u,v) = \vert F(u,v) \vert^2$ | Energy distribution |
| 6 | Separability | $F(u,v) = \text{DFT}_{\text{col}}\left[\text{DFT}_{\text{row}}[f(x,y)]\right]$ | Enables FFT, reduces complexity |
| 7 | Periodicity | $F(u,v) = F(u+M, v) = F(u, v+N)$ | Spectrum is inherently periodic |
| 8 | Conjugate Symmetry | $F(u,v) = F^{*}(-u, -v)$ | Real images have symmetric spectra |
| 9 | Translation | $f(x,y)\, e^{j2\pi(u_0 x/M + v_0 y/N)} \leftrightarrow F(u-u_0, v-v_0)$ | Shifts spectrum origin |
| 10 | Rotation | $f(r, \theta) \leftrightarrow F(\omega, \phi)$ with $\phi = \theta$ | Rotates spectrum equivalently |
| 11 | Convolution Theorem | $f(x,y) \ast g(x,y) \leftrightarrow F(u,v) \cdot G(u,v)$ | Spatial conv. = freq. multiplication |
| 12 | Correlation Theorem | $f(x,y) \circ g(x,y) \leftrightarrow F^{*}(u,v) \cdot G(u,v)$ | Template matching |
| 13 | Average Value | $F(0,0) = \sum\sum f(x,y)$ | DC component = total image energy |
| 14 | Parseval's Theorem | $\sum\sum \vert f(x,y) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{MN}\sum\sum \vert F(u,v) \vert^2$ | Energy conservation |
| 15 | Distance from Origin | $D(u,v) = \sqrt{(u - M/2)^2 + (v - N/2)^2}$ | Defines low/high freq. regions |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 2D-DFT powers a remarkable array of applications:

- **Medical Imaging:** MRI and CT scan reconstruction from frequency-domain samples (k-space).
- **Astronomy:** Deconvolution of atmospheric blur from telescope images.
- **Compression:** JPEG uses DCT (mathematically similar to DFT) to discard perceptually unimportant high-frequency coefficients.
- **Forensics:** Detecting tampering via inconsistencies in periodic noise patterns.
- **Biometrics:** Fingerprint enhancement and texture-based recognition.
- **Manufacturing:** Automated defect detection on printed circuit boards via frequency signatures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state the **separability property** when asked about computational aspects. Examiners award bonus marks for recognizing that the FFT exploits this property.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous to Discrete 2D Fourier Transform

**Starting Point — Continuous 2D Fourier Transform:**

For a continuous 2D function $f(x,y)$, the Fourier transform is:

$$F(u,v) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi(ux + vy)} \, dx \, dy$$

**Discretization — Sampling the Domain:**

In digital image processing, we sample the continuous function at integer coordinates $x = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$ and $y = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$. The integrals become summations, and the frequency variables become discrete:

$$\begin{aligned}
F(u,v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi\left(\frac{ux}{M} + \frac{vy}{N}\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Justification of the Sampling Interval:**

- If the spatial sampling interval is $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$, then frequency samples occur at $\Delta u = \frac{1}{M\Delta x}$ and $\Delta v = \frac{1}{N\Delta y}$.
- The transform inherently assumes the image is **periodic with period $M$ in $x$ and $N$ in $y$**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Proof of Separability

**Goal:** Show that $F(u,v) = \text{DFT}_{\text{col}}\left[\text{DFT}_{\text{row}}[f(x,y)]\right]$.

**Step 1:** Begin with the full 2D-DFT:

$$\begin{aligned}
F(u,v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi\left(\frac{ux}{M} + \frac{vy}{N}\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Distribute the exponent into the product (using $e^{a+b} = e^a \cdot e^b$):

$$\begin{aligned}
F(u,v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi ux/M} \cdot e^{-j2\pi vy/N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Group terms by isolating the inner summation over $y$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F(u,v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} e^{-j2\pi ux/M} \left[ \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi vy/N} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the inner bracketed term as the 1D-DFT of each **row** $x$:

$$\begin{aligned}
G(x,v) &= \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi vy/N} \quad \text{(1D-DFT along rows)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back and recognize the outer sum as 1D-DFT along columns:

$$\begin{aligned}
F(u,v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} G(x,v) \, e^{-j2\pi ux/M} \quad \text{(1D-DFT along columns)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The 2D-DFT equals **row-wise 1D-DFT** followed by **column-wise 1D-DFT**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: Proof of Conjugate Symmetry for Real Images

**Given:** $f(x,y)$ is real-valued.
**Prove:** $F(u,v) = F^{*}(-u, -v)$.

**Step 1:** Compute $F(-u, -v)$ by substitution:

$$\begin{aligned}
F(-u,-v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi\left(\frac{-ux}{M} + \frac{-vy}{N}\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the exponent sign:

$$\begin{aligned}
F(-u,-v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{+j2\pi\left(\frac{ux}{M} + \frac{vy}{N}\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Take the complex conjugate of the original $F(u,v)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F^{*}(u,v) &= \left[\sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j2\pi\left(\frac{ux}{M} + \frac{vy}{N}\right)}\right]^{*}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the conjugation property $(a \cdot b)^{*} = a^{*} \cdot b^{*}$ and $(e^{j\theta})^{*} = e^{-j\theta}$. Since $f(x,y)$ is real, $f^{*}(x,y) = f(x,y)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F^{*}(u,v) &= \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} f(x,y) \, e^{+j2\pi\left(\frac{ux}{M} + \frac{vy}{N}\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** $F(-u,-v) = F^{*}(u,v)$, hence $F(u,v) = F^{*}(-u,-v)$. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Consequence:** For real images, only **half the spectrum** is unique. This halves memory storage in optimized implementations and explains why we often "shift" the spectrum so that low frequencies are centered — placing them at $(M/2, N/2)$ rather than the corner $(0,0)$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: 2x2 DFT

Consider a 2x2 image:

$$\begin{aligned}
f(x,y) &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $F(0,0)$ — DC Component:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F(0,0) &= \sum_{x=0}^{1}\sum_{y=0}^{1} f(x,y) \, e^{0} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $F(0,1)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F(0,1) &= \sum_{x=0}^{1}\sum_{y=0}^{1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j\pi y} = 1(-j^0) + 2(-j^1) + 3(-j^0) + 4(-j^1)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F(0,1) &= (1 + 3) + (-j)(2 + 4) = 4 - 6j
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $F(1,0)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F(1,0) &= \sum_{x=0}^{1}\sum_{y=0}^{1} f(x,y) \, e^{-j\pi x} = 1 + 2 - 3 - 4 = -4
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $F(1,1)$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F(1,1) &= 1 \cdot e^{0} + 2 \cdot e^{-j\pi} + 3 \cdot e^{-j\pi} + 4 \cdot e^{-j2\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F(1,1) &= 1 - 2 - 3 + 4 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Spectrum:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F(u,v) &= \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 4 - 6j \\ -4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verify Conjugate Symmetry:** $F(1,0) = -4$ is real, and $F^{*}(0,1) = 4 + 6j$. Check: $F(1,0) = -4$ vs $F^{*}(-1,-1)$... For a 2x2 image, indices wrap, so $F(-1,-1) = F(1,1) = 0$. This example is too small to fully illustrate symmetry, but the methodology applies.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 2D-DFT Pipeline

This diagram shows how an image is transformed into the frequency domain and back, illustrating the major processing blocks encountered in KTU lab work.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image f of x,y]:::inputNode --> B[Image Padding Optional]
    B --> C[Row-wise 1D DFT using FFT]:::processNode
    C --> D[Intermediate Spectrum G of x,v]
    D --> E[Column-wise 1D DFT using FFT]:::processNode
    E --> F[2D Spectrum F of u,v]:::outputNode
    F --> G[Center Shift fftshift]
    G --> H[Compute Magnitude log 1 plus F]
    H --> I[Display Frequency Image]
    I --> J[Apply Filter H of u,v]
    J --> K[Filtered Spectrum]
    K --> L[Inverse Shift ifftshift]
    L --> M[Inverse 2D DFT using IFFT]:::processNode
    M --> N[Output Image g of x,y]:::outputNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mapping: Spatial Domain ↔ Frequency Domain

This diagram maps how image features correspond to specific regions of the 2D frequency spectrum.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPATIAL_DOMAIN
        A1[Constant Region] -->|Low Frequency| F1[Center of Spectrum]
        A2[Smooth Gradient] -->|Low Frequency| F1
        A3[Sharp Edge] -->|Mid Frequency| F2[Middle Ring of Spectrum]
        A4[Fine Texture] -->|High Frequency| F3[Outer Ring of Spectrum]
        A5[Random Noise] -->|Very High Frequency| F3
    end

    subgraph FREQUENCY_DOMAIN
        F1 -->|DC Component| G1[F of 0,0 holds Sum of Pixels]
        F2 -->|Energy Concentrated| G2[Mid frequencies encode Edges]
        F3 -->|Energy Spread| G3[High frequencies encode Detail and Noise]
    end

    classDef spatialStyle fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef freqStyle fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 spatialStyle
    class F1,F2,F3 freqStyle
    class G1,G2,G3 freqStyle
```

A decision-making flowchart that KTU students can use to identify which DFT property applies when solving exam problems.

```mermaid
    Q5 -->|Yes| P5[Use DC Component F of 0,0]

    P2 --> End
    P3 --> End
    P4 --> End
    P5 --> End
```
