```
IF (object is round AND has 4 wheels)
THEN (object is a vehicle)
```
- **Advantage:** Modular, easy to add/remove.
- **Limitation:** Rule explosion in complex domains.

**5. Ontologies:**
Formal, explicit specifications of shared conceptualizations, often expressed in OWL/RDF.
*Example:* WordNet hierarchy used for image annotation.
- **Advantage:** Standardized, interoperable, web-scalable.
- **Limitation:** Construction is labor-intensive.

#### Part (b): Role of Context in Object Recognition (7 Marks)

**Context** refers to the surrounding information that aids in disambiguating objects. It is critical because low-level features alone are often insufficient for robust recognition (e.g., a "blue blob" could be sky, water, or a painted wall).

**Types of Context:**

1. **Spatial Context:** Objects have typical spatial relations. *Example: A "computer mouse" is usually on a desk, near a keyboard.*
2. **Scale Context:** Objects appear at expected sizes. *Example: A pedestrian is roughly 1.5–2 m tall.*
3. **Semantic Context:** Objects co-occur functionally. *Example: A "fork" is almost always near a "plate".*
4. **Photometric Context:** Illumination and color distributions. *Example: Sky is usually blue (outdoor daytime).*

**Mathematical Formulation (Bayesian Context Fusion):**

$$
P(C_i \mid \mathbf{x}, \text{context}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_i) \cdot P(C_i \mid \text{context})}{P(\mathbf{x} \mid \text{context})}
$$

The prior $P(C_i \mid \text{context})$ is updated using contextual cues, which in turn refines the posterior probability of the class.

> **Conclusion:** Context transforms object recognition from an *isolated pixel-level classification* into a *holistic scene understanding* task, dramatically improving accuracy in real-world applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Object Recognition** = labeling objects in an image; it is the *high-level* stage of computer vision, sitting atop *image processing* and *image analysis*.
- **Three levels of knowledge:** Procedural (how), Declarative (what — split into *structural* and *semantic*), Heuristic (rules of thumb).
- **Five main KR schemes:** Logical, Semantic Networks, Frames, Production Rules, Ontologies.
- **Frame structure** = slots (attributes) + fillers (values); supports inheritance.
- **Production rules** = IF-THEN statements; ideal for heuristic expert systems.
- **Context types:** Spatial, Scale, Semantic, Photometric — each reduces recognition ambiguity.
- **Bayes' Theorem** is the canonical probabilistic framework: $P(C_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_i) P(C_i)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$.
- **Minimum distance classifier:** $D_i(\mathbf{x}) = \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{m}_i \Vert$; choose class with smallest distance.
- **Decision function (simplified):** $d_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{m}_i^T \mathbf{x} - \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{m}_i^T \mathbf{m}_i$.
- **Normalized Cross-Correlation** is robust to linear illumination changes; output $\gamma \in [-1, +1]$.
- **Real-world applications:** Autonomous driving, medical imaging, biometrics, industrial inspection, remote sensing.
- **Common exam trap:** Confusing *segmentation* (groups pixels by similarity) with *recognition* (assigns semantic labels).
- **Drawing tip:** Always include a labelled block diagram in 14-mark answers — it is worth 2–4 marks by itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE, questions on this module frequently test the **distinction between data-driven processing and knowledge-driven reasoning**. Frame your answers to highlight this duality — it scores extra conceptual marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Pattern Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT525 - FUNDAMENTALS OF DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Statistical Pattern Recognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Statistical Pattern Recognition** is a branch of pattern recognition theory that uses probabilistic and statistical models to classify objects (such as image regions, pixels, or features extracted from images) into predefined categories. It relies on the statistical properties of features — typically their probability density functions, mean vectors, and covariance matrices — to make optimal classification decisions.

In the context of digital image processing, statistical pattern recognition treats each image region (or its extracted feature vector) as a random observation drawn from an underlying probability distribution. The goal is to assign a **class label** $\omega_i$ to an unknown observation $\mathbf{x}$ using a decision rule derived from statistical inference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Statistical Pattern Recognition involves classification of image patterns based on quantifiable statistical features (mean, variance, covariance) and the application of decision-theoretic frameworks such as **Bayes Decision Theory**, **Minimum Distance Classifiers**, and **Nearest Neighbor Rules**.

### 1.2 Core Terminology
| Term | Notation | Meaning |
|------|----------|---------|
| Pattern | $\mathbf{x}$ | A feature vector (e.g., pixel intensities, texture descriptors) |
| Class / Category | $\omega_i$ | One of $C$ predefined classes (e.g., tumor / non-tumor) |
| Prior Probability | $P(\omega_i)$ | Probability that a pattern belongs to class $i$ before observation |
| Class-Conditional Density | $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$ | Probability density of $\mathbf{x}$ given it belongs to class $i$ |
| Posterior Probability | $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Updated probability of class membership after observing $\mathbf{x}$ |
| Discriminant Function | $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ | Function used to decide which class $\mathbf{x}$ belongs to |
| Decision Boundary | — | Locus where $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition
Imagine a doctor who must decide whether a tissue sample is **benign** or **malignant** based on measurable quantities (cell size, nucleus radius, texture uniformity). Without absolute certainty, the doctor uses a *statistical model*: "If the cell is large AND the texture is irregular, the probability of malignancy is high."

This is precisely what **statistical pattern recognition** does — but mathematically. It replaces intuition with **probability rules**.

- The doctor's training data (hundreds of past cases) is the **training set**.
- The measurable quantities are the **features** (the pattern vector $\mathbf{x}$).
- The decision "benign vs malignant" is the **classification rule**.
- The probability the doctor assigns to "malignant" is the **posterior probability** $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Statistical pattern recognition is fundamentally about *decision-making under uncertainty*. Images contain noise, variability, and ambiguity — probability theory gives us the optimal mathematical framework to handle this uncertainty rigorously.

### 1.4 Why Statistical Pattern Recognition Matters in Image Processing
- **Robustness to Noise:** Probabilistic models inherently tolerate noisy pixel data.
- **Optimal Decision Making:** Bayes classifiers are *provably optimal* in minimizing classification error.
- **Multi-class Problems:** Easily extended to dozens of categories (e.g., land-cover classification in remote sensing).
- **Foundation for Modern ML:** All modern deep learning classifiers ultimately rely on statistical learning principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Pattern Recognition Pipeline
A complete statistical pattern recognition system consists of **five sequential stages**:

1. **Data Acquisition:** Capture image samples from the sensor (camera, MRI, satellite).
2. **Pre-processing:** Noise removal, normalization, segmentation of regions of interest.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Convert image regions into compact numerical vectors $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
4. **Training (Learning):** Estimate class statistics $\mu_i$, $\Sigma_i$, and priors $P(\omega_i)$ from labeled samples.
5. **Classification (Decision):** Apply decision rule to assign $\mathbf{x}$ to the most likely class $\omega^*$.

> [!TIP]
> **"Why" Explanation:** Steps 1–3 prepare the data. Step 4 *learns* the statistical model. Step 5 *applies* it. The quality of Step 3 (feature extraction) typically determines the overall accuracy more than the choice of classifier.

### 2.2 Bayes Decision Theory — The Foundation

#### 2.2.1 Bayes Theorem (for Pattern Recognition)
For a pattern $\mathbf{x}$ and class $\omega_i$:

$$P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

where the **evidence** $p(\mathbf{x})$ is computed by the total probability theorem:

$$p(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{C} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)$$

#### 2.2.2 Bayes Decision Rule (Minimum Error)
Assign $\mathbf{x}$ to class $\omega^*$ that maximizes the posterior:

$$\omega^* = \arg\max_{i} \; P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$$

Because $p(\mathbf{x})$ is constant for all classes, this is equivalent to:

$$\omega^* = \arg\max_{i} \; p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i)$$

#### 2.2.3 Discriminant Functions
Define a discriminant function for each class:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

Classification rule: $\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Bayes classifiers are used in medical diagnosis (tumor detection), biometric authentication (fingerprint/face recognition), optical character recognition (OCR), industrial defect detection, and remote sensing land-cover classification.

### 2.3 Gaussian (Normal) Density Model
The most widely used class-conditional density. For $n$-dimensional feature vectors:

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert\Sigma_i\vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^{T} \Sigma_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)\right)$$

Parameters:
- **Mean vector:** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = E[\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i]$
- **Covariance matrix:** $\Sigma_i = E[(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^{T} \mid \omega_i]$
- **Determinant:** $\vert\Sigma_i\vert$ — a scalar measure of spread

### 2.4 Minimum Distance Classifier
A special case of the Bayes classifier when:
- All classes are **equally likely** ($P(\omega_i) = P(\omega_j)$)
- All classes have **identical isotropic covariance** $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$

The decision rule reduces to: **assign $\mathbf{x}$ to the class with the nearest mean.**

$$d_i(\mathbf{x}) = \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i \vert\vert = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^{T}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)}$$

**Mahalanobis Distance** (generalization when $\Sigma_i$ is class-specific but not isotropic):

$$d_M(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^{T} \Sigma_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)}$$

### 2.5 k-Nearest Neighbor (k-NN) Classifier
A **non-parametric** method that classifies $\mathbf{x}$ by majority vote among its $k$ closest training samples.

Algorithm:
1. Compute distance $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_m)$ from $\mathbf{x}$ to **every** training point $\mathbf{x}_m$.
2. Identify the $k$ smallest distances — these are the $k$ nearest neighbors.
3. Count how many neighbors belong to each class $\omega_i$.
4. Assign $\mathbf{x}$ to the class with the **maximum count** $k_i$.

$$\omega^* = \arg\max_{i} \; k_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Cases:** $k = 1$ → **Nearest Neighbor (NN)** rule. $k = \sqrt{N}$ (where $N$ is training set size) is a common heuristic.

### 2.6 Performance Evaluation Metrics
After classification, the **confusion matrix** $C$ of size $C \times C$ is built:

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|--------|---------|---------|
| Accuracy | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Fraction of all correct predictions |
| Precision | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of predicted positives, how many are correct |
| Recall (Sensitivity) | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual positives, how many were detected |
| Specificity | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Of actual negatives, how many were correctly rejected |
| F1-Score | $2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |

where $TP, TN, FP, FN$ denote True Positive, True Negative, False Positive, False Negative respectively.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Key Notes |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Bayes Theorem | $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$ | Foundation of statistical classification |
| 2 | Total Probability | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{C} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ | Denominator normalization |
| 3 | Bayes Decision Rule | $\omega^* = \arg\max_i P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$ | Minimizes probability of error |
| 4 | Discriminant Function | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | Equivalent to Bayes rule |
| 5 | Gaussian Density | $p(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} \exp(-\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mu))$ | Most common assumption |
| 6 | Euclidean Distance | $d(\mathbf{x},\mu) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mu)^{T}(\mathbf{x}-\mu)}$ | For equal-covariance classes |
| 7 | Mahalanobis Distance | $d_M = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x}-\mu)^{T}\Sigma^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\mu)}$ | For unequal covariance |
| 8 | k-NN Rule | $\omega^* = \arg\max_i k_i$ | $k_i$ = neighbors in class $i$ |
| 9 | Accuracy | $(TP+TN)/(TP+TN+FP+FN)$ | Overall performance |
| 10 | F1-Score | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Balance between Precision & Recall |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Do NOT use Euclidean distance when classes have different covariances — it produces biased boundaries. Use Mahalanobis distance instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Bayes Decision Rule Minimizes Probability of Error

**Step 1 — Define the probability of error.**
For a two-class problem ($\omega_1, \omega_2$), the probability of misclassification is:

$$P(\text{error}) = P(\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{R}_2, \omega_1) + P(\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{R}_1, \omega_2)$$

where $\mathcal{R}_i$ is the decision region assigned to class $\omega_i$.

**Step 2 — Express as integrals over the decision regions.**

$$P(\text{error}) = \int_{\mathcal{R}_2} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1) \, d\mathbf{x} + \int_{\mathcal{R}_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2) \, d\mathbf{x}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a single integral over the entire feature space.**

$$P(\text{error}) = \int \min\!\big[ P(\omega_1) p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1),\; P(\omega_2) p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2) \big] \, d\mathbf{x}$$

> **Logic:** To minimize $P(\text{error})$, the integrand at every $\mathbf{x}$ must be minimized. Since we cannot change the densities or priors, we must assign $\mathbf{x}$ to whichever class has the *smaller* value of the integrand in the *other* region's integral — equivalently, assign to the class with the **larger** posterior.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (Bayes Decision Rule):**

$$\boxed{\text{Decide } \omega_1 \text{ if } P(\omega_1) p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) > P(\omega_2) p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2); \text{ else decide } \omega_2}$$

This is the *minimum-error* Bayes classifier.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Bayes Classifier with Gaussian Densities

**Problem Statement:**
Two classes $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ have 1-D Gaussian distributions:
- $\omega_1$: $\mu_1 = 2$, $\sigma_1^2 = 1$, $P(\omega_1) = 0.6$
- $\omega_2$: $\mu_2 = 5$, $\sigma_2^2 = 4$, $P(\omega_2) = 0.4$

A new pattern has feature value $x = 3$. Classify it.

**Step 1 — Write the Gaussian density formula:**

$$p(x \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma_i^2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu_i)^2}{2\sigma_i^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $p(x \mid \omega_1)$ at $x = 3$:**

$$p(3 \mid \omega_1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 1}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(3-2)^2}{2 \cdot 1}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-0.5}$$

Numerically: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 0.3989$ and $e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$.

$$p(3 \mid \omega_1) \approx 0.3989 \times 0.6065 \approx 0.2419$$

**Step 3 — Compute $p(x \mid \omega_2)$ at $x = 3$:**

$$p(3 \mid \omega_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 4}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(3-5)^2}{2 \cdot 4}\right) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot e^{-0.5}$$

Numerically: $\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 0.1995$.

$$p(3 \mid \omega_2) \approx 0.1995 \times 0.6065 \approx 0.1210$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by priors (the unnormalized posteriors):**

$$P(\omega_1) \, p(3 \mid \omega_1) = 0.6 \times 0.2419 \approx 0.1451$$

$$P(\omega_2) \, p(3 \mid \omega_2) = 0.4 \times 0.1210 \approx 0.0484$$

**Step 5 — Apply Bayes decision rule:**

$$0.1451 \; (\omega_1) > 0.0484 \; (\omega_2)$$

Therefore, $\mathbf{x = 3}$ is classified as **$\omega_1$**.

**Step 6 — (Bonus) Compute normalized posterior probabilities:**

$$p(3) = 0.1451 + 0.0484 = 0.1935$$

$$P(\omega_1 \mid 3) = \frac{0.1451}{0.1935} \approx 0.75$$

$$P(\omega_2 \mid 3) = \frac{0.0484}{0.1935} \approx 0.25$$

The classifier assigns the pattern to $\omega_1$ with **75% confidence** and $\omega_2$ with **25% confidence**.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Minimum Distance Classifier

**Problem Statement:**
Two classes have 2-D feature vectors with mean vectors:
- $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = (1, 2)^{T}$
- $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = (4, 6)^{T}$

An unknown pattern is $\mathbf{x} = (2, 3)^{T}$. Using Euclidean distance, classify it.

**Step 1 — Compute $d_1 = \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 \vert\vert$:**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (3-2)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

**Step 2 — Compute $d_2 = \vert\vert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 \vert\vert$:**

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(2-4)^2 + (3-6)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$$

**Step 3 — Apply minimum distance rule:**

Since $d_1 = 1.414 < d_2 = 3.606$, the pattern is assigned to **class $\omega_1$**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: k-NN Classifier (k = 3)

**Problem Statement:**
Training set contains 6 labeled 2-D points:
- Class A: $(1,1), (2,2), (2,1)$
- Class B: $(5,5), (6,6), (5,6)$

Test point: $\mathbf{x} = (3,3)$. Use $k = 3$ Euclidean nearest neighbors.

**Step 1 — Compute distances from $\mathbf{x}$ to every training point.**

| Point | Class | Distance to (3,3) |
|-------|-------|-------------------|
| (1,1) | A | $\sqrt{4+4} = 2.828$ |
| (2,2) | A | $\sqrt{1+1} = 1.414$ |
| (2,1) | A | $\sqrt{1+4} = 2.236$ |
| (5,5) | B | $\sqrt{4+4} = 2.828$ |
| (6,6) | B | $\sqrt{9+9} = 4.243$ |
| (5,6) | B | $\sqrt{4+9} = 3.606$ |

**Step 2 — Select the 3 smallest distances.**

| Rank | Point | Class | Distance |
|------|-------|-------|----------|
| 1 | (2,2) | A | 1.414 |
| 2 | (2,1) | A | 2.236 |
| 3 | (1,1) | A | 2.828 (tied with (5,5)) |

**Step 3 — Apply majority vote.**
- Class A: **3 votes** (all three nearest neighbors are from A)
- Class B: **0 votes**

**Step 4 — Decision:**
$$\mathbf{x} = (3,3) \text{ is classified as Class A.}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Classifier Selection Guide

| Classifier Type | Parametric? | Assumes Density Form? | Training Cost | Query Cost | Best Use Case |
|----------------|-------------|----------------------|---------------|------------|---------------|
| Bayes (Gaussian) | Yes | Multivariate Gaussian | Low | Very Low | Normally distributed features |
| Min Distance | Yes | Equal spherical covariance | Very Low | Very Low | Quick prototyping |
| k-NN | No | None | None (lazy) | High | Small datasets, complex boundaries |
| Neural Network | Yes (weights) | Implicit | High | Low | Large data, complex patterns |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 The Statistical Pattern Recognition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Image Acquisition] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Segmentation]
    C --> D[Feature Extraction]
    D --> E[Feature Vector X]
    E --> F[Training Phase]
    F --> G[Estimated Class Statistics]
    G --> H[Classifier Model]
    H --> I[Test Pattern X New]
    I --> J[Apply Decision Rule]
    J --> K[Predicted Class Label]
    K --> L[Performance Evaluation]
    L --> M[Confusion Matrix and Metrics]
```

### 4.2 Bayes Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Observed Pattern X] --> Q[Compute Evidence P of X]
    P --> R[Compute Likelihood P of X given W1]
    P --> S[Compute Likelihood P of X given W2]
    P --> T[Compute Likelihood P of X given WC]
    Q --> U[Bayes Posterior for Class W1]
    Q --> V[Bayes Posterior for Class W2]
    Q --> W[Bayes Posterior for Class WC]
    R --> U
    S --> V
    T --> W
    G1[Prior P of W1] --> U
    G2[Prior P of W2] --> V
    G3[Prior P of WC] --> W
    U --> X[Argmax of All Posteriors]
    V --> X
    W --> X
    X --> Y[Final Decision Class Label]
```

### 4.3 k-NN Classification Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[New Pattern X] --> DIST[Distance Computation Module]
    TR[Stored Training Patterns] --> DIST
    DIST --> SORT[Sort Distances Ascending]
    SORT --> TOPK[Select K Smallest Distances]
    TOPK --> VOTE[Majority Voting Module]
    VOTE --> COUNT1[Count Class W1]
    VOTE --> COUNT2[Count Class W2]
    VOTE --> COUNTC[Count Class WC]
    COUNT1 --> ARG[Argmax of Vote Counts]
    COUNT2 --> ARG
    COUNTC --> ARG
    ARG --> OUT[Predicted Class for X]
```

### 4.4 Classifier Family Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SPR[Statistical Pattern Recognition]
    SPR --> P[Parametric Methods]
    SPR --> NP[Non Parametric Methods]
    P --> B[Bayes Classifier]
    P --> MD[Minimum Distance Classifier]
    P --> G[Gaussian Density Models]
    NP --> KNN[k Nearest Neighbor]
    NP --> PNN[Parzen Window Estimator]
    B --> MDE[Minimum Error Decision]
    B --> MDR[Minimum Risk Decision]
    MD --> EUC[Euclidean Distance]
    MD --> MAH[Mahalanobis Distance]
    KNN --> K1[k = 1 Nearest Neighbor]
    KNN --> KGEN[k Greater than 1 Vote]
```

### 4.5 Confusion Matrix Layout (Binary Case)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Actual Positive] --> B[Predicted Positive gives TP]
    A --> C[Predicted Negative gives FN]
    D[Actual Negative] --> E[Predicted Positive gives FP]
    D --> F[Predicted Negative gives TN]
    B --> G[Recall = TP over TP plus FN]
    F --> H[Specificity = TN over TN plus FP]
    B --> I[Precision = TP over TP plus FP]
    B --> J[Accuracy = TP plus TN over Total]
    F --> J
    E --> J
    C --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The flowcharts above represent the *information flow* of each recognition method, not the geometric decision boundaries. For geometric visualization (e.g., Voronoi tessellation in k-NN), students are encouraged to refer to standard textbook figures.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the term "statistical pattern recognition" and list any four features commonly used for image classification.*

**Model Answer:**

Statistical pattern recognition is a framework that classifies patterns (such as image regions) into predefined categories by modeling their statistical properties (mean, variance, distribution).

Four commonly used features:
1. **Intensity-based features** — mean gray level, standard deviation.
2. **Texture features** — Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) descriptors.
3. **Geometric features** — area, perimeter, compactness of regions.
4. **Spectral features** — frequency-domain coefficients (e.g., from FFT/DCT).

*(Any four valid features accepted.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State Bayes Theorem as used in pattern recognition. What is the minimum-error Bayes decision rule?*

**Model Answer:**

$$P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Minimum-Error Bayes Rule:** Classify $\mathbf{x}$ into the class $\omega^*$ that has the *largest* posterior probability:

$$\omega^* = \arg\max_{i} \; P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$$

This rule **minimizes the probability of misclassification**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain Bayes classifier for minimum error rate classification. Derive the Bayes decision rule and show that it minimizes the probability of error. (7 marks)**

**(b) For a 2-class problem in 1-D, the class-conditional densities are Gaussian with $\mu_1 = 0, \sigma_1^2 = 1, P(\omega_1) = 0.5$ and $\mu_2 = 3, \sigma_2^2 = 1, P(\omega_2) = 0.5$. Find the decision boundary. Classify the test point $x = 1.2$. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Bayes classifier assigns a pattern $\mathbf{x}$ to the class $\omega_i$ with the highest posterior probability $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$.

**Derivation of the decision rule:**

The probability of error for a 2-class case is:

$$P(\text{error}) = P(\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{R}_2, \omega_1) + P(\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{R}_1, \omega_2)$$

Converting to integrals:

$$P(\text{error}) = \int_{\mathcal{R}_2} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) P(\omega_1) \, d\mathbf{x} + \int_{\mathcal{R}_1} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2) P(\omega_2) \, d\mathbf{x}$$

For each $\mathbf{x}$, we must place it in $\mathcal{R}_1$ or $\mathcal{R}_2$. To minimize the integrand everywhere, we choose $\omega_1$ if $P(\omega_1) p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) > P(\omega_2) p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2)$, else $\omega_2$.

**Decision Rule:** 
$$\text{Choose } \omega_1 \text{ if } P(\omega_1) p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_1) > P(\omega_2) p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_2); \text{ else } \omega_2.$$

**Conclusion:** This rule guarantees the minimum achievable classification error and is therefore called the *Bayes optimal classifier*.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

Since $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = 1$ and $P(\omega_1) = P(\omega_2) = 0.5$, the Bayes rule reduces to:

$$p(x \mid \omega_1) = p(x \mid \omega_2)$$

Substituting Gaussian densities:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-0)^2}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-3)^2}{2}\right)$$

Taking natural log and equating:

$$-\frac{x^2}{2} = -\frac{(x-3)^2}{2}$$

$$x^2 = (x-3)^2 = x^2 - 6x + 9$$

$$0 = -6x + 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 1.5$$

**Decision boundary:** $x_0 = 1.5$.

**Classification of $x = 1.2$:** Since $1.2 < 1.5$, the pattern is in $\mathcal{R}_1$. The point is classified as **class $\omega_1$**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the k-Nearest Neighbor (k-NN) classifier algorithm. Discuss the effect of varying k on the decision boundary and mention two advantages and two disadvantages of the method. (7 marks)**

**(b) Given a training set with the following 2-D points: Class A — (1, 2), (2, 3), (2, 1); Class B — (5, 6), (6, 5), (5, 5). Apply the k-NN algorithm with k = 3 to classify the test point (3, 3). Show all distance computations. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Compute the distance from the test point $\mathbf{x}$ to every training point using Euclidean (or Mahalanobis) distance.
2. Sort distances in ascending order.
3. Select the $k$ training points with smallest distances — the *k nearest neighbors*.
4. Count the number of neighbors belonging to each class: $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_C$.
5. Assign $\mathbf{x}$ to the class with the largest $k_i$.

**Effect of k on the decision boundary:**
- **Small k (e.g., k = 1):** Highly flexible, jagged boundaries. Sensitive to noise → overfitting.
- **Large k (e.g., k = N):** Very smooth boundaries, approaches always predicting the majority class → underfitting.
- **Moderate k:** Provides a good bias-variance trade-off.

**Advantages:**
1. No training phase — instance-based learning.
2. Naturally handles multi-class problems and complex, non-linear decision boundaries.

**Disadvantages:**
1. High computational cost at query time (must scan all training data).
2. Sensitive to irrelevant features and the choice of distance metric.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distances from test point (3, 3) to each training point:**

For point $(a, b)$: $d = \sqrt{(3-a)^2 + (3-b)^2}$

| Training Point | Class | Distance Calculation | Distance |
|----------------|-------|----------------------|----------|
| (1, 2) | A | $\sqrt{4+1}$ | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$ |
| (2, 3) | A | $\sqrt{1+0}$ | $1.000$ |
| (2, 1) | A | $\sqrt{1+4}$ | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$ |
| (5, 6) | B | $\sqrt{4+9}$ | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$ |
| (6, 5) | B | $\sqrt{9+4}$ | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$ |
| (5, 5) | B | $\sqrt{4+4}$ | $\sqrt{8} \approx 2.828$ |

**Step 2 — Sort and select top 3 nearest neighbors:**

| Rank | Point | Class | Distance |
|------|-------|-------|----------|
| 1 | (2, 3) | A | 1.000 |
| 2 | (1, 2) | A | 2.236 |
| 3 | (2, 1) | A | 2.236 |

**Step 3 — Majority voting:**
- Class A: 3 votes
- Class B: 0 votes

**Step 4 — Final classification:** Test point (3, 3) is classified as **Class A**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the prior probabilities** in Bayes rule — many students compute only $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$ and forget to multiply by $P(\omega_i)$. This is the single most common mistake and costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Using Euclidean distance with unequal covariances** — the minimum distance classifier is only valid when $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$. Otherwise, use Mahalanobis.
> 3. **Not taking $\ln$ in discriminant functions** — when comparing Gaussians, the discriminant simplifies considerably *only after* taking the natural log. Students often forget this.
> 4. **For k-NN, students forget to sort** and simply pick the first $k$ points in the dataset — this is incorrect. Sorting is mandatory.
> 5. **Confusion matrix confusion** — many students mix up the rows and columns. **Convention:** rows = actual class, columns = predicted class.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points:**

- [x] **Statistical Pattern Recognition** classifies patterns using probabilistic models rather than deterministic rules.
- [x] **Bayes Theorem** is the central mathematical tool: $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$.
- [x] The **minimum-error Bayes rule** maximizes the posterior probability → provably optimal in error rate.
- [x] The **discriminant function** form $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$ simplifies Gaussian classification dramatically.
- [x] The **Gaussian density assumption** is widely used because of the Central Limit Theorem and mathematical tractability.
- [x] **Euclidean distance classifier** is a special case of the Bayes classifier (equal priors, equal isotropic covariances).
- [x] **Mahalanobis distance** accounts for class-specific covariance and is preferred for elliptical clusters.
- [x] **k-NN is non-parametric** — it makes no distributional assumption but is computationally expensive at query time.
- [x] **Confusion matrix** is the standard evaluation tool: rows = actual, columns = predicted.
- [x] **Accuracy alone is misleading** for imbalanced datasets — always report **Precision, Recall, and F1-score**.
- [x] KTU frequently tests: Bayes rule derivations, Gaussian density classification, k-NN numerical problems, and metric computations.
- [x] Always state the **assumptions** (equal priors, Gaussian form) before applying any formula.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** If you remember only three things, remember: **(1) Bayes Theorem, (2) Gaussian density formula, (3) k-NN majority vote algorithm.** These three cover ~80% of KTU 2024 exam questions on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT525 - FUNDAMENTALS OF DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Digital Image Processing, **Classification** is a supervised pattern-recognition operation in which each pixel (or object/region) of a multispectral or hyperspectral image is assigned to one of several pre-defined thematic classes (e.g., water, vegetation, soil, built-up area) based on its spectral signature — i.e., the vector of digital numbers (DNs) recorded across multiple spectral bands. The classifier is first *trained* using a set of labelled samples (the **training set** or **ground truth**) so that it can learn the statistical properties of each class, and then used to *predict* the class of every unknown pixel in the image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Classification Principles" in Module 4 of *PEADT525 – Fundamentals of Digital Image Processing* specifically refers to the **theory, mathematical foundation, and decision logic** of pixel-based supervised classifiers such as the **Minimum Distance Classifier, Parallelepiped Classifier, and Maximum Likelihood (Bayesian) Classifier**, together with the concepts of feature space, training samples, and decision boundaries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a fruit-sorting machine in a large warehouse. Before sorting begins, a senior worker shows you a few baskets of clearly labelled fruits — apples, oranges, and grapes (this is the *training phase*). The worker tells you, "Apples are red and round, oranges are orange and round, grapes are small and green/purple." You memorize a *mental model* of weight, colour, and size for each fruit.

Now, a conveyor belt brings thousands of unknown fruits. For each one you instantly measure its colour and size (the *feature vector*) and compare it with your mental model. You place it in the most appropriate basket (the *decision rule*). That is **classification**.

In remote sensing, every pixel is a "fruit", its **spectral values across bands** are the "features", the labelled samples are the "training baskets", and the **classifier** is the rule that decides which basket each new pixel belongs to.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary to remember:**
> - **Feature Vector** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$ — pixel's DN in $n$ bands.
> - **Training Set** — a collection of pixels whose true class is known.
> - **Decision Boundary** — a hypersurface in feature space separating one class from another.
> - **Accuracy / Error Rate** — fraction of pixels correctly / incorrectly classified.

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

Classification is the bridge between *raw imagery* and *thematic maps*. It is used in:
- Land-use / land-cover mapping
- Medical image diagnosis (tumour vs. healthy tissue in MRI)
- Agricultural crop monitoring
- Urban planning and change detection
- Defence and surveillance applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Constant / Convention:** In KTU 2024 scheme notation, the **a-priori probability** of class $\omega_i$ is denoted $P(\omega_i)$, and the **conditional probability density** of feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ given class $\omega_i$ is $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$. The total number of classes is $m$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stages of a Supervised Classification Workflow

A complete classification task, as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus, involves the following sequential steps:

1. **Define the classification scheme** — Decide the thematic classes (e.g., Water, Forest, Urban, Barren).
2. **Select training areas** — Choose representative pixels of each class from the image (or from ground-truth data).
3. **Compute class statistics** — For each class $\omega_i$, estimate the **mean vector** $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ and the **covariance matrix** $\mathbf{\Sigma}_i$.
4. **Evaluate separability** (optional) — Use divergence, Jeffries–Matusita distance, or transformed divergence to check whether the chosen classes are statistically distinguishable.
5. **Apply the decision rule** — Classify every pixel $\mathbf{x}$ by the chosen rule.
6. **Post-classification processing** — Apply majority filter, clump/sieve, and accuracy assessment.

### 2.2 Types of Classifiers (KTU 2024 Focus)

#### 2.2.1 Minimum Distance Classifier
Assigns an unknown pixel $\mathbf{x}$ to the class whose **mean vector is closest** in Euclidean spectral distance.

$$d_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)}$$

**Decision Rule:** $\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i$ if $d_i(\mathbf{x}) < d_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$.

- *Pros:* Simple, fast, no covariance estimation required.
- *Cons:* Ignores class variance and shape of distribution.

#### 2.2.2 Parallelepiped Classifier
Uses a simple **upper and lower bound (decision box)** for each band based on training data.

$$\text{Low}_i^{(k)} \le x_k \le \text{High}_i^{(k)} \quad \text{for every band } k$$

- *Pros:* Computationally the fastest.
- *Cons:* Many overlaps and unclassified pixels; sensitive to noise.

#### 2.2.3 Maximum Likelihood Classifier (Bayesian / Gaussian)
Assumes each class has a **multivariate normal (Gaussian) distribution**. The decision is based on the **discriminant function**:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln P(\omega_i) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)$$

**Decision Rule:** Assign $\mathbf{x}$ to $\omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$.

- *Pros:* Most accurate, accounts for variance and covariance.
- *Cons:* Computationally expensive; needs a good training set.

### 2.3 Bayes' Theorem in Classification

The **posterior probability** $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$ — the probability that pixel $\mathbf{x}$ belongs to class $\omega_i$ — is given by:

$$P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

where $p(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{m} p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$. The **Maximum A-Posteriori (MAP)** rule is identical to the Maximum Likelihood rule when $P(\omega_i)$ is uniform.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Euclidean distance (Min. Distance) | $d_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)}$ | Simplest, ignores $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ |
| 2 | Mean vector | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i = \dfrac{1}{N_i} \sum_{k=1}^{N_i} \mathbf{x}_k$ | $N_i$ = no. of training pixels in $\omega_i$ |
| 3 | Covariance matrix | $\mathbf{\Sigma}_i = \dfrac{1}{N_i - 1} \sum_{k=1}^{N_i} (\mathbf{x}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)(\mathbf{x}_k - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T$ | $n \times n$ symmetric |
| 4 | Multivariate Gaussian PDF | $p(\mathbf{x} \vert \omega_i) = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left[-\tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)\right]$ | Foundation of MLC |
| 5 | Bayes discriminant | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln P(\omega_i) - \tfrac{1}{2}\ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert - \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu}_i)$ | MLC decision function |
| 6 | Posterior (Bayes) | $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$ | MAP assigns to max |
| 7 | Overall accuracy | $\text{OA} = \dfrac{\text{Correctly classified pixels}}{\text{Total pixels}} \times 100\%$ | Reported in percent |
| 8 | Kappa coefficient | $\kappa = \dfrac{N \sum x_{ii} - \sum (x_{i+} x_{+i})}{N^2 - \sum (x_{i+} x_{+i})}$ | $x_{ii}$ diagonal of confusion matrix |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the **determinant** of a matrix — never confuse it with the markdown table cell separator. The notation $\mathbf{x}_{k}$ represents the $k^{th}$ training sample vector.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agriculture:** Crop-type classification for yield estimation.
- **Disaster Management:** Flood / fire-affected area mapping.
- **Medical Imaging:** Tumour segmentation in MRI/CT.
- **Defence:** Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) from satellite imagery.
- **Urban Planning:** Built-up vs. green-space mapping for smart-city design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Likelihood Discriminant Function

We begin with the Bayes posterior probability:

$$P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

For classification, $p(\mathbf{x})$ is the same for every class and can therefore be dropped. Hence the optimal MAP rule becomes:

$$\text{Decide } \omega_i \;\; \text{if} \;\; p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i) > p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j) \;\; \forall \, j \neq i$$

Substituting the multivariate Gaussian PDF for $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$:

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert^{1/2}} \, \exp\!\left[ -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) \right]$$

Taking the natural logarithm of $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)$ — since $\ln(\cdot)$ is monotonically increasing, the arg-max is preserved — we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(\mathbf{x}) &= \ln\!\left[ p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i) \right] \\[4pt]
&= \ln P(\omega_i) - \ln\!\left[ (2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert^{1/2} \right] - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) \\[4pt]
&= \ln P(\omega_i) - \frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

The term $-\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi)$ is class-independent and is dropped. The final, **practical** discriminant function becomes:

$$
\boxed{\, g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln P(\omega_i) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) \,}
$$

This is the equation that students must memorise for the KTU 2024 examination.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Minimum Distance Classifier (KTU Style)

A two-band image has two training classes $\omega_1$ (Water) and $\omega_2$ (Vegetation) with the following mean vectors (in 8-bit DN units):

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 40 \\ 30 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 80 \\ 120 \end{pmatrix}$$

An unknown pixel has feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (60, 70)^T$. Apply the **Minimum Distance Classifier**.

**Step 1 — Compute distance from $\mathbf{x}$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1$:**

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(60-40)^2 + (70-30)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 1600} = \sqrt{2000} \approx 44.72$$

**Step 2 — Compute distance from $\mathbf{x}$ to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_2$:**

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(60-80)^2 + (70-120)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 2500} = \sqrt{2900} \approx 53.85$$

**Step 3 — Compare and decide:**

$$d_1 \approx 44.72 \;<\; d_2 \approx 53.85 \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{x} \in \omega_1 \;\; (\text{Water})$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Maximum Likelihood Classifier

For the same two classes, suppose the a-priori probabilities and covariance matrices are:

$$P(\omega_1) = 0.6, \;\; P(\omega_2) = 0.4, \;\; \mathbf{\Sigma}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 100 & 20 \\ 20 & 80 \end{pmatrix}, \;\; \mathbf{\Sigma}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 90 & 10 \\ 10 & 110 \end{pmatrix}$$

We test the same pixel $\mathbf{x} = (60, 70)^T$ with $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2$ as in §3.2.

**Step 1 — Determinant of $\mathbf{\Sigma}_1$:**

$$\vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_1 \vert = 100 \times 80 - 20 \times 20 = 8000 - 400 = 7600$$

**Step 2 — Inverse of $\mathbf{\Sigma}_1$:**

$$\mathbf{\Sigma}_1^{-1} = \frac{1}{7600}\begin{pmatrix} 80 & -20 \\ -20 & 100 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.01053 & -0.00263 \\ -0.00263 & 0.01316 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Mahalanobis distance squared for $\omega_1$:**

$$(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = \begin{pmatrix} 20 \\ 40 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1^2 &= \begin{pmatrix} 20 & 40 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.01053 & -0.00263 \\ -0.00263 & 0.01316 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 20 \\ 40 \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{pmatrix} 20 & 40 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.1053 \\ 0.4737 \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= 20(0.1053) + 40(0.4737) = 2.106 + 18.947 = 21.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Discriminant for $\omega_1$:**

$$g_1(\mathbf{x}) = \ln(0.6) - \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(7600) - \tfrac{1}{2}(21.05)$$

Numerically:

$$g_1(\mathbf{x}) = -0.5108 - 4.4308 - 10.527 \approx -15.47$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for $\omega_2$:**

$$\vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_2 \vert = 90 \times 110 - 10 \times 10 = 9900 - 100 = 9800$$

$$\mathbf{\Sigma}_2^{-1} = \frac{1}{9800}\begin{pmatrix} 110 & -10 \\ -10 & 90 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.01122 & -0.00102 \\ -0.00102 & 0.00918 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \begin{pmatrix} -20 \\ -50 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2^2 &= \begin{pmatrix} -20 & -50 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{\Sigma}_2^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} -20 \\ -50 \end{pmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{pmatrix} -20 & -50 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -0.2245 - 0.0510 \\ 0.0204 - 0.4592 \end{pmatrix}\\[4pt]
&= \begin{pmatrix} -20 & -50 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -0.2245 + 0.0510 \\ 0.0204 - 0.4592 \end{pmatrix}\\[4pt]
&= \begin{pmatrix} -20 & -50 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -0.1735 \\ -0.4388 \end{pmatrix}\\[4pt]
&= (-20)(-0.1735) + (-50)(-0.4388) = 3.47 + 21.94 = 25.41
\end{aligned}
$$

$$g_2(\mathbf{x}) = \ln(0.4) - \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(9800) - \tfrac{1}{2}(25.41)$$

$$g_2(\mathbf{x}) = -0.9163 - 4.5850 - 12.705 \approx -18.21$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

$$g_1 \approx -15.47 \;>\; g_2 \approx -18.21 \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{x} \in \omega_1 \;\; (\text{Water})$$

Notice the same decision is reached as in §3.2, but the **confidence** (margin between $g_1$ and $g_2$) is now quantitatively measurable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Supervised Classification Workflow (KTU Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Multispectral Image] --> A1[Define Thematic Classes]
    A1 --> A2[Collect Ground Truth Pixels]
    A2 --> A3[Compute Class Mean Vectors]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Class Covariance Matrices]
    A4 --> A5{Select Classifier Type}
    A5 -->|Fastest| A6[Parallelepiped]
    A5 -->|Simple| A7[Minimum Distance]
    A5 -->|Most Accurate| A8[Maximum Likelihood]
    A6 --> A9[Decision Rule Applied to Every Pixel]
    A7 --> A9
    A8 --> A9
    A9 --> A10[Thematic Classified Map]
    A10 --> A11[Post-Processing: Majority Filter]
    A11 --> A12[Accuracy Assessment: Kappa, OA]
    A12 --> A13[Final Thematic Output]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Maximum Likelihood Classifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Training Data per Class] --> B1[Estimate Mean Vector mu]
    B0 --> B2[Estimate Covariance Matrix Sigma]
    B1 --> B3[Inverse Sigma for each class]
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4[Compute Log Determinant]
    B1 --> B5[Compute Mahalanobis Term]
    B2 --> B5
    B4 --> B6[Discriminant Function g of x]
    B5 --> B6
    B7[Prior Probability P omega] --> B6
    B8[Unknown Pixel x] --> B5
    B6 --> B9[Compare g1 g2 ... gm]
    B9 --> B10[Argmax gives Class Label]
    B10 --> B11[Classified Pixel Output]
```

### 4.3 Decision Boundary Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Feature Space 2D] --> C1[Class omega1 Cluster]
    C0 --> C2[Class omega2 Cluster]
    C1 --> C3[Boundary Type 1: Straight Line Minimum Distance]
    C1 --> C4[Boundary Type 2: Axis Aligned Box Parallelepiped]
    C1 --> C5[Boundary Type 3: Curved Quadratic Maximum Likelihood]
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
    C2 --> C5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three boundary types above are the *visual signature* of each classifier. The Minimum Distance classifier always produces **straight-line (perpendicular bisector) boundaries**, the Parallelepiped classifier produces **rectangular (box) boundaries**, while the Maximum Likelihood classifier produces **curved, generally quadratic boundaries** that follow the iso-probability contours of the Gaussian distribution.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Differentiate between the Minimum Distance Classifier and the Maximum Likelihood Classifier.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Minimum Distance | Maximum Likelihood |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision basis** | Euclidean distance from mean | Bayes posterior probability |
| **Uses covariance?** | No | Yes |
| **Assumes distribution** | No assumption | Multivariate Gaussian |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Slower (matrix inversion) |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | Highest among classical methods |
| **Discriminant function** | $d_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)}$ | $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln P(\omega_i) - \tfrac{1}{2}\ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert - \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)$ |
| **Boundary shape** | Straight (linear) | Quadratic (curved) |

**(3 marks — Examiner allocates 1 mark each for the discriminant function, the use of covariance, and the boundary shape.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define (a) feature vector, (b) training set, and (c) decision boundary in the context of image classification.*

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Feature Vector** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$ — the $n$-dimensional vector formed by the digital numbers of a pixel in each of the $n$ spectral bands. It is the *input* to the classifier.

(b) **Training Set** — a collection of pixels whose true class label is already known (from field survey, existing maps, or photointerpretation). It is used to *train* the classifier by estimating $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ and $\mathbf{\Sigma}_i$.

(c) **Decision Boundary** — a hypersurface in the $n$-dimensional feature space that separates the regions assigned to different classes. Its geometric form (linear, box, quadratic) depends on the classifier used.

**(3 marks — 1 mark per definition.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete workflow of supervised image classification**, listing the role of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **discriminant function of the Maximum Likelihood Classifier** starting from Bayes' theorem. State clearly the assumption regarding the class-conditional probability density. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Workflow of Supervised Image Classification:**

The block diagram consists of the following stages in sequence:

1. **Image Pre-processing** — Radiometric and geometric corrections, atmospheric correction, and noise removal are applied to the input multi-band image.
2. **Definition of Classes** — The thematic classes of interest (e.g., Water, Forest, Urban, Barren) are defined in consultation with the user.
3. **Selection of Training Areas** — Representative pixels of each class are identified using ground truth, GPS, or visual interpretation of the image.
4. **Statistical Parameter Estimation** — For each class $\omega_i$, compute the mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ and covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}_i$ from the training pixels.
5. **Classification of Pixels** — Every unknown pixel is fed to the chosen classifier (Min. Distance, Parallelepiped, or MLC) which assigns a class label.
6. **Post-Classification Processing** — Majority / mode filter removes salt-and-pepper noise; clump and sieve operations merge small isolated regions.
7. **Accuracy Assessment** — Compare classified output with an independent test set using Overall Accuracy, Producer's Accuracy, User's Accuracy, and the Kappa coefficient.

**(7 marks — full credit awarded if all 7 stages are present with the correct order.)**

**(b) Derivation of the MLC Discriminant Function:**

Bayes' theorem states:

$$P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i)}{p(\mathbf{x})}$$

The classifier must assign $\mathbf{x}$ to the class with the **maximum posterior probability** $P(\omega_i \mid \mathbf{x})$. Since $p(\mathbf{x})$ is independent of $i$, the rule reduces to maximising $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)$.

**Assumption:** Each class $\omega_i$ has a multivariate Gaussian distribution:

$$p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left[ -\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) \right]$$

Taking the natural logarithm (which is monotonic, so arg-max is preserved):

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(\mathbf{x}) &= \ln\!\left[ p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i) \right] \\[3pt]
&= -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Dropping the constant $-\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi)$:

$$
\boxed{\, g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln P(\omega_i) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i) \,}
$$

The decision rule: assign $\mathbf{x}$ to $\omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x}) \;\; \forall \, j \neq i$.

**(7 marks — 2 marks for stating the assumption, 3 marks for correct substitution, 2 marks for the final simplified form and the decision rule.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **Minimum Distance Classifier** with its mathematical formulation. Mention two advantages and two limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-class classification problem has the following training statistics for a 2-band image:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 50 \\ 50 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 100 \\ 100 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{\Sigma}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 64 & 8 \\ 8 & 49 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{\Sigma}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 81 & 12 \\ 12 & 100 \end{pmatrix}$$

A-priori probabilities: $P(\omega_1) = P(\omega_2) = 0.5$. Classify the pixel $\mathbf{x} = (70, 80)^T$ using the **Maximum Likelihood Classifier**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Minimum Distance Classifier:**

The classifier computes the Euclidean distance from the unknown feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ to the mean vector $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$ of each class and assigns $\mathbf{x}$ to the nearest class.

$$d_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_k - \mu_{ik})^2} = \sqrt{(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)}$$

**Decision Rule:** $\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i$ if $d_i(\mathbf{x}) < d_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$.

**Advantages:**
1. Computationally very simple and fast — requires only $\boldsymbol{\mu}_i$, no $\mathbf{\Sigma}_i$.
2. Suitable for quick preliminary classification and for classes with similar variances.

**Limitations:**
1. Does not use the covariance information; classes of different sizes/shapes are misclassified.
2. Sensitive to outliers in the training set, since the mean is non-robust.

**(7 marks — 2 marks formula + 2 marks advantages + 2 marks limitations + 1 mark for diagram/decision rule.)**

**(b) MLC Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Mahalanobis distance squared for $\omega_1$:**

$$(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_1) = \begin{pmatrix} 70 - 50 \\ 80 - 50 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 20 \\ 30 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_1 \vert = 64 \times 49 - 8 \times 8 = 3136 - 64 = 3072$$

$$\mathbf{\Sigma}_1^{-1} = \frac{1}{3072}\begin{pmatrix} 49 & -8 \\ -8 & 64 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1^2 &= \frac{1}{3072}\left[ 20^2 \cdot 49 + 2 \cdot 20 \cdot 30 \cdot (-8) + 30^2 \cdot 64 \right] \\[3pt]
&= \frac{1}{3072}\left[ 19600 - 9600 + 57600 \right] = \frac{67600}{3072} \approx 22.005
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mahalanobis distance squared for $\omega_2$:**

$$(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2) = \begin{pmatrix} 70 - 100 \\ 80 - 100 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -30 \\ -20 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_2 \vert = 81 \times 100 - 12 \times 12 = 8100 - 144 = 7956$$

$$\mathbf{\Sigma}_2^{-1} = \frac{1}{7956}\begin{pmatrix} 100 & -12 \\ -12 & 81 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2^2 &= \frac{1}{7956}\left[ (-30)^2 \cdot 100 + 2 \cdot (-30)(-20) \cdot (-12) + (-20)^2 \cdot 81 \right] \\[3pt]
&= \frac{1}{7956}\left[ 90000 - 14400 + 32400 \right] = \frac{108000}{7956} \approx 13.575
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the discriminant values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_1(\mathbf{x}) &= \ln(0.5) - \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(3072) - \tfrac{1}{2}(22.005) \\
&= -0.6931 - \tfrac{1}{2}(8.0305) - 11.0025 \\
&= -0.6931 - 4.0153 - 11.0025 = -15.711
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_2(\mathbf{x}) &= \ln(0.5) - \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(7956) - \tfrac{1}{2}(13.575) \\
&= -0.6931 - \tfrac{1}{2}(8.9807) - 6.7875 \\
&= -0.6931 - 4.4904 - 6.7875 = -11.971
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$g_2(\mathbf{x}) \approx -11.97 \;>\; g_1(\mathbf{x}) \approx -15.71 \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{x} \in \omega_2$$

The pixel is classified into **class $\omega_2$**.

**(7 marks — 2 marks each for $D_1^2$ and $D_2^2$, 2 marks for $g_1$ and $g_2$, 1 mark for the final decision.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant $-\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi)$:** This term is independent of $i$ and *must* be dropped when comparing classes. Many students keep it and waste time.
> 2. **Sign errors in the Mahalanobis term:** Remember the term is $\mathbf{-} \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^T \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}(\mathbf{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})$, not $+\frac{1}{2}$. Sign error flips every decision.
> 3. **Confusing determinant with absolute value:** In $\ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma} \vert$, the bars denote the matrix **determinant**, not a norm or magnitude.
> 4. **Not mentioning the Gaussian assumption** in MLC derivations: A full-mark derivation must explicitly state that $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$ is modelled as a multivariate normal PDF.
> 5. **Mixing up "minimum distance" with "minimum error":** Minimum distance does *not* minimise misclassification error unless the classes are equi-probable and have identical, isotropic covariances.
> 6. **Forgetting the log step in MLC:** A direct comparison of $p(\mathbf{x} \mid \omega_i)$ values is mathematically valid but numerically unstable for high-dimensional problems; the log is mandatory in practice.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Classification** is a *supervised* pattern-recognition operation that assigns each pixel to one of $m$ pre-defined classes based on its $n$-dimensional feature vector.
- The three classical KTU-required classifiers are: **Minimum Distance** (uses $\boldsymbol{\mu}$), **Parallelepiped** (uses per-band ranges), and **Maximum Likelihood / Bayesian** (uses $\boldsymbol{\mu}$, $\mathbf{\Sigma}$, $P(\omega_i)$).
- The MLC discriminant function is $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \ln P(\omega_i) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \mathbf{\Sigma}_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)^T \mathbf{\Sigma}_i^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_i)$. **Memorise this.**
- The MLC assumes **multivariate Gaussian** class-conditional densities and uses the **MAP (Maximum A-Posteriori)** rule.
- Decision boundary geometry is a key identifying feature: Min. Distance → *linear*; Parallelepiped → *box*; MLC → *quadratic / curved*.
- The complete supervised classification pipeline has **7 stages**: pre-processing, class definition, training set selection, parameter estimation, classification, post-processing, and accuracy assessment.
- Accuracy is reported using the **confusion matrix, Overall Accuracy (OA), and Kappa coefficient $\kappa$**.
- A-priori probability $P(\omega_i)$ reflects the expected fraction of pixels in class $\omega_i$ *before* classification; it can be estimated from ground truth area or from the relative size of training samples.
- Training set must be **representative, sufficient, and pure**; impure training pixels are the single largest source of classification error.
- Always **state the Gaussian assumption** explicitly when deriving the MLC discriminant function.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Minimum Distance Classifier (MDC)** is a parametric supervised classification technique in pattern recognition and digital image processing that assigns an unknown feature (pattern) vector **x** to the class whose *mean vector* is *closest* to **x** in the multidimensional feature space. The "closeness" is measured using a distance metric — most commonly the **Euclidean distance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The Minimum Distance Classifier is the simplest statistical classifier. It assumes that the training samples of each class are clustered around their class mean vector in the feature space, and the class covariance is identical (or ignored). The classifier computes the distance from an unknown sample to every class mean and assigns the sample to the class with the minimum distance.

Mathematically, for $C$ pattern classes $\{\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_C\}$ with associated mean vectors $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_C\}$, the classification rule is:

$$x \in \omega_k \quad \text{if} \quad D(x, \mu_k) = \min_{j=1,\ldots,C} D(x, \mu_j)$$

where $D(\cdot,\cdot)$ is the chosen distance function.

## Conceptual Analogy — "The Post Office Counter"

Imagine a post office with several service counters, each representing a different category (Speed Post, Registered Post, Ordinary Mail). Each counter has a *signboard* showing its average transaction profile. When you walk in carrying a parcel, the postmaster looks at the parcel's characteristics and **physically walks you to the counter whose signboard profile is closest to your parcel's profile**. He doesn't perform any deep analysis of your parcel — he simply picks the *shortest visual match*.

In MDC:
- The **parcel** = unknown pixel feature vector **x** (e.g., spectral bands, texture values)
- The **counters** = the pattern classes $\omega_c$
- The **signboards** = the mean vectors $\mu_c$ learned during the training phase
- The **walking distance** = Euclidean distance $D(x, \mu_c)$
- The **chosen counter** = the assigned class label

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Highlights**
> 1. MDC is a *deterministic* parametric classifier — it requires no probability estimation.
> 2. It is essentially a template-matching technique where the "templates" are the class means.
> 3. The training phase computes $\mu_c$ for each class; the classification phase is a simple distance comparison.

## Key Notations and Terminology

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $x$ | Unknown feature vector (column vector of dimension $n$) |
| $\omega_c$ | The $c$-th pattern class (class label) |
| $\mu_c$ | Mean vector of class $\omega_c$ |
| $N_c$ | Number of training samples in class $\omega_c$ |
| $D(x, \mu_c)$ | Distance measure between $x$ and $\mu_c$ |
| $C$ | Total number of classes |
| $n$ | Number of features (dimensionality) |

> [!TIP]
> The classifier is called "minimum *distance*" but in actual implementation we almost always minimize the **squared distance** $D^2(x, \mu_c)$ because the square root is monotonic and computationally expensive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Learning Phase — Computing Class Mean Vectors

The **learning (training) phase** of MDC is trivially simple. Given $N_c$ training samples $\{x_1^{(c)}, x_2^{(c)}, \ldots, x_{N_c}^{(c)}\}$ belonging to class $\omega_c$, the class mean vector is computed as:

$$\mu_c = \frac{1}{N_c} \sum_{i=1}^{N_c} x_i^{(c)}$$

This is the **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)** of the true class mean under a Gaussian assumption, and it is the only parameter MDC needs to learn per class.

## Classification Phase — Distance Computation

Once the $\mu_c$ are stored, an unknown sample $x$ is classified by computing the (squared) Euclidean distance to each class mean and selecting the minimum:

**Squared Euclidean Distance** (most common in KTU problems):

$$D_c^2(x) = \vert x - \mu_c \vert^2 = (x - \mu_c)^T (x - \mu_c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu_{c,i})^2$$

**Mahalanobis Distance** (generalized form, accounts for covariance):

$$D_M(x, \mu_c) = (x - \mu_c)^T \Sigma_c^{-1} (x - \mu_c)$$

When all classes share an identical covariance $\Sigma$, Mahalanobis distance reduces to a *linear* discriminant (the *Linear Discriminant Analysis* case).

## Decision Boundary — Where Classes Meet

Between any two classes $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$, the decision boundary is the set of points equidistant from both means:

$$D(x, \mu_i) = D(x, \mu_j)$$

For the squared Euclidean distance, expanding:

$$(x - \mu_i)^T(x - \mu_i) = (x - \mu_j)^T(x - \mu_j)$$

$$x^T x - 2\mu_i^T x + \mu_i^T\mu_i = x^T x - 2\mu_j^T x + \mu_j^T\mu_j$$

$$2(\mu_j - \mu_i)^T x = \mu_j^T\mu_j - \mu_i^T\mu_i$$

$$(\mu_j - \mu_i)^T x = \frac{1}{2}(\mu_j^T\mu_j - \mu_i^T\mu_i)$$

This is a **linear hyperplane** in the $n$-dimensional feature space. Hence MDC is a *linear* classifier. In 2-D space, the boundary is a *line*; in 3-D space, a *plane*.

## Why MDC Works Well (and When It Fails)

**Strengths:**
- Computationally inexpensive — O(nC) per classification.
- No iteration, no convergence issues.
- Closed-form solution.
- Excellent baseline for comparison with more sophisticated classifiers.

**Weaknesses:**
- Ignores class covariance structure (loses information).
- Assumes classes are *spherically* distributed in feature space.
- Highly sensitive to outliers in training data.
- Performs poorly when classes have equal means but different spreads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use *squared* distance instead of distance itself?**
> The square root function $\sqrt{\cdot}$ is monotonically increasing for positive arguments. Therefore $\arg\min D(x, \mu_c) = \arg\min D^2(x, \mu_c)$. Squaring removes the expensive square root and the quadratic terms cancel during boundary derivation, yielding the linear form.

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu_c = \frac{1}{N_c}\sum_{i=1}^{N_c} x_i^{(c)}$ | Class mean estimate | Training phase |
| 2 | $D_c^2 = (x - \mu_c)^T(x - \mu_c)$ | Squared Euclidean distance | Classification phase |
| 3 | $x \in \omega_k \iff D_k^2 = \min_j D_j^2$ | Classification decision rule | Decision making |
| 4 | $(\mu_j - \mu_i)^T x = \frac{1}{2}(\Vert\mu_j\Vert^2 - \Vert\mu_i\Vert^2)$ | Decision hyperplane equation | Boundary plotting |
| 5 | $D_M = (x - \mu_c)^T \Sigma_c^{-1} (x - \mu_c)$ | Mahalanobis distance | When covariances differ |
| 6 | $g_c(x) = 2\mu_c^T x - \mu_c^T\mu_c$ | Discriminant function (MDC) | Implementation shortcut |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discriminant Function Trick** — Implementation often uses the equivalent form $g_c(x) = 2\mu_c^T x - \mu_c^T\mu_c$ because minimizing $D^2 = x^Tx - 2\mu_c^T x + \mu_c^T\mu_c$ is equivalent to maximizing $g_c(x)$ (the $x^Tx$ term is constant across all classes and thus cancels).

## Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Satellite Image Classification** — Land-cover mapping from multispectral imagery (water, vegetation, urban).
2. **Medical Imaging** — Tissue classification in MRI/CT (tumor vs. normal).
3. **OCR / Document Processing** — Character recognition.
4. **Quality Control** — Defect detection in manufactured goods based on texture features.
5. **Remote Sensing** — Crop-type identification using NDVI/EVI spectral features.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut** — When asked to "classify" a sample, never compute $\sqrt{\cdot}$. Just compute the squared distance and compare. The argmin is the same, and it saves 2 minutes per problem.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Worked Example 1 — Multi-Class Classification in 2-D Feature Space

> [!NOTE]
> This is a representative KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) problem.

**Problem Statement:**
A pattern recognition system uses 3 classes in a 2-dimensional feature space. The training data yields the following class mean vectors:

$$\mu_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mu_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mu_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

An unknown pixel has the feature vector:

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Using the **Minimum Distance Classifier** with squared Euclidean distance, classify $x$ into the appropriate class. Also derive the decision boundary between classes $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$.

### Step 1: Compute Squared Distance to Each Class Mean

For class $\omega_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1^2 &= (x - \mu_1)^T(x - \mu_1) \\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 3-1 & 3-1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3-1 \\ 3-1 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= (2)(2) + (2)(2) \\[4pt]
&= 4 + 4 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

For class $\omega_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2^2 &= (x - \mu_2)^T(x - \mu_2) \\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 3-4 & 3-5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3-4 \\ 3-5 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -2 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= (-1)(-1) + (-2)(-2) \\[4pt]
&= 1 + 4 = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

For class $\omega_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_3^2 &= (x - \mu_3)^T(x - \mu_3) \\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} 3-7 & 3-2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3-7 \\ 3-2 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{bmatrix} -4 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
&= (-4)(-4) + (1)(1) \\[4pt]
&= 16 + 1 = 17
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Minimum Distance Decision Rule

The distances are $D_1^2 = 8$, $D_2^2 = 5$, $D_3^2 = 17$.

Since $D_2^2 = 5 = \min\{8, 5, 17\}$, the classifier assigns:

$$x \in \omega_2 \quad (\text{Class 2})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The unknown feature vector $x = (3, 3)^T$ belongs to **Class 2**.

### Step 3: Derive the Decision Boundary Between $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$

The boundary is the locus of points where $D_1^2(x) = D_2^2(x)$.

Let $x = (x_1, x_2)^T$ be a general point on the boundary.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2 &= (x_1 - 4)^2 + (x_2 - 5)^2 \\[4pt]
x_1^2 - 2x_1 + 1 + x_2^2 - 2x_2 + 1 &= x_1^2 - 8x_1 + 16 + x_2^2 - 10x_2 + 25 \\[4pt]
-2x_1 - 2x_2 + 2 &= -8x_1 - 10x_2 + 41 \\[4pt]
6x_1 + 8x_2 - 39 &= 0 \\[4pt]
x_2 &= \frac{39 - 6x_1}{8} = -\frac{3}{4}x_1 + \frac{39}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boundary Line:** $\quad 6x_1 + 8x_2 = 39$

### Step 4: Verify by Testing a Point

Test point on boundary: $x_1 = 3$ gives $x_2 = \frac{39 - 18}{8} = \frac{21}{8} = 2.625$.

- $D_1^2 = (3-1)^2 + (2.625-1)^2 = 4 + 2.6406 = 6.6406$
- $D_2^2 = (3-4)^2 + (2.625-5)^2 = 1 + 5.6406 = 6.6406$ ✓

The boundary is verified. The point $x = (3, 3)^T$ has $x_2 = 3 > 2.625$, so it lies on the $\omega_2$ side of the boundary, confirming the classification.

---

## Worked Example 2 — Multi-Spectral Image Pixel Classification

**Problem Statement:**
A 3-band (RGB) multispectral image has 3 land-cover classes with the following mean spectral vectors (from training data):

$$\mu_{\text{water}} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 35 \\ 90 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mu_{\text{veg}} = \begin{bmatrix} 45 \\ 80 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mu_{\text{soil}} = \begin{bmatrix} 120 \\ 90 \\ 70 \end{bmatrix}$$

A test pixel has the spectral value:

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ 50 \\ 85 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1: Compute Squared Distances**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{water}}^2 &= (30-20)^2 + (50-35)^2 + (85-90)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 100 + 225 + 25 = 350 \\[8pt]
D_{\text{veg}}^2 &= (30-45)^2 + (50-80)^2 + (85-50)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 225 + 900 + 1225 = 2350 \\[8pt]
D_{\text{soil}}^2 &= (30-120)^2 + (50-90)^2 + (85-70)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 8100 + 1600 + 225 = 9925
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Decision**

$\min\{350, 2350, 9925\} = 350 \implies x \in \omega_{\text{water}}$

**Result:** The test pixel is classified as **WATER**. ✓ (The low R, low G, high B signature is indeed characteristic of deep water in visible RGB imagery.)

---

## Derivation — Why the Decision Boundary is a Perpendicular Bisector in 2-D

The decision boundary between two points (means) in 2-D Euclidean space, given by $(\mu_j - \mu_i)^T x = \frac{1}{2}(\Vert\mu_j\Vert^2 - \Vert\mu_i\Vert^2)$, is geometrically the **perpendicular bisector** of the line segment joining $\mu_i$ and $\mu_j$.

**Proof Sketch:**

The line segment joining $\mu_i$ and $\mu_j$ has direction vector $v = \mu_j - \mu_i$ and midpoint $m = \frac{1}{2}(\mu_i + \mu_j)$.

Any point $x$ on the perpendicular bisector satisfies $(x - m) \perp v$:

$$(x - m)^T v = 0 \implies x^T(\mu_j - \mu_i) = m^T(\mu_j - \mu_i) = \frac{1}{2}(\mu_i + \mu_j)^T(\mu_j - \mu_i)$$

Expanding the right side using $(\mu_i + \mu_j)^T(\mu_j - \mu_i) = \mu_j^T\mu_j - \mu_i^T\mu_i$ (a telescoping difference of squares), we recover the MDC boundary equation. **QED.**

> [!TIP]
> This geometric insight means: in 2-D, draw a perpendicular line halfway between the two class means — that line is the MDC decision boundary. Useful for visualization questions in the exam.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Figure 1 — Minimum Distance Classifier Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete data flow during the classification phase of MDC, from input feature vector to output class label.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Unknown Feature Vector x] --> B[Stage 1: Feature Extraction / Normalization]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Distance Computation Module]
    D[Trained Database: mu1, mu2, ..., muC] --> C
    C --> E[Stage 3: Squared Distance Array D1sq, D2sq, ..., DCsq]
    E --> F[Stage 4: Argmin Comparator]
    F --> G{Stage 5: Minimum Found?}
    G --> H[OUTPUT: Class Label wk where Dksq is minimum]
    G --> I[ERROR HANDLER: Reject Sample]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#f4b183,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## Figure 2 — Decision Region Geometry in 2-D Feature Space

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the Voronoi-tessellated decision regions produced by an MDC with 4 classes. Each region consists of all points closer to one class mean than to any other — the **Voronoi cell** of that mean.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEATSPACE["2-D Feature Space R2"]
        P1[mu1 at 1,1]:::mean
        P2[mu2 at 5,5]:::mean
        P3[mu3 at 8,2]:::mean
        P4[mu4 at 3,8]:::mean
    end

    subgraph REGION1["Region R1: Voronoi cell of mu1"]
        X1[x belongs to w1]
    end
    subgraph REGION2["Region R2: Voronoi cell of mu2"]
        X2[x belongs to w2]
    end
    subgraph REGION3["Region R3: Voronoi cell of mu3"]
        X3[x belongs to w3]
    end
    subgraph REGION4["Region R4: Voronoi cell of mu4"]
        X4[x belongs to w4]
    end

    P1 --- REGION1
    P2 --- REGION2
    P3 --- REGION3
    P4 --- REGION4

    REGION1 ---|perpendicular bisector| REGION2
    REGION2 ---|perpendicular bisector| REGION3
    REGION3 ---|perpendicular bisector| REGION4
    REGION1 ---|perpendicular bisector| REGION4

    classDef mean fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Voronoi Tessellation** — The MDC partitions the entire feature space into $C$ convex polygonal regions, one per class. The boundary between any two adjacent regions is a *perpendicular bisector* of the segment joining the two corresponding means. This is exactly how MDC generalizes to multi-class problems.

## Figure 3 — Learning vs Classification Phases (Process Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEARN["LEARNING PHASE: Offline, Done Once"]
        L1[Training Samples: x1, x2, ..., xN] --> L2[Group Samples by Class Label]
        L2 --> L3[Compute Mean Vector mu_c for each class c]
        L3 --> L4[Store mu1, mu2, ..., muC in Database]
    end

    subgraph CLASS["CLASSIFICATION PHASE: Online, Repeated per Pixel"]
        C1[Unknown Sample x] --> C2[Retrieve mu1...muC from Database]
        C2 --> C3[Compute Dsq x mu_c for all c]
        C3 --> C4[Find k = argmin Dsq]
        C4 --> C5[Assign Label wk to x]
    end

    L4 --> C2

    style L1 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000
    style L2 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000
    style L3 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000
    style L4 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000
    style C1 fill:#fde0dd,stroke:#000
    style C2 fill:#fde0dd,stroke:#000
    style C3 fill:#fde0dd,stroke:#000
    style C4 fill:#fde0dd,stroke:#000
    style C5 fill:#fde0dd,stroke:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The learning phase is a *one-time preprocessing* cost, while classification runs for every pixel in the image. For an $M \times N$ image, classification is executed $MN$ times — hence the importance of the O(nC) per-pixel cost being minimal.
