# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define Minimum Distance Classifier. List its two main limitations.

**Model Answer:**

The **Minimum Distance Classifier** is a supervised parametric classification technique that assigns an unknown pattern vector $x$ to the class $\omega_k$ whose mean vector $\mu_k$ is closest to $x$ in the Euclidean sense. The decision rule is:

$$x \in \omega_k \iff D(x, \mu_k) = \min_{j=1,\ldots,C} D(x, \mu_j)$$

**Two limitations:**

1. **Ignores class covariance structure** — it treats all directions in feature space equally, so it fails when classes are elongated or correlated.
2. **Sensitive to outliers in training data** — the mean is not a robust statistic, so a single outlier in $\omega_c$ can shift $\mu_c$ and misclassify many test samples.

> **[3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for rule + 0.5 each for limitations]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q2. State the formula for computing the class mean vector during the training phase of MDC and explain its significance.

**Model Answer:**

For class $\omega_c$ with $N_c$ training samples $\{x_1^{(c)}, x_2^{(c)}, \ldots, x_{N_c}^{(c)}\}$, the mean vector is:

$$\mu_c = \frac{1}{N_c} \sum_{i=1}^{N_c} x_i^{(c)}$$

**Significance:**
- $\mu_c$ is the *Maximum Likelihood Estimate* of the true class mean under a Gaussian assumption.
- It serves as the *representative prototype* of the class in the feature space.
- It is the *only parameter* that MDC learns — making it computationally very cheap.
- The MDC implicitly assumes that all training samples of a class are clustered around $\mu_c$.

> **[3 marks: 1.5 marks for formula + 1.5 marks for explanation]**

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice — Select ONE of the following:**

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the equation of the decision boundary between two classes $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ in an MDC using squared Euclidean distance. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Consider a 2-class problem in 2-D feature space with mean vectors:

$$\mu_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mu_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Classify the sample $x = (4, 3)^T$ using the Minimum Distance Classifier. Also write the equation of the decision boundary and verify whether the sample lies on the correct side. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Step 1: Setup.** A point $x$ lies on the decision boundary between $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ iff it is equidistant from both class means:

$$D^2(x, \mu_1) = D^2(x, \mu_2)$$

**Step 2: Expand the squared Euclidean distances:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x - \mu_1)^T(x - \mu_1) &= (x - \mu_2)^T(x - \mu_2) \\[4pt]
x^T x - 2\mu_1^T x + \mu_1^T\mu_1 &= x^T x - 2\mu_2^T x + \mu_2^T\mu_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Cancel $x^T x$ from both sides:**

$$- 2\mu_1^T x + \mu_1^T\mu_1 = - 2\mu_2^T x + \mu_2^T\mu_2$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to bring $x$ terms to one side and constant terms to the other:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\mu_2^T x - 2\mu_1^T x &= \mu_2^T\mu_2 - \mu_1^T\mu_1 \\[4pt]
2(\mu_2 - \mu_1)^T x &= \Vert\mu_2\Vert^2 - \Vert\mu_1\Vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final boundary equation:**

$$\boxed{(\mu_2 - \mu_1)^T x = \frac{1}{2}\left(\Vert\mu_2\Vert^2 - \Vert\mu_1\Vert^2\right)}$$

This is a linear hyperplane in the feature space — proof that MDC is a **linear classifier**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Step 1: Compute the squared Euclidean distance to $\mu_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1^2 &= (x - \mu_1)^T(x - \mu_1) \\[4pt]
&= (4-2)^2 + (3-1)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 4 + 4 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the squared Euclidean distance to $\mu_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2^2 &= (x - \mu_2)^T(x - \mu_2) \\[4pt]
&= (4-6)^2 + (3-5)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 4 + 4 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Decision:**

$D_1^2 = D_2^2 = 8$. The sample is **equidistant from both means**, i.e., it lies **exactly on the decision boundary**. By convention, it is assigned to the class with the *lower index* (or it can be flagged as ambiguous).

$$\boxed{x \text{ lies on the boundary between } \omega_1 \text{ and } \omega_2}$$

**Step 4: Derive the boundary equation.** With $x = (x_1, x_2)^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2 &= (x_1 - 6)^2 + (x_2 - 5)^2 \\[4pt]
x_1^2 - 4x_1 + 4 + x_2^2 - 2x_2 + 1 &= x_1^2 - 12x_1 + 36 + x_2^2 - 10x_2 + 25 \\[4pt]
-4x_1 - 2x_2 + 5 &= -12x_1 - 10x_2 + 61 \\[4pt]
8x_1 + 8x_2 - 56 &= 0 \\[4pt]
x_1 + x_2 &= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Verification.** For $x = (4, 3)$: $x_1 + x_2 = 4 + 3 = 7$ ✓ (lies exactly on the boundary). **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **learning phase** and the **classification phase** of the Minimum Distance Classifier. Why is the squared distance preferred over the actual distance? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A 3-class land-cover classification problem has the following mean spectral vectors in 4 spectral bands:

$$\mu_{\text{water}} = \begin{bmatrix} 30 \\ 40 \\ 25 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mu_{\text{forest}} = \begin{bmatrix} 50 \\ 70 \\ 60 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mu_{\text{urban}} = \begin{bmatrix} 110 \\ 100 \\ 95 \\ 85 \end{bmatrix}$$

A test pixel has feature vector $x = (35, 45, 30, 10)^T$. Classify it using the MDC. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Learning Phase (offline, done once):**

1. Collect $N$ training samples with known class labels.
2. Partition the samples into $C$ groups according to their class.
3. For each class $\omega_c$, compute the mean vector $\mu_c = \frac{1}{N_c}\sum_{i=1}^{N_c} x_i^{(c)}$.
4. Store all $C$ mean vectors in a reference database.

**Classification Phase (online, done per test sample):**

1. For an unknown $x$, retrieve the $C$ stored mean vectors.
2. Compute the squared Euclidean distance $D_c^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu_{c,i})^2$ to each class.
3. Find the index $k = \arg\min_c D_c^2$.
4. Assign $x$ to class $\omega_k$.

**Why squared distance is preferred:**

1. **Removes the square root** — $\sqrt{\cdot}$ is computationally expensive.
2. **Monotonicity** — since $D^2$ and $D$ have the same ordering, $\arg\min D = \arg\min D^2$. So no information is lost.
3. **Algebraic convenience** — the squared form is differentiable, simplifying derivations of the decision boundary (the quadratic $x^Tx$ terms cancel across classes, leaving a *linear* boundary).
4. **Numerical stability** — avoids numerical errors from the square root operation on small numbers. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Step 1: Distance to water:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{water}}^2 &= (35-30)^2 + (45-40)^2 + (30-25)^2 + (10-5)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 25 + 25 + 25 + 25 = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Distance to forest:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{forest}}^2 &= (35-50)^2 + (45-70)^2 + (30-60)^2 + (10-30)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 225 + 625 + 900 + 400 = 2150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Distance to urban:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{urban}}^2 &= (35-110)^2 + (45-100)^2 + (30-95)^2 + (10-85)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 5625 + 3025 + 4225 + 5625 = 18500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Decision rule:**

$\min\{100, 2150, 18500\} = 100 \implies x \in \omega_{\text{water}}$

**Result:** The test pixel is classified as **WATER** (low spectral response in all bands is characteristic of clear deep water in multispectral imagery). **[7 marks]**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in MDC Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the square root and computing actual distance** — Although $D^2$ is sufficient, some textbooks teach the unsquared form. Pick one convention and stick to it. The argmin is *identical*.
>
> 2. **Transposing the wrong way** — $(x - \mu)^T (x - \mu)$ is a scalar; $(x - \mu)(x - \mu)^T$ is a matrix. Don't confuse them. The squared distance is always the *scalar* form.
>
> 3. **Sign errors in boundary derivation** — When you bring $x$ terms to one side, be careful with sign flips: $-2\mu_1^T x = -2\mu_2^T x + \text{const}$ becomes $2(\mu_2 - \mu_1)^T x = \text{const}$. Sloppy sign handling costs 2-3 marks.
>
> 4. **Not canceling the $x^T x$ term** — This is the most common algebraic slip. Both sides contain $x^T x$ and they cancel. Forgetting to cancel leaves a *quadratic* boundary (which is wrong for MDC) and indicates a fundamental misunderstanding.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Mahalanobis with Euclidean** — If the question specifies "use Mahalanobis distance" or gives a covariance matrix, you must use $\Sigma^{-1}$. Otherwise, MDC means Euclidean.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to mention "squared" or "monotonic"** — In theory questions, examiners expect the one-line justification for using $D^2$ instead of $D$. Omitting it costs 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Minimum Distance Classifier**

- [x] **Definition** — Assigns $x$ to the class whose mean $\mu_c$ is closest in Euclidean feature space.
- [x] **Training Formula** — $\mu_c = \frac{1}{N_c}\sum x_i^{(c)}$ (mean vector of class $\omega_c$).
- [x] **Classification Rule** — $x \in \omega_k \iff D_k^2(x) = \min_j D_j^2(x)$.
- [x] **Squared Euclidean Distance** — $D^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu_{c,i})^2$.
- [x] **Decision Boundary** — $(\mu_j - \mu_i)^T x = \frac{1}{2}(\Vert\mu_j\Vert^2 - \Vert\mu_i\Vert^2)$ — a *linear* hyperplane.
- [x] **Discriminant Function** — $g_c(x) = 2\mu_c^T x - \mu_c^T\mu_c$ (maximize this equivalently).
- [x] **Voronoi Tessellation** — MDC partitions feature space into polygonal Voronoi cells, one per class.
- [x] **Why squared distance** — removes square root, monotonic, algebraically convenient.
- [x] **Why use MDC** — simple, fast (O(nC)), closed-form, no iteration.
- [x] **Limitations** — ignores covariance, sensitive to outliers, assumes spherical clusters.
- [x] **When MDC ≠ Bayes optimal** — when classes have different covariances or non-spherical shapes, prefer **Mahalanobis distance** or **Maximum Likelihood classifier**.
- [x] **Geometric meaning** — 2-D boundary is the perpendicular bisector between the two class means.
- [x] **Implementation Order** — (1) Compute all $D_c^2$, (2) find min, (3) assign label. Never the other way around.
- [x] **Connection to KNN** — MDC is essentially a 1-NN classifier with the *class mean* as the single prototype (cheaper but less accurate than full KNN).

> [!TIP]
> **One-line Exam Hook** — *"The MDC classifies by computing squared distances to class mean vectors; the boundary between any two classes is a linear hyperplane defined by the perpendicular bisector of the line joining their means."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nearest Neighbor Search with K-D Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT525 - FUNDAMENTALS OF DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nearest Neighbor Search with K-D Trees

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a K-D Tree

A **K-Dimensional Tree (K-D Tree)** is a space-partitioning binary tree data structure that recursively subdivides a k-dimensional feature space into progressively smaller axis-aligned rectangular regions. Each **internal node** of the tree stores a single k-dimensional data point together with a **splitting hyperplane** (in 2-D this is a vertical or horizontal line, in higher dimensions a hyperplane) that partitions the remaining space into two half-spaces. Points lying on one side of the hyperplane form the **left subtree**, and points on the other side form the **right subtree**. The **leaf nodes** contain the actual data points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):**
> A K-D tree is a binary tree in which every node represents a k-dimensional point, every non-leaf node generates a splitting hyperplane perpendicular to one of the coordinate axes, and the left/right subtrees correspond to points lying on either side of that hyperplane. For nearest neighbor search in image processing, k typically equals the descriptor dimension (e.g., **k = 128 for SIFT**, **k = 64 for SURF**).

The **Nearest Neighbor (NN) Search** problem is defined as: given a set $\mathcal{S}$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^k$ and a **query point** $q \in \mathbb{R}^k$, find the point $p^* \in \mathcal{S}$ such that the distance $d(q, p^*)$ is minimized, i.e.,

$$p^* = \arg\min_{p \in \mathcal{S}} \, d(q, p)$$

where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is typically the **squared Euclidean distance** $d(q,p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}(q_i - p_i)^2$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Card Catalogue" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a huge library containing **millions of books**, and you want the single book whose topic vector (subject keywords) is most similar to a reference book you already have. A *brute-force* approach would be to pull every book off the shelf and read its keywords — this is $O(n)$ and impossibly slow for $n = 10^6$.

A K-D tree is the librarian's secret weapon. The librarian first splits the library by **subject area** (e.g., Science on the left, Arts on the right), then within each half, splits again by **publication year**, then by **author nationality**, and so on. To find the closest book, you walk down a single path, finding a *candidate*, and then **backtrack** only when a side branch might still hide a closer match. Most of the library is never visited — the search drops to roughly $O(\log n)$ on average.

> [!NOTE]
> **In image processing**, the "library" is a database of feature descriptors (SIFT/SURF/ORB vectors) extracted from one image, and the "query book" is a descriptor from another image. K-D trees power **feature matching**, **image stitching**, **object recognition**, and **3-D reconstruction**.

## 1.3 Why K-D Trees Matter in Digital Image Processing

| Application | Role of K-D Tree |
|---|---|
| **SIFT / SURF Feature Matching** | Find best descriptor match in $O(\log n)$ |
| **Image Stitching (Panorama)** | Match keypoints between overlapping images |
| **Content-Based Image Retrieval (CBIR)** | Search large image databases by visual similarity |
| **Bag-of-Features classification** | Assign visual words to image patches |
| **Optical Flow / Tracking** | Match feature points across video frames |
| **Point Cloud Registration** | Correspond 3-D points in LiDAR / stereo reconstruction |

> [!TIP]
> A K-D tree is **NOT** a balanced binary search tree in the classical sense — it is a *spatial* binary tree. The ordering is by spatial partition, not by a single scalar key. This distinction is a common KTU exam trap.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 K-D Tree Construction — Step-by-Step Logic

The construction algorithm cycles through axes, and at each level picks a **splitting coordinate** along that axis. The most common variant (used in SIFT/FLANN) is the **median-split balanced K-D tree**:

### Construction Algorithm (Recursive)

1. **Axis Selection Cycle**: At depth $d$ of the tree, split along axis $j = (d \bmod k) + 1$, where $k$ is the dimensionality. So a 2-D tree cycles through $(x, y, x, y, \ldots)$, a 128-D tree cycles through $1 \to 128 \to 1 \to \ldots$.
2. **Splitting Value Selection**: Find the **median** of the coordinate values of all points in the current node along axis $j$. The point with the median coordinate becomes the **splitting node**.
3. **Partition**: Place all points with coordinate $< \text{median}$ in the **left subtree**, and points with coordinate $\geq \text{median}$ in the **right subtree**.
4. **Recurse**: Apply steps 1–3 to the left and right subtrees until each subtree contains only one point (leaf).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **balanced K-D tree** (median split) gives $O(\log n)$ depth and $O(n \log n)$ construction. A simple mid-point split is $O(n \log n)$ but faster in practice. A naive random split is faster to build but degrades search performance.

## 2.2 Nearest Neighbor Search Algorithm

The search is a two-phase process: **descend-then-backtrack**.

### Phase 1: Recursive Descent to Leaf (Find Initial Candidate)

1. Start at the root node.
2. At each node, compare the query point $q$ with the splitting hyperplane along axis $j$. If $q_j < \text{node}_j$, recurse into the **left** child; otherwise, recurse into the **right**.
3. When a leaf is reached, record that point as the **current best** $p^*$ and compute the **current best distance** $d^* = d(q, p^*)$.

### Phase 2: Backtracking with Pruning

1. **Unwind** the recursion. At each ancestor node, check whether the point stored at that node is closer than $d^*$. If yes, update $p^*$ and $d^*$.
2. **Hyperplane test**: Check whether the *splitting hyperplane* (perpendicular distance from $q$ to the split plane) is closer than $d^*$. If yes, the **other subtree** may contain a closer point and **must be searched recursively**. If not, **prune** the other subtree entirely.
3. Continue until the root is processed.

> [!NOTE]
> The squared distance from query $q$ to the splitting hyperplane at axis $j$ (with split value $s$) is simply $(q_j - s)^2$. If $(q_j - s)^2 \geq d^*$, the opposite subtree is guaranteed not to contain a closer point.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Squared Euclidean Distance (L2²) | $d(q,p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}(q_i - p_i)^2$ | Most common; avoids square root |
| 2 | Manhattan Distance (L1) | $d(q,p) = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\vert q_i - p_i \vert$ | Used when speed matters |
| 3 | Chebyshev Distance (L∞) | $d(q,p) = \max_{i} \vert q_i - p_i \vert$ | Useful for box-distance pruning |
| 4 | Hyperplane Distance | $d_{\text{plane}} = (q_j - s)^2$ | Distance to split plane at axis $j$ |
| 5 | Tree Depth (balanced) | $h = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | Median-split K-D tree |
| 6 | Construction Time | $O(n \log n)$ | Median computation at each level |
| 7 | Search Time (average) | $O(\log n)$ | For low-dimensional, uniform data |
| 8 | Search Time (worst case) | $O(n)$ | Adversarial / very high $k$ |
| 9 | Search Time (high-d) | $O(k \cdot n^{1-1/k})$ | Effective behavior for $k > 20$ |
| 10 | Bounding Box Distance | $d_{\text{box}}(q,R) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \max(0, \min_{x \in R} x_i - q_i, q_i - \max_{x \in R} x_i)^2$ | Lower bound on true distance |
| 11 | Best-Bin-First (BBF) priority | priority = distance to hyperplane | Heuristic — visits "closest" bins first |
| 12 | Approximate NN threshold | $d_{\text{ratio}} = d_1 / d_2 < 0.8$ | Lowe's ratio test for matching |

> [!WARNING]
> **Distance-metric pitfall**: K-D trees assume the **same units** across all dimensions. If you mix raw pixel intensities (range 0–255) with normalized gradient magnitudes (range 0–1), the high-variance dimension will dominate. **Always standardize/normalize** descriptors (e.g., SIFT is already L2-normalized).

## 2.4 Variants of K-D Trees Used in Practice

1. **Classical Balanced K-D Tree** — Best for $k \le 20$, exact NN.
2. **Best-Bin-First (BBF) K-D Tree** (Lowe, 1999) — Uses a priority queue ordered by hyperplane distance, allows bounded search. Used in SIFT matching.
3. **Randomized K-D Tree** (Silpa-Anan & Hartley, 2008) — Builds many trees with random split dimensions; query searches all in parallel.
4. **Hierarchical K-Means Tree** (Nister & Stewenius, 2006) — Used in **Vocabulary Trees** for large-scale CBIR; conceptually similar but uses k-means clustering instead of medians.
5. **Approximate K-D Tree** (FLANN library) — Automatically picks the best variant for your data.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles**: Match LiDAR point-cloud descriptors in real-time (10–100 ms budget).
- **Medical Imaging**: Register MRI/CT volumes by matching SIFT keypoints.
- **Augmented Reality (AR)**: Match feature points for plane detection and pose estimation (ARKit, ARCore).
- **Forensics / Surveillance**: Match face descriptors across millions of images in a watch-list database.
- **Satellite Imaging**: Mosaic large satellite swaths by matching overlapping features.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Median-Split Construction for a 2-D Example

Consider the 2-D point set $\mathcal{S} = \{(2,3), (5,4), (9,6), (4,7), (8,1), (7,2)\}$. We construct a balanced K-D tree with $k=2$ axes $(x,y)$.

**Level 0 (Root) — Split along axis $x$:**

Step 1: List $x$-coordinates: $2, 5, 9, 4, 8, 7$.

Step 2: Sort: $2, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9$.

Step 3: Median index $= \lfloor 6/2 \rfloor = 3$ (0-indexed), so median value $= 5$ (point $(5,4)$ becomes the root).

Step 4: Partition — points with $x < 5$: $\{(2,3), (4,7)\}$. Points with $x \geq 5$: $\{(9,6), (8,1), (7,2)\}$.

**Level 1 (Left Subtree) — Split along axis $y$:**

Left set = $\{(2,3), (4,7)\}$. List $y$-values: $3, 7$. Median: $3$ → node $(2,3)$ becomes the left child. Right child of $(2,3)$: $(4,7)$.

**Level 1 (Right Subtree) — Split along axis $y$:**

Right set = $\{(9,6), (8,1), (7,2)\}$. Sort $y$: $1, 2, 6$. Median $= 2$ → node $(7,2)$ becomes the right child. Partition $y<2$: $\{(8,1)\}$. Partition $y \geq 2$: $\{(9,6)\}$.

**Level 2 (Final leaves):**

$(4,7)$ leaf under $(2,3)$'s right child. $(8,1)$ leaf under $(7,2)$'s left child. $(9,6)$ leaf under $(7,2)$'s right child.

The final K-D tree looks like:

```
                (5, 4)              [split: x = 5]
               /       \
           (2,3)        (7,2)         [split: y = 3, y = 2]
           /    \        /   \
         leaf  (4,7)  (8,1) (9,6)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hyperplanes are *implicit* — the tree stores the **split point** and the **axis** of the split. The hyperplane is the line $x = 5$ at the root, $y = 3$ at the left child, $y = 2$ at the right child.

## 3.2 Derivation: Nearest Neighbor Query Walkthrough

**Query**: $q = (6, 5)$, looking for the nearest neighbor in $\mathcal{S} = \{(2,3), (5,4), (9,6), (4,7), (8,1), (7,2)\}$ using the tree built above.

**Phase 1: Descend**

Step 1: At root $(5,4)$, split axis is $x$. Compare $q_x = 6$ with split $5$: $6 \geq 5$, so go **right** to $(7,2)$. Update priority stack: $[(5,4)]$.

Step 2: At $(7,2)$, split axis is $y$. Compare $q_y = 5$ with split $2$: $5 \geq 2$, so go **right** to $(9,6)$. Push $(7,2)$.

Step 3: At $(9,6)$, this is a leaf. Compute distance:

$$d^2(q, (9,6)) = (6-9)^2 + (5-6)^2 = 9 + 1 = 10$$

Set $d^* = 10$, $p^* = (9,6)$.

**Phase 2: Backtrack**

Step 4: Unwind to $(7,2)$. Check its point: $d^2(q,(7,2)) = (6-7)^2 + (5-2)^2 = 1 + 9 = 10$. Tie with current best, keep $(9,6)$ (or update per implementation).

Compute distance from $q$ to the splitting hyperplane at $(7,2)$: hyperplane is $y = 2$, so distance squared is $(q_y - 2)^2 = (5-2)^2 = 9$.

Since $9 < 10 = d^*$, the **other subtree** (left child $(8,1)$) may contain a closer point. **Search it.**

Step 5: Visit $(8,1)$. $d^2(q,(8,1)) = (6-8)^2 + (5-1)^2 = 4 + 16 = 20 > 10$. No update.

Step 6: Unwind to root $(5,4)$. Check its point: $d^2(q,(5,4)) = (6-5)^2 + (5-4)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2 < 10$. **Update** $p^* = (5,4)$, $d^* = 2$.

Compute distance to hyperplane $x = 5$: $(q_x - 5)^2 = (6-5)^2 = 1$. Since $1 < 2 = d^*$, the other subtree (left child $(2,3)$) may contain a closer point. **Search it.**

Step 7: Visit $(2,3)$. $d^2(q,(2,3)) = (6-2)^2 + (5-3)^2 = 16 + 4 = 20 > 2$. No update.

Step 8: Visit $(4,7)$. $d^2(q,(4,7)) = (6-4)^2 + (5-7)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8 > 2$. No update.

**Final Answer**: $p^* = (5,4)$ with $d^* = 2$. The query visits only **5 of 6** points — the K-D tree skipped $(7,2)$'s right child in one branch and (eventually) all of left's right was explored. In larger trees, this pruning is far more dramatic.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why K-D Tree Search is Efficient

Define the search cost as the number of nodes visited. The efficiency comes from the **pruning test**:

$$\text{Prune opposite subtree} \iff (q_j - s_j)^2 \geq d^*$$

Equivalently, the opposite subtree is searched iff

$$(q_j - s_j)^2 < d^*$$

In a balanced 2-D tree of $n$ points, the expected number of visited nodes is:

$$E[\text{visited}] \approx 2 \cdot n^{1/2} + O(\log n)$$

For general $k$:

$$E[\text{visited}] \approx c_k \cdot n^{1 - 1/k} + O(\log n)$$

where $c_k$ is a constant depending on dimensionality. This shows why K-D trees excel in **low dimensions** but degrade as $k$ grows past ~20 (the "curse of dimensionality").

## 3.4 The Curse of Dimensionality — Quantified

For $k$-dimensional Euclidean NN search, the ratio of the **expected nearest neighbor distance** $d_{\text{NN}}$ to the **average point-to-point distance** $d_{\text{avg}}$ behaves as:

$$\frac{d_{\text{NN}}}{d_{\text{avg}}} \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{k}} \quad \text{(uniform distribution, large n)}$$

This means in high dimensions, the NN is **nearly as far as the average point** — there is no meaningful "nearest." This is why SIFT ($k=128$) often uses **Randomized K-D Forest** or **PQ (Product Quantization)** instead of a single K-D tree for $n > 10^6$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical rule**: Use a single K-D tree for $n < 10^5$ and $k < 16$. Use randomized K-D forest for $n \in [10^5, 10^7]$. Use approximate methods (PQ, HNSW) for $n > 10^7$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — BBF Search Priority

Using the same tree, query $q = (6,5)$, but now with **Best-Bin-First** ordering. The priority queue stores subtrees by their *hyperplane distance*, lowest first.

| Step | Node Visited | Hyperplane Distance | Best $d^*$ so far |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Root $(5,4)$ | — | — |
| 2 | Right child $(7,2)$ | $(5-2)^2 = 9$ | — |
| 3 | Right-right $(9,6)$ | leaf | 10 |
| 4 | Right-left $(8,1)$ | $(6-2)^2 = 16$ | 10 |
| 5 | Root left $(2,3)$ | $(6-5)^2 = 1$ | 2 (after $(5,4)$ check) |

The BBF search **caps** the number of leaf checks (e.g., 200) to guarantee bounded runtime. This is the key to making SIFT matching work on millions of keypoints in real time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-D Tree Partitioning of 2-D Space (Block-Level Schematic)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **recursive spatial partitioning** performed by a K-D tree. Each split halves the remaining space along one axis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ROOT SPLIT: Vertical Line x = 5. Splits 2D space into LEFT and RIGHT halves. Root node stores point (5, 4)"] --> B["LEFT SUBSPACE: All points with x less than 5"]
    A --> C["RIGHT SUBSPACE: All points with x greater than or equal to 5"]
    B --> D["LEFT-LEFT SPLIT: Horizontal Line y = 3. Splits left subspace. Node (2, 3)"]
    B --> E["LEFT-RIGHT LEAF: Point (4, 7). Reached when left subtree has only one point"]
    C --> F["RIGHT-LEFT SPLIT: Horizontal Line y = 2. Splits right subspace. Node (7, 2)"]
    C --> G["RIGHT-RIGHT LEAF: Point (9, 6)"]
    D --> H["LEFT-LEFT-RIGHT LEAF: Point (4, 7)"]
    D --> I["LEFT-LEFT-LEFT LEAF: Point (2, 3) is the split itself"]
    F --> J["RIGHT-LEFT-LEFT LEAF: Point (8, 1)"]
    F --> K["RIGHT-LEFT-RIGHT LEAF: Point (9, 6)"]
```

## 4.2 Nearest Neighbor Search Flow (Descend + Backtrack)

The following Mermaid sequence illustrates the **two-phase search** algorithm with the hyperplane-pruning decision at each backtrack.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START: Query point q arrives at root node"] --> S2["At current node, compare q with split axis. Recurse into LEFT or RIGHT child."]
    S2 --> S3{"Did we reach a LEAF node?"}
    S3 -- NO --> S2
    S3 -- YES --> S4["Record leaf point as CURRENT BEST candidate. Compute initial best distance d*."]
    S4 --> S5["Begin BACKTRACK: pop ancestor from recursion stack."]
    S5 --> S6["Compute distance from query to the ancestor node point. If closer than d*, update best."]
    S6 --> S7["Compute distance from query to ancestor SPLITTING HYPERPLANE: d_plane = q_j minus split squared."]
    S7 --> S8{"Is d_plane LESS THAN d*?"}
    S8 -- NO --> S9["PRUNE the other subtree. Skip it entirely."]
    S8 -- YES --> S10["SEARCH the other subtree recursively. May contain a closer point."]
    S9 --> S11{"More ancestors on stack?"}
    S10 --> S11
    S11 -- YES --> S5
    S11 -- NO --> S12["RETURN the final best candidate as nearest neighbor."]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: K-D Tree within a Feature-Matching Pipeline

This block diagram shows how K-D trees fit into a typical **SIFT-based image matching** system (panorama stitching example).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG1["Input Image 1: Reference Scene"] --> K1["SIFT Keypoint Detector: Find stable interest points in scale space"]
    IMG2["Input Image 2: Query Scene"] --> K2["SIFT Keypoint Detector: Find stable interest points in scale space"]
    K1 --> D1["Descriptor Extraction: 128D vector per keypoint"]
    K2 --> D2["Descriptor Extraction: 128D vector per keypoint"]
    D1 --> B["BUILD PHASE: Construct Balanced K-D Tree on Image 1 descriptors. Median split, cycle through 128 axes."]
    B --> T["KDTree: Balanced Binary Tree with depth log base 2 of n"]
    D2 --> Q["Query: For each Image 2 descriptor, search the K-D Tree"]
    Q --> P["Best-Bin-First Search with priority queue, capped at 200 leaves"]
    P --> R["Ratio Test: Lowe threshold 0.8 to filter ambiguous matches"]
    R --> M["Matched Keypoint Pairs: Correspondences between the two images"]
    M --> H["Homography Estimation: RANSAC to find transform"]
    H --> O["Stitched Output Panorama"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right NN Search Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Problem: Nearest Neighbor Search in k dimensions"] --> D{"What is the dimensionality k?"}
    D -- "k less than 16" --> B["Use CLASSICAL Balanced K-D Tree. Exact NN, O log n average."]
    D -- "k between 16 and 64" --> R["Use RANDOMIZED K-D FOREST. 4 to 8 trees, parallel query."]
    D -- "k greater than 64" --> A["Use APPROXIMATE methods: PQ, HNSW, or LSH."]
    B --> N1["Best for small to medium databases, n less than 10^5"]
    R --> N2["Best for large databases, n up to 10^7. Used in SIFT matching with millions of features."]
    A --> N3["Best for very large or very high dimensional data. Trade exactness for speed."]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define a K-D tree. Explain how it is constructed using the median-split method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A K-D tree is a binary space-partitioning data structure that recursively divides a k-dimensional space using axis-aligned hyperplanes. **Construction using median-split method (1 mark):**

1. At each level, the splitting axis cycles as $j = (depth \bmod k) + 1$. **(0.5 mark)**
2. The median of the coordinate values of all points in the current node along axis $j$ is computed. The point with the median coordinate becomes the current node. **(1 mark)**
3. Points with coordinate value less than the median form the left subtree, and points with value greater than or equal to the median form the right subtree. The process recurses on both subtrees. **(0.5 mark)**

This produces a balanced tree of depth $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ with $O(n \log n)$ construction time.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the hyperplane pruning test in K-D tree nearest neighbor search?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

During backtracking in NN search, the algorithm must decide whether to explore the *opposite* subtree. **The hyperplane test (1 mark):** at a node with splitting axis $j$ and split value $s$, compute the squared distance from query $q$ to the hyperplane: $d_{\text{plane}}^2 = (q_j - s)^2$.

**Pruning rule (1 mark):** if $d_{\text{plane}}^2 \geq d^*$ (the current best distance), then no point in the opposite subtree can be closer than $d^*$, and the entire subtree is **pruned** (skipped). Otherwise, the subtree is explored recursively.

**Significance (1 mark):** This test is the *only* reason K-D tree search is faster than brute force — it eliminates entire subtrees from consideration, reducing average complexity from $O(n)$ to $O(\log n)$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Internal Choice Option 1]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the nearest neighbor search algorithm using K-D trees. Describe the descent and backtracking phases with the hyperplane pruning condition.

**(b) [7 marks)** Consider the 2-D point set $S = \{(2,3), (5,4), (9,6), (4,7), (8,1), (7,2)\}$.
   (i) Construct a balanced K-D tree using median split, cycling axes as $x, y, x, y, \ldots$ **(3 marks)**
   (ii) Find the nearest neighbor of the query point $q = (6, 5)$ using the constructed tree. Show all steps of descent and backtracking. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Nearest Neighbor Search Algorithm (4 marks for explanation):**

The algorithm has two phases.

**Phase 1: Recursive Descent (2 marks)**
- Begin at the root. At each node with split axis $j$ and split value $s$, compare query coordinate $q_j$ with $s$.
- If $q_j < s$, traverse the **left** child; otherwise, traverse the **right** child.
- Continue until a leaf node is reached. The point at this leaf becomes the initial best candidate $p^*$ with distance $d^* = d(q, p^*)$.

**Phase 2: Backtracking with Pruning (2 marks)**
- Unwind the recursion. For each ancestor node on the path:
  1. **Test the ancestor's stored point**: compute $d(q, \text{ancestor})$; if less than $d^*$, update $p^*$ and $d^*$.
  2. **Test the hyperplane**: compute $d_{\text{plane}}^2 = (q_j - s)^2$.
     - If $d_{\text{plane}}^2 \geq d^*$, **prune** the opposite subtree (no need to visit).
     - If $d_{\text{plane}}^2 < d^*$, **recurse** into the opposite subtree.
- Terminate when the root is fully processed. Return $p^*$.

This algorithm is exact (always finds the true NN) with average complexity $O(\log n)$ and worst case $O(n)$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) K-D Tree Construction (3 marks)** — refer to Section 3.1 derivation above.

The final tree is:

```
                (5, 4)              [split: x = 5]
               /       \
           (2,3)        (7,2)         [split: y = 3, y = 2]
           /    \        /   \
         leaf  (4,7)  (8,1) (9,6)
```

**(ii) Nearest Neighbor Search for q = (6, 5) (4 marks)** — refer to Section 3.2 derivation above.

| Step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Descend: root $(5,4)$, $q_x=6 \geq 5$ → right | push $(5,4)$ |
| 2 | Descend: $(7,2)$, $q_y=5 \geq 2$ → right | push $(7,2)$ |
| 3 | Leaf $(9,6)$: $d^2 = 9+1 = 10$ | $d^* = 10$, $p^* = (9,6)$ |
| 4 | Backtrack to $(7,2)$: $d^2 = 1+9 = 10$ | tied, no update |
| 5 | Hyperplane distance: $(5-2)^2 = 9 < 10$ → search left | recurse to $(8,1)$ |
| 6 | At $(8,1)$: $d^2 = 4+16 = 20 > 10$ | no update |
| 7 | Backtrack to root $(5,4)$: $d^2 = 1+1 = 2$ | **update** $d^* = 2$, $p^* = (5,4)$ |
| 8 | Hyperplane: $(6-5)^2 = 1 < 2$ → search left | recurse to $(2,3)$ |
| 9 | At $(2,3)$: $d^2 = 16+4 = 20 > 2$ | no update |
| 10 | At $(4,7)$: $d^2 = 4+4 = 8 > 2$ | no update |

**Final answer**: Nearest neighbor is $\mathbf{p^* = (5, 4)}$ with squared distance $\mathbf{d^* = 2}$.

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Internal Choice Option 2]

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the limitations of K-D trees in high-dimensional spaces. Explain the "curse of dimensionality" with quantitative justification. What alternative structures are used for $k > 20$?

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Best-Bin-First (BBF)** K-D tree variant. How does it differ from the classical K-D tree? Why is it preferred for SIFT feature matching? Show the BBF search order using the same query as above.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Limitations of K-D Trees in High Dimensions (3 marks):**

1. **Curse of dimensionality**: The expected number of nodes visited grows as $n^{1-1/k}$. As $k$ increases, the exponent approaches 1, meaning the search visits nearly all nodes — equivalent to brute force.
2. **No meaningful "nearest"**: In high dimensions, the ratio $d_{\text{NN}} / d_{\text{avg}} \to 1/\sqrt{k}$ for uniform data. The nearest neighbor is not meaningfully closer than an average point, so exact NN becomes less informative.
3. **Splitting axis cycling becomes inefficient**: With $k = 128$ (SIFT), the cycle takes 128 levels before revisiting an axis. Many splits are along dimensions with low variance.
4. **Memory overhead**: Each node stores a $k$-dimensional point plus left/right pointers and axis index.

**Quantitative Justification (2 marks):**

For a balanced K-D tree on $n$ points in $k$ dimensions, the average search visits approximately $c \cdot n^{1-1/k}$ nodes. For $n = 10^6$:
- $k = 2$: $\sim 1000$ nodes
- $k = 10$: $\sim 10^5$ nodes
- $k = 128$: $\sim 10^5.99 \approx 977{,}000$ nodes (nearly all)

**Alternatives for $k > 20$ (2 marks):**

- **Randomized K-D Forest**: Build 4–8 trees with random split dimensions; query in parallel.
- **Hierarchical K-Means Tree / Vocabulary Tree**: Cluster points recursively; search is tree traversal.
- **Product Quantization (PQ)**: Compress descriptors into compact codes; search via asymmetric distance.
- **Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH)**: Hash similar points to the same bucket; sub-linear search.
- **HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World)**: Graph-based, scales to millions of high-dim points.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Best-Bin-First (BBF) K-D Tree (4 marks):**

The BBF variant, introduced by David Lowe (1999) for SIFT matching, modifies the **backtracking order** to achieve bounded-runtime approximate nearest neighbor search.

**Key Idea**: Instead of strict LIFO (stack) backtracking, BBF uses a **priority queue** ordered by the **distance from the query to the splitting hyperplane**. Subtrees whose hyperplane is closest to the query are explored first.

**Algorithm Modifications**:
1. During descent, push all visited nodes onto a priority queue keyed by hyperplane distance.
2. Pop the highest-priority (closest) node from the queue and search its unvisited subtrees.
3. **Cap** the search at a maximum number of leaves (e.g., 200 in Lowe's original SIFT paper) or a maximum priority threshold.
4. Return the best candidate found within the search budget.

**Why BBF is Preferred for SIFT (2 marks):**

- SIFT databases often have $n > 10^5$ keypoints with $k = 128$ dimensions.
- Classical K-D tree becomes slow in high $k$.
- BBF guarantees **bounded runtime** per query, enabling real-time matching.
- Approximate matches are sufficient for SIFT, provided Lowe's **ratio test** ($d_1/d_2 < 0.8$) filters ambiguous matches.
- The priority-queue ordering biases the search toward the "most promising" regions of the tree, which empirically gives high-quality matches with far less work.

**BBF Search Order for q = (6, 5) (1 mark):**

| Step | Node | Hyperplane Distance (priority) | Best $d^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Root $(5,4)$ | — | — |
| 2 | Right child $(7,2)$ | 9 | — |
| 3 | Left child $(2,3)$ | 1 (highest priority, queued) | — |
| 4 | Process left child first: leaf $(2,3)$, then $(4,7)$ | 1 | 2 (after checking $(5,4)$ at root) |
| 5 | Process right child $(7,2)$ subtree | 9 | 2 (final) |

Final answer remains $p^* = (5,4)$ with $d^* = 2$, but the *order* differs from classical LIFO backtracking.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing K-D tree with BST**: K-D trees partition *space*, not a sorted sequence. Don't write "in-order traversal yields sorted points" — it does not.
> 2. **Forgetting the axis cycle**: When constructing, you must specify which axis is being split at each level. Cycling $x \to y \to x \to y$ is mandatory.
> 3. **Using unsquared distance for pruning**: The hyperplane test is on **squared** distance to avoid square roots. If you use raw Euclidean distance, your answer is conceptually correct but notationally wrong for many KTU solutions.
> 4. **Skipping backtracking steps**: In exam answers, **always show every pop from the stack and every pruning decision**. Examiners award partial credit per step.
> 5. **Ignoring the "other subtree" check**: Many students only check the stored node at each ancestor, forgetting the **hyperplane pruning test** that decides whether to recurse into the opposite subtree.
> 6. **Confusing the K-D tree height**: $h = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ for balanced median-split, **not** $h = n$ (that's a degenerate tree).
> 7. **Ratio test confusion**: The Lowe ratio is $d_1 / d_2$ where $d_1$ is the best match and $d_2$ is the *second-best* match. Writing $d_1 / d_{\text{avg}}$ is wrong.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - [x] A **K-D tree** is a binary space-partitioning data structure; each node stores a k-D point and a split axis.
> - [x] **Construction**: cycle axes $(j = depth \bmod k + 1)$, split at the **median** of the chosen coordinate.
> - [x] Construction complexity: $O(n \log n)$ for balanced median-split.
> - [x] **Search** has two phases: **descent** to leaf, then **backtracking** with hyperplane pruning.
> - [x] **Hyperplane pruning condition**: prune opposite subtree iff $(q_j - s)^2 \geq d^*$.
> - [x] **Distance metrics**: L2² (default), L1 (Manhattan), L∞ (Chebyshev). Use L2² for speed.
> - [x] Average search time: $O(\log n)$ for low $k$. Worst case: $O(n)$.
> - [x] High-dim behavior: $O(k \cdot n^{1-1/k})$ — degrades sharply for $k > 20$.
> - [x] **BBF variant** uses a priority queue ordered by hyperplane distance; bounds the search.
> - [x] **Lowe's ratio test**: $d_1 / d_2 < 0.8$ to filter ambiguous SIFT matches.
> - [x] K-D trees are used in **feature matching, image stitching, CBIR, object recognition**.
> - [x] Alternatives for high-d: **Randomized K-D Forest, Vocabulary Tree, PQ, HNSW, LSH**.
> - [x] K-D tree is **NOT** a balanced BST — it is a *spatial* tree.
> - [x] At every backtrack, **check both** the ancestor's stored point AND the opposite subtree.
> - [x] **Standardize descriptors** before insertion if dimensions have different units/scales.
